Organic Chemistry Some Basic Principles Techniques and Hydrocarbons

    Variables used
  • \(R, R', Ar\): alkyl group, another alkyl group, aryl group
  • \(X\): halogen atom
  • \(\sigma\), \(\pi\): sigma bond, pi bond
  • \(sp^3, sp^2, sp\): hybridisation states of carbon
  • \(h\nu\): light / UV energy
  • \(\Delta\): heat
  • \([O]\): oxidation shorthand
  • \(\mathrm{DBE}\): degree of unsaturation
  • \(o\)-, \(m\)-, \(p\)-: ortho, meta, para positions in benzene

    Nomenclature and structure recognition
  • In IUPAC naming, choose the longest chain containing the principal multiple bond / functional group, then number to give that principal feature the lowest locant before optimising substituent positions.
  • For branched hydrocarbons, do not just chase the visually longest chain; check whether another chain of the same length gives more substituents or a lower-priority numbering pattern.
  • For unsaturated alcohol naming, the parent chain must include both the \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group and the multiple bond whenever possible, and numbering must favour the alcohol first in standard IUPAC priority.
  • Hybridisation questions are usually direct if you count from the final structure: benzene ring carbon is \(sp^2\), carbonyl carbon is \(sp^2\), saturated alkyl carbon is \(sp^3\), and a carbon in \(\mathrm{C \equiv C}\) is \(sp\).

    Resonance and electronic effects
  • Hyperconjugation means delocalisation of \(\sigma(\mathrm{C-H})\) electrons into an adjacent \(\pi\)-system or vacant p-orbital. If a C–H bond sits next to \(\mathrm{C=C}\), a benzylic system, or a carbocation, hyperconjugation is a strong clue.
  • \(-R\) effect means electron withdrawal by resonance. Carbonyl-containing groups such as \(\mathrm{-CHO}\), \(\mathrm{-COR}\), \(\mathrm{-COOR}\) are classic \(-R\) groups because they pull electron density from the conjugated system.
  • For resonance-contributor stability: uncharged form > charge-separated form, and among charged forms, the one placing negative charge on the more electronegative atom is more stable.
  • Do not confuse \(-R\) with \(-I\), and do not call every electron-shift picture hyperconjugation. If the delocalisation is through conjugation, think resonance; if it specifically uses adjacent \(\sigma(\mathrm{C-H})\) overlap, think hyperconjugation.
  • Copy this image into the images folder.

    Nitrogen estimation
  • Kjeldahl’s method is worth remembering mainly through its limitations: it works for ordinary amino-type nitrogen, but not for nitro compounds, azo compounds, diazonium salts, or many aromatic ring-nitrogen systems such as pyridine.
  • If the options are amine vs nitro vs diazonium vs ring nitrogen, amino-type nitrogen is usually the safe inclusion and the others are the standard exclusions.

    Alkane preparation and conversion
  • Wurtz reaction: \(\mathrm{2R-X + 2Na \xrightarrow[dry\ ether]{} R-R + 2NaX}\). It couples two identical alkyl halides to give a higher alkane, so count carbons carefully before moving to the next step.
  • Kolbe electrolysis: \(\mathrm{2RCOO^- \xrightarrow[electrolysis]{} R-R + 2CO_2 + 2e^-}\). It usually gives a symmetrical higher alkane; sodium acetate gives ethane and carbon dioxide at the anode.
  • Soda-lime decarboxylation: \(\mathrm{RCOONa + NaOH \xrightarrow[CaO]{\Delta} RH + Na_2CO_3}\). The alkane formed has one carbon less than the parent carboxylate.
  • In multistep hydrocarbon conversions, a very common pattern is: Wurtz coupling \(\rightarrow\) higher alkane \(\rightarrow\) catalytic aromatization \(\rightarrow\) benzene derivative chemistry.

    Alkanes and aromatic conversion logic
  • Higher straight-chain alkanes under catalytic dehydrogenation / aromatization conditions can give aromatic compounds such as benzene. This is a repeated conversion pattern and is worth treating as must-know.
  • If benzene formed in one step is then treated with \(\mathrm{RCOCl/AlCl_3}\), the next step is Friedel–Crafts acylation, not ordinary addition.
  • Once the final product is known, count \(sp^2\) and \(sp^3\) carbons directly from the final structure instead of tracing hybridisation through every intermediate.

    Alkenes: cleavage and product prediction
  • Hot acidic \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) and ozonolysis-type logic both reward the same skill: read the double bond and split it correctly. The product depends on how substituted each alkene carbon is.
  • A terminal \(\mathrm{=CH_2}\) carbon under strong oxidative cleavage can end up as \(\mathrm{CO_2}\), while the more substituted partner can give a ketone or carboxylic acid depending on substitution.
  • Among \(\mathrm{C_4H_8}\) alkenes, the one showing geometrical isomerism is but-2-ene. This is a very standard identification point.
  • If a symmetrical alkene such as but-2-ene undergoes strong oxidative cleavage, both sides give the same product; this makes downstream conversion chains much easier to track.
  • Copy this image into the images folder.

    Markovnikov, peroxide effect and alkene reaction shortcuts
  • Markovnikov rule: in normal ionic addition of HX to an unsymmetrical alkene, H goes to the carbon already carrying more hydrogens.
  • Peroxide effect is the anti-Markovnikov addition of HBr only. Do not generalise it to HCl or HI.
  • If hydration of an alkyne / special alkene sequence is involved, remember that the initially formed enol usually rearranges by keto-enol tautomerism to the more stable carbonyl compound.

    Alkynes: acidic hydrogen and counting
  • Only terminal alkynes have acidic hydrogen: \(\mathrm{R-C \equiv C-H}\). Internal alkynes do not.
  • The acidity comes from the high s-character of the \(sp\)-hybridised carbon, which stabilises the conjugate base better than in alkenes or alkanes.
  • For isomer-counting questions, do not start with acidity. First list all open-chain alkyne isomers, then split them into terminal and internal. That is the cleanest route.
  • Useful quick checks: for one open-chain alkyne, the general formula is \(\mathrm{C_{n}H_{2n-2}}\), and \(\mathrm{DBE = C + 1 - \frac{H}{2}}\) helps confirm whether the formula fits one triple bond.
  • For \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\), total open-chain alkyne isomers \(= 7\), of which terminal \(= 4\) and internal \(= 3\). This exact count is worth remembering because it has already been asked.

    \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) and carbocation stability
  • \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) reactivity follows carbocation stability. The leaving group leaves first, so the more stable the carbocation, the faster the reaction.
  • Resonance stabilisation is especially powerful in benzylic and allylic systems; alkyl substitution adds extra help through \(+I\) effect and hyperconjugation.
  • A tertiary benzylic carbocation can be far more stable than a primary benzylic carbocation because it combines resonance with extra alkyl stabilisation.
  • Watch for the dangerous opposite case too: a vinylic carbocation is very unstable, so options placing positive charge directly on a double-bond carbon are usually poor choices.

    Friedel–Crafts and benzene substitution
  • Benzene prefers electrophilic substitution, not addition, because aromaticity must be preserved.
  • Friedel–Crafts alkylation: benzene + alkyl halide / \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) gives alkylbenzene. Friedel–Crafts acylation: benzene + acyl chloride / \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) gives aryl ketone.
  • For acylation, the real electrophile is the acylium ion \(\mathrm{RCO^+}\). So benzene with \(\mathrm{CH_3COCl/AlCl_3}\) gives acetophenone directly.
  • Groups donating electron density by resonance are usually activating and often ortho/para-directing; carbonyl-based withdrawing groups are usually deactivating because of \(-R\) effect.

    Must-remember equations
  • \(\mathrm{2R-X + 2Na \xrightarrow[dry\ ether]{} R-R + 2NaX}\) — Wurtz reaction
  • \(\mathrm{2RCOO^- \xrightarrow[electrolysis]{} R-R + 2CO_2 + 2e^-}\) — Kolbe electrolysis
  • \(\mathrm{RCOONa + NaOH \xrightarrow[CaO]{\Delta} RH + Na_2CO_3}\) — soda-lime decarboxylation
  • \(\mathrm{Ar-H + RCOCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} Ar-COR}\) — Friedel–Crafts acylation
  • \(\mathrm{R-C \equiv C-H}\) — terminal alkyne pattern
  • \(\mathrm{C_{n}H_{2n-2}}\) — open-chain alkyne formula
  • \(\mathrm{DBE = C + 1 - \frac{H}{2}}\) — quick unsaturation check

    Pitfalls and final triggers
  • Do not confuse resonance with hyperconjugation, and do not confuse \(-R\) with \(-I\). The direction and type of electron movement matter.
  • Do not generalise peroxide effect to all hydrogen halides; in this syllabus block, anti-Markovnikov addition belongs to HBr only.
  • Do not forget that only terminal alkynes have acidic hydrogen, and do not forget the standard exclusions of Kjeldahl’s method.
  • In hydrocarbon conversion chains, the safest workflow is: identify reagent pattern \(\rightarrow\) write the named transformation \(\rightarrow\) count carbons / hybridisation only at the end.

    Variables used
  • \(R, R', Ar\): alkyl group, another alkyl group, aryl group
  • \(X\): halogen atom
  • \(\mathrm{Nu^-}\): nucleophile, an electron-rich attacking species
  • \(E^+\): electrophile, an electron-deficient attacking species
  • \(\sigma\), \(\pi\): sigma bond and pi bond
  • \(sp^3, sp^2, sp\): hybridisation states of carbon
  • \(h\nu\): light / UV energy
  • \(\Delta\): heat
  • \([O]\), \([H]\): oxidation and reduction shorthand
  • \(\delta^+\), \(\delta^-\): partial positive and partial negative charge
  • \(o\)-, \(m\)-, \(p\)-: ortho, meta, para positions in benzene

    Organic framework and classification
  • Organic chemistry mainly deals with compounds of carbon. The huge number of organic compounds comes from catenation and the tetravalency of carbon, which let carbon form long chains, branched chains and rings.
  • The first broad split is open-chain (aliphatic) versus closed-chain (cyclic). Open-chain compounds may be saturated or unsaturated, while cyclic compounds may be alicyclic, aromatic or heterocyclic.
  • For quick exam sorting: only single bonds \(\rightarrow\) alkane; one or more \(\mathrm{C=C}\) bonds \(\rightarrow\) alkene; one or more \(\mathrm{C \equiv C}\) bonds \(\rightarrow\) alkyne; benzene-type delocalised ring \(\rightarrow\) aromatic.

    Tetravalency, structural representation and homologous series
  • Carbon is tetravalent, so it forms four covalent bonds and builds stable frameworks. In saturated compounds carbon is usually \(sp^3\)-hybridised; in alkenes the double-bond carbons are \(sp^2\)-hybridised; in alkynes the triple-bond carbons are \(sp\)-hybridised.
  • A homologous series is a family of compounds in which successive members differ by a \(\mathrm{CH_2}\) unit. For this chapter, the core general formulae are: alkanes \(\mathrm{C_{n}H_{2n+2}}\), alkenes \(\mathrm{C_{n}H_{2n}}\), alkynes \(\mathrm{C_{n}H_{2n-2}}\).
  • Use condensed formulae, expanded structures and skeletal structures comfortably. Many questions here are really structure-recognition questions disguised as theory.
  • Please copy this page-based image into this topic’s images folder.

    IUPAC nomenclature: the exam-safe rules
  • Use this exact naming method every time:
    1. Find the principal functional group.
    2. Choose the main chain containing that group and the maximum number of multiple bonds.
    3. Number by: \(\mathrm{Principal\ FG > C=C > C\equiv C > substituents}\).
    4. Write substituents in alphabetical order.
    5. Final order: \(\mathrm{prefixes + parent + en/yn + suffix}\).

  • Functional-group priority ladder: \(\mathrm{COOH > SO_3H > Anhydride > Ester > COX > CONH_2 > CN > CHO > CO > OH > NH_2 > C=C > C\equiv C > substituents}\)

  • Numbering rule: Principal functional group gets first priority. If both double and triple bonds are present, \(\mathrm{ene}\) gets lower locant than \(\mathrm{yne}\) in a tie.

  • Useful instant prefixes: \(\mathrm{-OH \rightarrow hydroxy,\ -NH_2 \rightarrow amino,\ -CHO \rightarrow formyl,\ >C=O \rightarrow oxo,\ -CN \rightarrow cyano,\ -COOH \rightarrow carboxy,\ -OR \rightarrow alkoxy,\ -NO_2 \rightarrow nitro,\ -F/-Cl/-Br/-I \rightarrow fluoro/chloro/bromo/iodo}\)

  • High-yield traps: Do not choose the longest chain blindly; \(\mathrm{-OH}\) beats \(\mathrm{C=C}\) and \(\mathrm{C\equiv C}\); substituents are written in alphabetical order; in esters write \(\mathrm{alkyl\ first,\ alkanoate\ second}\); in \(\mathrm{-COOH}\), \(\mathrm{-CN}\), and \(\mathrm{-CHO}\), the functional carbon is usually carbon \(1\).
  • In branched compounds, choose the longest chain containing the multiple bond or principal functional group. Number it so that the principal feature gets the lowest locant first; only after that should you optimise substituent positions.
  • In branched chain hydrocarbons, one or more alkyl groups are present as side chain attached to the main straight chain of carbon atoms. The carbon atoms of the side chain constitute alkyl groups. Some examples with their common names are given below:
  • If two chains have equal length, prefer the one with more substituents. When different substituents are present, write them in alphabetical order; prefixes such as di-, tri-, tetra- are ignored for alphabetising.
  • For cyclic compounds, use the prefix cyclo-. For benzene derivatives, lowest locants still apply, and disubstituted benzene patterns can also be recognised as \(o\)-, \(m\)- and \(p\)- forms.

    Functional groups and priority
  • A functional group is the atom or group of atoms responsible for the characteristic reactions of an organic compound. In naming, the principal functional group decides the suffix and usually gets the lowest locant.
  • For carbon-containing groups such as \(\mathrm{-CHO}\) and \(\mathrm{-COOH}\), the main chain must include the carbon atom of the functional group itself. This is a very common source of mistakes in nomenclature problems.
  • The priority order in this text is: \(\mathrm{-COOH > -COOR > -SO_3H > -COX > -CONH_2 > -CHO > >CO > -CN > -OH > -SH > -O- > -NH_2 > -X > -NO_2 > C=C > C \equiv C}\).
  • Serial No Group Formula Group FG Suffix Substituent Prefix TFG when attached directly to C-ring
    1 -COOH Oic acid Carboxy Carboxylic Acid
    2 -SO3H Sulphonic acid Sulpho X
    3 (RCO)2O Oic anhydride X X
    4 -COOR Alkyl Alkanoate Alkoxy Carbonyl Alkyl Carboxylate
    5 -COX Oyl halide Haloformyl Carbonyl halide
    6 -CONH2 Amide Carbamoyl Carboxamide
    7 -C≡N Nitrile Cyano Carbonitrile
    8 -N≡C Iso-nitrile Iso-cyano X
    9 -CHO Al Formyl/Oxo Carbaldehyde
    10 >C=O One Oxo/keto X
    11 -OH Ol Hydroxy X
    12 -SH Thiol Mercapto X
    13 -NH2 Amine Amino X
    14 epoxy ring Epoxy X X
  • Please copy the page-based image into this topic’s images folder, and fetch the functional-group-priority-order image using the exact alt text if you want that visual included.

    Isomerism
  • Isomerism means same molecular formula but different arrangement of atoms. For quick revision here, divide it into structural isomerism, geometrical isomerism, and the required conformational idea of ethane.
  • Structural isomerism means same molecular formula but different connectivity. The most useful types here are chain isomerism, position isomerism and functional-group isomerism.
  • In chain isomerism, the carbon skeleton changes. A standard memory example is \(\mathrm{C_5H_{12}}\): n-pentane, isopentane and neopentane all have the same molecular formula but different carbon branching.
  • In position isomerism, the carbon skeleton remains the same but the position of a double bond, triple bond, substituent or functional group changes. For example, but-1-ene and but-2-ene are position isomers; but-1-yne and but-2-yne are another standard pair.
  • In functional-group isomerism, the same molecular formula corresponds to different functional groups. This becomes more important in functional-group chapters, but the idea should already be clear here because it explains why identical formulae may behave very differently.
  • Geometrical isomerism appears in alkenes because rotation about the \(\mathrm{C=C}\) bond is restricted. It is possible only when each carbon of the double bond is attached to two different groups.
  • That is why but-2-ene shows cis/trans isomerism but but-1-ene does not. In but-1-ene, one double-bond carbon carries two identical H atoms, so no geometrical pair is possible.
  • Cis and trans isomers may differ in physical properties such as melting point and boiling point because their shapes and dipole effects differ, even though their molecular formula is the same.
  • The only required conformational part in this block is ethane: the staggered conformation is more stable than the eclipsed conformation because torsional strain is lower in the staggered form.
  • In exam language: if two forms differ only by rotation about a single bond, think conformation; if they differ because rotation is restricted across a double bond, think geometrical isomerism.
  • For hydrocarbon PYQs, a useful fast workflow is: first ask whether the skeleton changed, then whether only the position changed, then whether the double bond can give cis/trans forms.


    Bond fission, nucleophiles and electrophiles
  • Homolytic fission breaks a covalent bond equally and gives free radicals. This is the bond-breaking pattern used in free-radical halogenation of alkanes.
  • Heterolytic fission breaks a covalent bond unequally and gives ions such as carbocations and carbanions.
  • Electrophiles are electron-deficient species such as \(\mathrm{H^+}\), \(\mathrm{NO_2^+}\), \(\mathrm{Br^+}\), \(\mathrm{CH_3CO^+}\). They attack regions of high electron density.
  • Nucleophiles are negatively charged or electron-rich neutral species such as \(\mathrm{OH^-}\), \(\mathrm{CN^-}\), \(\mathrm{H_2O}\), \(\mathrm{:NH_3}\). They attack regions of low electron density.
  • Curved full-headed arrows show movement of an electron pair; fish-hook arrows show single-electron movement in radical mechanisms.

    Electronic effects in covalent bonds
  • Inductive effect is permanent polarisation through \(\sigma\)-bonds caused by electronegativity difference. It decreases rapidly with distance, and it explains many acidity and reactivity trends.
  • Electromeric effect is a temporary complete transfer of a \(\pi\)-electron pair in the presence of an attacking reagent.
  • Resonance means delocalisation of electrons through conjugation. The real molecule is the resonance hybrid, not any one single canonical structure.
  • Hyperconjugation is delocalisation of \(\sigma(\mathrm{C-H})\) electrons into an adjacent \(\pi\)-system or vacant p-orbital; it is often called no-bond resonance.
  • Steric hindrance means crowding near a reactive centre; it can slow or prevent a reaction.
  • The text gives the following decreasing order for \(-I\) effect: \((\mathrm{CH_3})_3\mathrm{N^+} > -\mathrm{NO_2} > -\mathrm{CN} > -\mathrm{F} > -\mathrm{Cl} > -\mathrm{Br} > -\mathrm{I} > -\mathrm{OH} > -\mathrm{OCH_3} > -\mathrm{C_6H_5} > -\mathrm{H}\).
  • The text gives the following decreasing order for \(+I\) effect: \((\mathrm{CH_3})_3\mathrm{C^-} > (\mathrm{CH_3})_2\mathrm{CH^-} > \mathrm{CH_3CH_2^-} > \mathrm{CH_3^-} > \mathrm{H^-}\). For practical memory, treat larger alkyl groups as stronger \(+I\) donors than smaller ones.
  • \(-I\) effect order

  • \(+I\) effect order

  • Acidic order


    Purification and elemental analysis Use purification methods in easy memory groups:
    1. Crystallisation / fractional crystallisation / sublimation for solids
    2. Simple distillation, fractional distillation, distillation under reduced pressure and steam distillation for liquids
    3. hromatography for separation based on adsorption or partition differences
  • Use the method by matching it to the physical property being exploited: difference in solubility for crystallisation, volatility / boiling point for distillation, and selective movement between two phases for chromatography.
  • In qualitative elemental analysis, carbon and hydrogen are commonly detected by oxidation of the compound to \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) and \(\mathrm{H_2O}\). The key named method for extra elements such as nitrogen, sulphur and halogens is Lassaigne’s test, where sodium fusion converts covalently bonded elements into ionic forms that can be tested more easily.
  • For nitrogen in Lassaigne’s test, sodium fusion forms \(\mathrm{NaCN}\); after treatment with ferrous / ferric ions and acidification, formation of Prussian blue confirms nitrogen. The memory equation worth keeping is: \(\mathrm{Na + C + N \rightarrow NaCN}\)
  • For sulphur, sodium fusion forms \(\mathrm{Na_2S}\), and for halogens it forms \(\mathrm{NaX}\). These conversions are the reason sodium fusion is such a central practical trick in organic analysis.
  • For quantitative nitrogen analysis, Kjeldahl’s method is high-value mainly through both its core idea and its limitations. On digestion with concentrated sulphuric acid, nitrogen in suitable organic compounds is converted into ammonium form, then liberated as ammonia and estimated volumetrically.
  • The exam-safe summary of Kjeldahl is: it works for ordinary amino-type nitrogen, but does not work for nitro compounds, azo compounds, diazonium salts, or many aromatic ring-nitrogen systems such as pyridine. In MCQs, this limitation is often more important than the full procedural detail.
  • Also remember the other named methods at least by purpose: Dumas method is another nitrogen estimation method, while Carius method is used for halogens.

    Types of organic reactions
  • Substitution: one atom or group is replaced by another. Benzene usually undergoes electrophilic substitution rather than addition.
  • Addition: reagent adds across a multiple bond. This is the core chemistry of alkenes and alkynes.
  • Elimination: removal of a small molecule such as \(\mathrm{H_2O}\) or HX to create a multiple bond.
  • Molecular rearrangement: internal reorganisation of the skeleton or electron arrangement.

    Alkanes: structure, preparation and physical properties
  • Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons with general formula \(\mathrm{C_{n}H_{2n+2}}\). They are comparatively less reactive and are also called paraffins.
  • Important preparation methods are: reduction of haloalkanes, Grignard reagent with active hydrogen compounds, hydrogenation of alkenes / alkynes, reduction of alcohols / aldehydes / ketones with HI / red P, soda-lime decarboxylation, and Kolbe electrolysis.
  • Wurtz reaction: \(\mathrm{2R-X + 2Na \xrightarrow[dry\ ether]{} R-R + 2NaX}\)
  • Soda-lime decarboxylation: \(\mathrm{RCOONa + NaOH \xrightarrow[CaO]{\Delta} RH + Na_2CO_3}\)
  • Kolbe electrolysis: \(\mathrm{2RCOO^- \xrightarrow[electrolysis]{} R-R + 2CO_2 + 2e^-}\)
  • Physical trends matter: lower alkanes are gases, middle members are liquids, higher ones are solids. More branching lowers surface area, so it usually lowers boiling point. Even-carbon alkanes often pack better than neighbouring odd-carbon members and may show higher melting points.

    Reactions of alkanes
  • Halogenation is the signature reaction. Chlorination of methane proceeds by a free-radical chain mechanism with three standard steps: initiation, propagation and termination.
  • Combustion: \(\mathrm{C_{x}H_{y} + O_{2} \rightarrow CO_{2} + H_{2}O}\). Limited oxygen can give incomplete combustion and carbon monoxide.
  • Pyrolysis / cracking breaks larger alkanes into smaller alkanes and alkenes at high temperature.
  • Isomerisation converts straight-chain alkanes into branched isomers. Aromatization of higher straight-chain alkanes under catalytic dehydrogenation conditions can give aromatic compounds such as benzene.

    Alkenes: preparation, properties and reactions
  • Alkenes are unsaturated hydrocarbons containing at least one \(\mathrm{C=C}\) bond. The double-bond carbons are \(sp^2\)-hybridised and trigonal planar, and rotation about the double bond is restricted.
  • Important laboratory preparations are dehydrohalogenation of haloalkanes and dehydration of alcohols. In such elimination reactions, the major product usually follows Saytzeff’s rule: the more substituted alkene is favoured.
  • Alkenes undergo electrophilic addition: hydrogenation, halogenation, addition of HX, hydration, addition of sulphuric acid and polymerisation.
  • Markovnikov’s rule: in addition of HX to an unsymmetrical alkene, hydrogen goes to the carbon already bearing more hydrogens and the halogen goes to the more substituted carbon.
  • Peroxide effect / anti-Markovnikov addition is the special case of HBr addition in the presence of peroxide.
  • Oxidation with cold dilute alkaline \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) gives vicinal diols and decolourises the purple reagent. Stronger oxidation can cleave the double bond.
  • Ozonolysis: ozone adds to the alkene to form ozonide, and on hydrolysis with Zn / water gives aldehydes and / or ketones. It is one of the cleanest ways to locate the original double bond.

    Acidity logic and acidic order
  • Acidity in this unit is often explained through the stability of the conjugate base. More \(-I\) effect nearby usually increases acidity, while \(+I\) effect usually decreases it.
  • Among hydrocarbons, terminal alkynes are noticeably more acidic than alkenes and alkanes because the negative charge in the conjugate base lies on an \(sp\)-hybridised carbon with high s-character.
  • For substituted carboxylic acids, stronger electron-withdrawing groups and their closer position to \(\mathrm{-COOH}\) generally increase acidity.

    Alkynes: structure, preparation and reactions
  • Alkynes contain at least one \(\mathrm{C \equiv C}\) bond. The two carbons of the triple bond are \(sp\)-hybridised and linear.
  • The model alkyne is ethyne (acetylene). A must-remember preparation is: \(\mathrm{CaC_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow HC \equiv CH + Ca(OH)_2}\)
  • Alkynes also form by strong dehydrohalogenation of vicinal or geminal dihalides.
  • Terminal alkynes show acidic character and react with strong bases, ammoniacal silver nitrate and ammoniacal cuprous chloride. Internal alkynes do not show this acidic-hydrogen behaviour.
  • Like alkenes, alkynes undergo addition of hydrogen, halogen, HX and water. Hydration of ethyne finally gives acetaldehyde after enol-keto tautomerism.
  • Polymerisation / trimerisation of ethyne can give benzene, linking alkyne chemistry back to aromatic hydrocarbons.

    Aromatic hydrocarbons: benzene, resonance and substitution
  • Benzene is the central aromatic hydrocarbon. It is not best represented as a simple alternating single-double bond structure; its real stability comes from resonance and delocalisation.
  • This aromatic stability is why benzene prefers electrophilic substitution over addition.
  • Important benzene reactions are nitration, sulphonation, halogenation, Friedel–Crafts alkylation and Friedel–Crafts acylation.
  • Nitration: \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + HNO_3 \xrightarrow[conc.]{conc.\ H_2SO_4} C_6H_5NO_2 + H_2O}\)
  • Sulphonation: \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + H_2SO_4(fuming) \rightarrow C_6H_5SO_3H + H_2O}\)
  • Halogenation: \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + Cl_2 \xrightarrow{FeCl_3/AlCl_3} C_6H_5Cl + HCl}\)
  • Friedel–Crafts alkylation: \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + RCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5R + HCl}\)
  • Friedel–Crafts acylation: \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + RCOCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5COR + HCl}\)
  • In monosubstituted benzene, groups already present control both the rate and orientation of further substitution. Electron-donating groups usually activate and direct ortho / para; strong electron-withdrawing groups usually deactivate and often favour meta attack.
  • Benzene is also important to remember as a toxic and carcinogenic aromatic solvent.

    Must-know equations
  • \(\mathrm{2R-X + 2Na \xrightarrow[dry\ ether]{} R-R + 2NaX}\) — Wurtz reaction
  • \(\mathrm{RCOONa + NaOH \xrightarrow[CaO]{\Delta} RH + Na_2CO_3}\) — soda-lime decarboxylation
  • \(\mathrm{2RCOO^- \xrightarrow[electrolysis]{} R-R + 2CO_2 + 2e^-}\) — Kolbe electrolysis
  • \(\mathrm{CH_4 + Cl_2 \xrightarrow{h\nu} CH_3Cl + HCl}\) — prototype free-radical halogenation
  • \(\mathrm{C_{n}H_{2n} + H_{2} \xrightarrow{Ni/Pt/Pd} C_{n}H_{2n+2}}\) — alkene hydrogenation
  • \(\mathrm{C=C \xrightarrow{O_3} ozonide \xrightarrow{Zn/H_2O} carbonyl\ compounds}\) — ozonolysis
  • \(\mathrm{CaC_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow HC \equiv CH + Ca(OH)_2}\) — preparation of ethyne
  • \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + HNO_3 \xrightarrow[conc.]{conc.\ H_2SO_4} C_6H_5NO_2 + H_2O}\) — nitration of benzene
  • \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + RCOCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5COR + HCl}\) — Friedel–Crafts acylation

    Final exam reminders
  • In nomenclature, first identify the principal functional group; then pick the correct parent chain; then number from the end that gives the principal feature the lowest locant; only after that should you worry about substituents.
  • Do not confuse \(-R\) effect with \(-I\) effect, and do not confuse resonance with hyperconjugation. The picture usually tells you which one is operating if you watch how electrons are moving.
  • Do not generalise the peroxide effect to all HX; in this syllabus block, anti-Markovnikov addition belongs specifically to HBr.
  • Remember the three especially exam-friendly contrast points: Wurtz vs Kolbe vs soda-lime; Markovnikov vs peroxide effect; terminal alkyne vs internal alkyne.
  • If you include the image placeholders above, please copy the page-based images into this topic’s images folder and fetch the Google-image placeholders using the exact alt text given.

    2025

    Answer: \((a)\ 8\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    1. Identify the first reaction

      The starting halide is \(1\)-bromopropane, \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br}\). With \(\mathrm{Na/dry\ ether}\), it undergoes Wurtz reaction.

      In Wurtz reaction:

      \(2\mathrm{RBr} + 2\mathrm{Na} \rightarrow \mathrm{R{-}R} + 2\mathrm{NaBr}\)

      So:

      \(2\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br} \xrightarrow[\text{dry ether}]{\mathrm{Na}} \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2CH_2CH_3}\)

      Therefore, \(X = n\)-hexane.

    2. Identify the second reaction

      \(X\) is treated with \(\mathrm{Cr_2O_3}\) at \(773\ \mathrm{K}\) and \(10{-}20\ \mathrm{atm}\). These are catalytic dehydrogenation conditions.

      Under such conditions, \(n\)-hexane gives benzene by aromatization:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_6H_6} + 4\mathrm{H_2}\)

      Therefore, \(Y =\) benzene.

    3. Identify the third reaction

      Benzene reacts with \(\mathrm{CH_3COCl/anhy.\ AlCl_3}\). This is Friedel–Crafts acylation.

      So benzene forms acetophenone:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_6} \xrightarrow[\text{anhy.\ AlCl}_3]{\mathrm{CH_3COCl}} \mathrm{C_6H_5COCH_3}\)

      Therefore, \(Z =\) acetophenone.

    4. Count \(sp^2\) and \(sp^3\) carbons in \(Z\)

      Structure of acetophenone:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5{-}CO{-}CH_3}\)

      • \(sp^2\) carbons:

        All \(6\) carbons of the benzene ring are \(sp^2\)-hybridised.

        The carbonyl carbon \(\mathrm{(C{=}O)}\) is also \(sp^2\)-hybridised.

        So, \(b = 6 + 1 = 7\).

      • \(sp^3\) carbons:

        The methyl carbon \(\mathrm{(-CH_3)}\) is \(sp^3\)-hybridised.

        So, \(a = 1\).

      Hence:

      \(a + b = 1 + 7 = 8\)

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(a)\ 8}\)

    Reaction flow:

    Why this method works:

    • Wurtz reaction couples two alkyl halide molecules to give a higher alkane.
    • Catalytic dehydrogenation/aromatization of straight-chain alkanes can convert them into aromatic compounds.
    • Friedel–Crafts acylation introduces the acyl group \(\mathrm{(-COCH_3)}\) onto benzene.
    • Carbon hybridisation is then counted directly from the final structure.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Wurtz reaction:

      \(2\mathrm{R{-}X} + 2\mathrm{Na} \xrightarrow[\text{dry ether}]{} \mathrm{R{-}R} + 2\mathrm{NaX}\)

    • Aromatization:

      Higher alkanes can form aromatic compounds on passing over suitable catalysts at high temperature.

    • Friedel–Crafts acylation:

      \(\mathrm{Ar{-}H + RCOCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} Ar{-}COR}\)

    • Hybridisation rules:
      • Carbon in benzene ring is \(sp^2\).
      • Carbonyl carbon is \(sp^2\).
      • Saturated alkyl carbon is \(sp^3\).

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Do not try to expand every mechanism fully.
    • Memorise these standard patterns:
      • \(\mathrm{Na/dry\ ether}\) \(\rightarrow\) Wurtz coupling
      • \(\mathrm{Cr_2O_3,\ 773\ K}\) on straight-chain alkane \(\rightarrow\) aromatization
      • \(\mathrm{RCOCl/AlCl_3}\) on benzene \(\rightarrow\) Friedel–Crafts acylation
    • Once you see benzene \(+\ \mathrm{CH_3COCl/AlCl_3}\), jump directly to acetophenone.
    • Then count:

      benzene ring \(= 6\ sp^2\), carbonyl carbon \(= 1\ sp^2\), methyl carbon \(= 1\ sp^3\)

      So total \(= 7 + 1 = 8\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Mistaking the Wurtz product as butane instead of the coupled product from propyl halide.
    2. Forgetting that the carbonyl carbon is also \(sp^2\)-hybridised.
    3. Counting the methyl carbon of \(\mathrm{-COCH_3}\) as \(sp^2\); it is actually \(sp^3\).
    4. Missing the aromatization step and stopping at an alkene or diene.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
    • Topic: Aromatic hydrocarbons and named reactions
    • Subtopic: Wurtz reaction, aromatization, Friedel–Crafts acylation, and hybridisation counting

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br}\) with \(\mathrm{Na/dry\ ether}\) gives \(n\)-hexane by Wurtz reaction.

    \(n\)-Hexane on catalytic dehydrogenation with \(\mathrm{Cr_2O_3}\) at \(773\ \mathrm{K}\) gives benzene.

    Benzene with \(\mathrm{CH_3COCl/anhy.\ AlCl_3}\) gives acetophenone, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5COCH_3}\).

    In acetophenone:

    • \(sp^2\) carbons \(= 6\) (benzene ring) \(+ 1\) (carbonyl carbon) \(= 7\)
    • \(sp^3\) carbons \(= 1\) (methyl carbon)

    Therefore:

    \(a + b = 1 + 7 = 8\)

    \(\boxed{(a)\ 8}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Wurtz reaction:

      \(2\mathrm{R{-}X} + 2\mathrm{Na} \rightarrow \mathrm{R{-}R}\)

    • Friedel–Crafts acylation:

      \(\mathrm{Ar{-}H + RCOCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} Ar{-}COR}\)

    • Benzene ring carbon: \(sp^2\)
    • Carbonyl carbon: \(sp^2\)
    • Alkyl carbon with only single bonds: \(sp^3\)
    • Fast counting in acetophenone:

      \(6\ sp^2 + 1\ sp^2 + 1\ sp^3 = 8\)

    Answer: \((a)\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    Hyperconjugation is the delocalisation of electrons of a \(\sigma\)-bond, usually a \(\mathrm{C-H}\) bond, into an adjacent \(\pi\)-system or vacant \(p\)-orbital.

    1. What hyperconjugation means

      In molecules like alkenes, if a \(\mathrm{C-H}\) bond is attached to the carbon next to a double bond, the electrons of that \(\sigma\) \(\mathrm{C-H}\) bond can overlap with the adjacent \(\pi\)-system.

      This is called hyperconjugation or no-bond resonance.

    2. Check option \((a)\)

      Option \((a)\) shows propene, \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH_2}\).

      Here, the \(\mathrm{C-H}\) bonds of the methyl group are adjacent to the \(\pi\)-bond of the alkene, so electron delocalisation from \(\sigma(\mathrm{C-H})\) to the \(\pi\)-system is possible.

      Therefore, this represents hyperconjugation.

      • A simplified no-bond form is represented as:

        \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH_2 \rightleftharpoons CH_2=CH-CH_2}\)

    3. Why the other options are not hyperconjugation
      • Option \((b)\): This shows resonance involving lone-pair donation and charge separation, not \(\sigma(\mathrm{C-H})\)-electron delocalisation.
      • Option \((c)\): This represents inductive effect, where electron density is shifted through \(\sigma\)-bonds because of the electron-withdrawing \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) group.
      • Option \((d)\): This represents electrophilic addition of \(\mathrm{H^+}\) to an alkene, not hyperconjugation.
    4. Hence

      The correct option is \((a)\).

    Why this method works:

    • Hyperconjugation always involves a \(\mathrm{C-H}\) \(\sigma\)-bond next to a multiple bond or electron-deficient centre.
    • So the fastest test is:

      Is there an alkyl \(\mathrm{C-H}\) bond adjacent to a \(\pi\)-bond or carbocation?

    • If yes, hyperconjugation is possible.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Definition:

      Hyperconjugation is the delocalisation of electrons of a \(\sigma\)-bond, generally \(\sigma(\mathrm{C-H})\), with an adjacent \(\pi\)-orbital or vacant \(p\)-orbital.

    • It is also called no-bond resonance.
    • It explains:
      • stability of alkenes
      • stability of carbocations
      • ortho/para directing nature of alkyl groups in benzene
    • More \(\alpha\)-hydrogens generally means more hyperconjugative structures and greater stabilisation.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Look for a structure where a \(\mathrm{C-H}\) bond is directly next to \(\mathrm{C=C}\) or \(\mathrm{C^+}\).
    • Ignore options showing only:
      • charge shifting from lone pairs \(\rightarrow\) resonance
      • bond polarization \(\rightarrow\) inductive effect
      • attack by reagent \(\rightarrow\) reaction mechanism
    • Here only \((a)\) matches the standard hyperconjugation pattern.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing resonance with hyperconjugation.
    2. Thinking every electron-shift arrow means hyperconjugation.
    3. Forgetting that hyperconjugation specifically involves a \(\sigma(\mathrm{C-H})\) bond.
    4. Confusing inductive effect of \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\) with hyperconjugation.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – General Organic Chemistry
    • Topic: Electronic effects in organic molecules
    • Subtopic: Hyperconjugation

    Final exam-style answer:

    Hyperconjugation involves delocalisation of electrons from a \(\mathrm{C-H}\) \(\sigma\)-bond adjacent to a \(\pi\)-bond or carbocation.

    In option \((a)\), \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH_2}\) has methyl \(\mathrm{C-H}\) bonds adjacent to the double bond, so it shows hyperconjugation.

    Therefore, the correct answer is \(\boxed{(a)}\).

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Hyperconjugation:

      \(\sigma(\mathrm{C-H})\) electrons delocalise into adjacent \(\pi\)-bond or vacant \(p\)-orbital

    • Needed condition:

      \(\alpha\)-hydrogen next to \(\mathrm{C=C}\) or \(\mathrm{C^+}\)

    • Also called: no-bond resonance
    • Not hyperconjugation:
      • lone-pair resonance
      • inductive effect
      • normal reaction step
    • In this question:

      \((a)\) only

    Answer: \((a)\) I and V only

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    1. Core idea of Kjeldahl’s method

      Kjeldahl’s method is used to estimate nitrogen in many organic compounds by converting the nitrogen present into ammonium sulfate during digestion with concentrated \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\).

      This method works well when nitrogen is present in forms such as amino \((-\mathrm{NH_2})\) or related easily convertible organic nitrogen forms.

      It does not work for nitrogen present in:

      • nitro group \((-\mathrm{NO_2})\)
      • azo group \((-\mathrm{N=N}-)\)
      • diazonium salts
      • nitrogen present inside certain aromatic heterocyclic ring systems
    2. Now check each compound
      • I: Aniline, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5NH_2}\)

        Nitrogen is present in the amino form \((-\mathrm{NH_2})\), so it is suitable for Kjeldahl’s method.

      • II: Benzene diazonium chloride, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5N_2^+Cl^-}\)

        This is a diazonium salt. Nitrogen in this form is not estimated by Kjeldahl’s method.

      • III: Nitrobenzene, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5NO_2}\)

        Nitrogen is present in the nitro group \((-\mathrm{NO_2})\), so it is not suitable.

      • IV: Pyridine

        Nitrogen is present in an aromatic heterocyclic ring. Such ring nitrogen is not estimated by Kjeldahl’s method.

      • V: Benzylamine, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2NH_2}\)

        Nitrogen is present in the amino form \((-\mathrm{NH_2})\), so it is suitable for Kjeldahl’s method.

    3. Hence

      Only compounds I and V are suitable.

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(a)\ \text{I and V only}}\)

    Why this method works:

    • In Kjeldahl’s method, organic nitrogen must be converted into \(\mathrm{NH_4^+}\) during digestion.
    • Amines and similar compounds usually do this readily.
    • Nitro nitrogen, diazo/diazonium nitrogen, and ring nitrogen do not get converted properly under Kjeldahl conditions, so the method fails for them.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Kjeldahl’s method estimates nitrogen in:

      amines, amides, and many ordinary organic nitrogen compounds

    • It does not estimate nitrogen in:
      • nitro compounds
      • azo compounds
      • diazonium salts
      • ring nitrogen-containing heterocycles such as pyridine
    • This is a very common direct-concept question from Practical Organic Chemistry / detection-estimation methods.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • First mark all compounds with plain amino group \((-\mathrm{NH_2})\) as usually allowed.
    • Immediately eliminate:
      • \((-\mathrm{NO_2})\) nitro
      • diazonium \(\mathrm{N_2^+}\)
      • ring nitrogen like pyridine
    • That leaves only I and V.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking every nitrogen-containing compound can be estimated by Kjeldahl’s method.
    2. Forgetting that nitro compounds are excluded.
    3. Forgetting that diazonium salts are excluded.
    4. Confusing amine nitrogen with aromatic ring nitrogen.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – Practical methods / detection of elements
    • Topic: Estimation of nitrogen
    • Subtopic: Kjeldahl’s method and its limitations

    Final exam-style answer:

    Kjeldahl’s method is suitable for compounds in which nitrogen is present in amino-type form, but not for nitrogen present in nitro group, diazonium salt, or aromatic ring nitrogen.

    Therefore:

    • I \((\text{aniline})\) is suitable
    • II \((\text{diazonium salt})\) is not suitable
    • III \((\text{nitrobenzene})\) is not suitable
    • IV \((\text{pyridine})\) is not suitable
    • V \((\text{benzylamine})\) is suitable

    Hence, the correct answer is \(\boxed{(a)\ \text{I and V only}}\).

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Kjeldahl works for:

      amine-type nitrogen

    • Kjeldahl does not work for:
      • nitro nitrogen
      • azo nitrogen
      • diazonium nitrogen
      • ring nitrogen in heterocycles
    • In this question:

      I and V are amines, so they are suitable.

    Answer: \((c)\ 4,\ 3\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    1. First understand what “acidic hydrogen in alkyne” means

      Only terminal alkynes contain an acidic hydrogen.

      General form of a terminal alkyne:

      \(\mathrm{R-C \equiv C-H}\)

      Internal alkynes do not have hydrogen directly attached to the triple-bond carbon, so they do not show this acidity.

    2. Check the molecular formula

      Given formula is \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\).

      For an acyclic alkyne, general formula is:

      \(C_nH_{2n-2}\)

      For \(n=6\):

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_{2(6)-2} = C_6H_{10}}\)

      So we need all open-chain alkynes having six carbons.

    3. Degree of unsaturation check

      Another quick check:

      \(\mathrm{DBE = C + 1 - \frac{H}{2}}\)

      \(\mathrm{DBE = 6 + 1 - \frac{10}{2} = 7 - 5 = 2}\)

      A triple bond contributes \(2\) degrees of unsaturation, so this fits an alkyne perfectly.

    4. Now list all possible alkynes of \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\)
      • Hex-\(1\)-yne

        \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3}\)

        Terminal alkyne \(\Rightarrow\) acidic hydrogen present

      • Hex-\(2\)-yne

        \(\mathrm{CH_3-C \equiv C-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3}\)

        Internal alkyne \(\Rightarrow\) no acidic hydrogen

      • Hex-\(3\)-yne

        \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH_2-C \equiv C-CH_2-CH_3}\)

        Internal alkyne \(\Rightarrow\) no acidic hydrogen

      • \(3\)-Methylpent-\(1\)-yne

        \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-CH(CH_3)-CH_2-CH_3}\)

        Terminal alkyne \(\Rightarrow\) acidic hydrogen present

      • \(4\)-Methylpent-\(1\)-yne

        \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-CH_2-CH(CH_3)-CH_3}\)

        Terminal alkyne \(\Rightarrow\) acidic hydrogen present

      • \(4\)-Methylpent-\(2\)-yne

        \(\mathrm{CH_3-C \equiv C-CH(CH_3)-CH_3}\)

        Internal alkyne \(\Rightarrow\) no acidic hydrogen

      • \(3,3\)-Dimethylbut-\(1\)-yne

        \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-C(CH_3)_2-CH_3}\)

        Terminal alkyne \(\Rightarrow\) acidic hydrogen present

    5. Now count them
      • Alkynes with acidic hydrogen \(=\) terminal alkynes

        Hex-\(1\)-yne, \(3\)-methylpent-\(1\)-yne, \(4\)-methylpent-\(1\)-yne, \(3,3\)-dimethylbut-\(1\)-yne

        So, \(x = 4\)

      • Alkynes with no acidic hydrogen \(=\) internal alkynes

        Hex-\(2\)-yne, Hex-\(3\)-yne, \(4\)-methylpent-\(2\)-yne

        So, \(y = 3\)

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{x=4,\ y=3}\)

    \(\boxed{(c)\ 4,\ 3}\)

    Why this method works:

    • The hydrogen attached directly to an \(sp\)-hybridised carbon in a terminal alkyne is relatively acidic.
    • This is because the conjugate base formed, \(\mathrm{R-C \equiv C^-}\), is stabilised by the high \(s\)-character of the \(sp\)-hybridised carbon.
    • Internal alkynes lack this terminal \(\mathrm{-C \equiv C-H}\) bond, so they do not have acidic hydrogen of this type.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • General formula of open-chain alkynes:

      \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n-2}}\)

    • Terminal alkyne:

      \(\mathrm{R-C \equiv C-H}\)

    • Internal alkyne:

      \(\mathrm{R-C \equiv C-R'}\)

    • Terminal alkynes are acidic enough to react with strong bases and also form precipitates with ammoniacal silver nitrate and ammoniacal cuprous chloride.
    • Internal alkynes do not show these reactions because they lack acidic terminal hydrogen.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Do not start with theory. First list all isomeric alkynes of \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\).
    • Then simply split them into:
      • \(\mathrm{-C \equiv C-H}\) present \(\Rightarrow\) count in \(x\)
      • \(\mathrm{-C \equiv C-H}\) absent \(\Rightarrow\) count in \(y\)
    • Fast memory pattern for \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\):

      straight chain \(= 3\) alkynes, branched \(= 4\) alkynes, total \(= 7\)

      Among these, terminal \(= 4\), internal \(= 3\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Forgetting that only terminal alkynes have acidic hydrogen.
    2. Missing one branched isomer such as \(3,3\)-dimethylbut-\(1\)-yne.
    3. Counting the same structure twice due to reversed numbering.
    4. Confusing alkene isomers with alkyne isomers.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
    • Topic: Alkynes
    • Subtopic: Isomer counting and acidic character of terminal alkynes

    Final exam-style answer:

    For molecular formula \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\), the possible open-chain alkyne isomers are:

    • Hex-\(1\)-yne
    • Hex-\(2\)-yne
    • Hex-\(3\)-yne
    • \(3\)-Methylpent-\(1\)-yne
    • \(4\)-Methylpent-\(1\)-yne
    • \(4\)-Methylpent-\(2\)-yne
    • \(3,3\)-Dimethylbut-\(1\)-yne

    Terminal alkynes contain acidic hydrogen, whereas internal alkynes do not.

    Therefore, alkynes with acidic hydrogen \(= 4\) and alkynes with no acidic hydrogen \(= 3\).

    \(\boxed{(c)\ 4,\ 3}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Open-chain alkyne formula:

      \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n-2}}\)

    • Terminal alkyne:

      \(\mathrm{R-C \equiv C-H}\)

    • Internal alkyne:

      \(\mathrm{R-C \equiv C-R'}\)

    • Acidic hydrogen present in:

      terminal alkynes only

    • For \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\):

      total alkyne isomers \(= 7\)

      terminal \(= 4\), internal \(= 3\)

    Answer: \((d)\ \mathrm{C_6H_5C(Br)(CH_3)C_6H_5}\)


    Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    1. Core idea of \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) reaction

      In an \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) mechanism, the leaving group first departs to form a carbocation.

      Therefore, rate depends mainly on the stability of the carbocation formed.

      Better carbocation stability \(\Rightarrow\) faster \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) reaction.

    2. Compare the carbocations formed by each option
      • (a) \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2Br}\)

        Forms benzyl carbocation: \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2^+}\)

        This is a primary benzylic carbocation. It is resonance-stabilised, so it is fairly stable.

      • (b) \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH(Br)CH_3}\)

        Forms: \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH^+CH_3}\)

        This is a secondary benzylic carbocation. It is more stable than (a) because it has both resonance stabilisation and \(+I\) effect / hyperconjugation from \(\mathrm{CH_3}\).

      • (c) \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH(Br)C_6H_5}\)

        Forms: \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH^+C_6H_5}\)

        Here the positive charge is stabilised by two phenyl rings, so this carbocation is very stable.

      • (d) \(\mathrm{C_6H_5C(Br)(CH_3)C_6H_5}\)

        Forms: \(\mathrm{C_6H_5C^+(CH_3)C_6H_5}\)

        This is a tertiary benzylic carbocation.

        It is stabilised by:

        • resonance with two phenyl groups
        • \(+I\) effect and hyperconjugation from \(\mathrm{CH_3}\)
        • overall greater substitution at the carbocation center

        Hence this is the most stable carbocation among all the options.

    3. Therefore

      Since \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) reactivity follows carbocation stability:

      \((d) > (c) > (b) > (a)\)

      So the most reactive compound towards \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) mechanism is \((d)\).

    Useful stability picture:

    Why this works (JEE / NCERT theory):

    • \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) stands for unimolecular nucleophilic substitution.
    • Its slow step is:

      \(\mathrm{R{-}X \rightarrow R^+ + X^-}\)

    • So its rate law is:

      \(\mathrm{Rate} = k[\mathrm{R{-}X}]\)

    • Since carbocation is formed in the rate-determining step, anything that stabilises the carbocation increases \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) reactivity.
    • Benzylic carbocations are especially stable because the positive charge is delocalised into the benzene ring by resonance.
    • More alkyl substitution usually increases carbocation stability because of \(+I\) effect and hyperconjugation.
    • Thus, a tertiary benzylic carbocation is usually more stable than a secondary or primary benzylic carbocation.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Do not think about nucleophile first. For \(S_\mathrm{N}1\), immediately think: which option gives the most stable carbocation?
    • Spot the benzylic carbon first.
    • Then check how many groups stabilise the positive charge:
      • phenyl rings by resonance
      • alkyl groups by \(+I\) effect / hyperconjugation
    • Option \((d)\) has the best combination: two phenyl groups + one methyl group.
    • So you can mark \((d)\) very quickly without writing full mechanism.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking only about degree of carbon and ignoring benzylic resonance.
    2. Confusing \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) with \(S_\mathrm{N}2\). In \(S_\mathrm{N}2\), steric hindrance matters more; in \(S_\mathrm{N}1\), carbocation stability is the key.
    3. Missing that option \((d)\) forms a carbocation stabilised by two phenyl groups, not one.
    4. Assuming primary benzylic is always best just because “benzylic is reactive”. Here a much more stabilised benzylic carbocation is possible in \((d)\).

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
    • Topic: Nucleophilic substitution reactions
    • Subtopic: \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) mechanism and carbocation stability of benzylic halides

    Final exam-style answer:

    In \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) mechanism, rate depends on stability of the carbocation formed after departure of the leaving group.

    Option \((d)\), \(\mathrm{C_6H_5C(Br)(CH_3)C_6H_5}\), forms the carbocation \(\mathrm{C_6H_5C^+(CH_3)C_6H_5}\), which is a highly stable tertiary benzylic carbocation.

    It is stabilised by resonance with two phenyl groups and by the \(+I\) effect of \(\mathrm{CH_3}\).

    Therefore, it shows maximum reactivity towards \(S_\mathrm{N}1\) mechanism.

    \(\boxed{(d)}\)

    Answer: \((a)\ C_2H_6,\ CO_2\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    1. Identify product \(X\)

      Ethane undergoes controlled oxidation in presence of manganese acetate \((\mathrm{CH_3COO})_2\mathrm{Mn}\) and heat to give acetic acid.

      \( \mathrm{C_2H_6 + \frac{3}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow[(\Delta)]{(CH_3COO)_2Mn} CH_3COOH} \)

      Hence, \(X = \mathrm{CH_3COOH}\) (ethanoic acid).

    2. Identify product \(Y\)

      Carboxylic acids react with sodium metal to form sodium carboxylate.

      \( \mathrm{CH_3COOH + Na \rightarrow CH_3COO^-Na^+ + \frac{1}{2}H_2} \)

      So, \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3COONa}\) (sodium acetate).

    3. Now apply Kolbe’s electrolysis

      In aqueous solution, sodium acetate gives acetate ion:

      \( \mathrm{CH_3COONa \rightarrow CH_3COO^- + Na^+} \)

      At the anode, acetate ion loses electron:

      \( \mathrm{CH_3COO^- \rightarrow CH_3^\bullet + CO_2 + e^-} \)

      Two methyl radicals combine:

      \( \mathrm{CH_3^\bullet + CH_3^\bullet \rightarrow C_2H_6} \)

      Therefore overall anode reaction is:

      \( \mathrm{2CH_3COO^- \rightarrow C_2H_6 + 2CO_2 + 2e^-} \)

      So the gases formed at anode are:

      • \(P = \mathrm{C_2H_6}\)
      • \(Q = \mathrm{CO_2}\)

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(a)\ C_2H_6,\ CO_2}\)

    Key theory you should know:

    • Kolbe’s electrolysis: Electrolysis of aqueous solution of sodium or potassium salts of carboxylic acids gives alkane at the anode.
    • General anode reaction:

      \( \mathrm{2RCOO^- \rightarrow R-R + 2CO_2 + 2e^-} \)

    • Thus:

      \( \mathrm{RCOO^- \xrightarrow{electrolysis} alkane\ with\ even\ number\ of\ carbon\ atoms} \)

    • For acetate ion \(\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}\), the radical formed is \(\mathrm{CH_3^\bullet}\), so coupling gives ethane.
    • The decarboxylation step explains why \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) is also produced.

    Very important JEE / NCERT pattern:

    • Sodium salt of carboxylic acid \(\rightarrow\) on Kolbe electrolysis gives alkane by radical coupling.
    • If the acid is:

      \(\mathrm{RCOOH}\)

      its sodium salt is:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa}\)

      and electrolysis gives:

      \(\mathrm{R-R}\)

    • Carbon count trick:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COO^- \rightarrow CH_3^\bullet \rightarrow C_2H_6}\)

    1-minute solving trick:

    • As soon as you see carboxylate salt + electrolysis, think Kolbe electrolysis.
    • Write instantly:

      \( \mathrm{2RCOO^- \rightarrow R-R + 2CO_2} \)

    • Here \(R=\mathrm{CH_3}\), so product alkane must be:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH_3 = C_2H_6}\)

    • The second gas is automatically \(\mathrm{CO_2}\).
    • So no need to work through long mechanism in exam if you recognize the pattern fast.

    Quick intuitive view:

    • Acetate ion:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COO^-}\)

    • Loses \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) at anode and leaves methyl fragment:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3^\bullet}\)

    • Two methyl fragments join: 
      CH3•  +  •CH3  →  CH3—CH3

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking \(\mathrm{CH_4}\) forms at anode. In Kolbe electrolysis, radical coupling gives \(\mathrm{C_2H_6}\), not methane.
    2. Forgetting that \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) is released due to decarboxylation.
    3. Confusing sodium metal reaction with soda lime decarboxylation. These are different reactions.
    4. Ignoring that the question asks gases at anode, not cathode.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Reactions of carboxylic acids and their salts
    • Subtopic: Kolbe’s electrolysis of sodium carboxylates

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(X\) is ethanoic acid, \(\mathrm{CH_3COOH}\).

    On reaction with sodium, it gives sodium acetate, so \(Y=\mathrm{CH_3COONa}\).

    During Kolbe’s electrolysis:

    \( \mathrm{2CH_3COO^- \rightarrow C_2H_6 + 2CO_2 + 2e^-} \)

    Hence, \(P=\mathrm{C_2H_6}\) and \(Q=\mathrm{CO_2}\).

    \(\boxed{(a)\ C_2H_6,\ CO_2}\)

    Answer: \((d)\) nitrogen

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    This question is based on Lassaigne’s test for detection of nitrogen in an organic compound.

    1. What happens in sodium fusion?

      In sodium fusion, covalently bonded nitrogen present in the organic compound is converted into ionic sodium cyanide:

      \(\mathrm{Na + C + N \rightarrow NaCN}\)

      So, if nitrogen is present, the sodium fusion extract contains \(\mathrm{CN^-}\).

    2. Action of iron(II) sulphate

      When the sodium fusion extract is boiled with iron(II) sulphate, \(\mathrm{CN^-}\) reacts with \(\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}\) to form sodium ferrocyanide:

      \(\mathrm{6NaCN + FeSO_4 \rightarrow Na_4[Fe(CN)_6] + Na_2SO_4}\)

    3. Action of concentrated \(H_2SO_4\)

      On acidification, some \(\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}\) gets oxidised to \(\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}\). Then ferric ions react with ferrocyanide ions to produce Prussian blue colour:

      \(\mathrm{3Na_4[Fe(CN)_6] + 4Fe^{3+} \rightarrow Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3 \cdot xH_2O}\)

      The compound \(\mathrm{Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3 \cdot xH_2O}\) is Prussian blue.

    4. Conclusion

      Since Prussian blue colour is obtained, the element confirmed is nitrogen.

      Therefore, the correct option is \((d)\) nitrogen.

    Why this test works:

    • Organic compounds contain nitrogen in covalent form, so it cannot be detected directly by ordinary ionic tests.
    • Sodium fusion converts it into ionic \(\mathrm{CN^-}\), which can then be detected.
    • The appearance of Prussian blue is the characteristic test for nitrogen.

    JEE / NCERT theory to remember:

    • Lassaigne’s test: used to detect extra elements like nitrogen, sulphur and halogens in organic compounds.
    • For nitrogen:

      \(\mathrm{NaCN}\) is formed during sodium fusion.

    • Main observation: formation of Prussian blue colour.
    • Blue colour means: nitrogen is present.
    • If both nitrogen and sulphur are present, sodium fusion may form \(\mathrm{NaSCN}\), which with \(\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}\) gives blood red colour, not Prussian blue.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Just memorize:

      Prussian blue \(\Rightarrow\) nitrogen

    • Blood red \(\Rightarrow\) nitrogen + sulphur together \(\left(\mathrm{SCN^-}\right)\)
    • In MCQs, whenever you see:

      sodium fusion extract \(+\) \(\mathrm{FeSO_4}\) \(+\) acid \(+\) Prussian blue

      mark nitrogen immediately.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing Prussian blue colour with halogen test.
    2. Thinking sulphur alone gives blue colour. It does not.
    3. Forgetting that the test detects nitrogen only after sodium fusion.
    4. Mixing up:
      • Prussian blue \(\Rightarrow\) nitrogen
      • Blood red \(\Rightarrow\) nitrogen + sulphur

    Quick memory map:

    • \(\mathrm{N}\) in organic compound \(\rightarrow \mathrm{NaCN}\)
    • \(\mathrm{NaCN + Fe^{2+} \rightarrow [Fe(CN)_6]^{4-}}\)
    • \([Fe(CN)_6]^{4-} + \mathrm{Fe^{3+}} \rightarrow\) Prussian blue
    • Hence nitrogen is confirmed.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – Qualitative Analysis
    • Topic: Detection of extra elements
    • Subtopic: Lassaigne’s test for nitrogen

    Final exam-style answer:

    In sodium fusion test, nitrogen present in the organic compound is converted into \(\mathrm{NaCN}\).

    This reacts with \(\mathrm{FeSO_4}\) to form sodium ferrocyanide, which on treatment with acid and \(\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}\) gives Prussian blue colour due to formation of \(\mathrm{Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3 \cdot xH_2O}\).

    Hence the element present is nitrogen.

    \(\boxed{(d)\ \text{nitrogen}}\)

    Answer: \((c)\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    We have to identify the reaction which is an electrophilic substitution reaction.

    1. Check option \((a)\)

      \(CH_3CHO + HCN \longrightarrow CH_3CH(OH)CN\)

      Here, HCN adds across the carbonyl group of aldehyde. This is an addition reaction, more specifically nucleophilic addition.

      So, this is not electrophilic substitution.

    2. Check option \((b)\)

      \((CH_3)_3CX + H_2O \longrightarrow (CH_3)_3COH + HX\)

      Here, halogen \(X\) is replaced by \(OH\). This is a substitution reaction, but it is nucleophilic substitution, not electrophilic substitution.

      So, this is not the correct answer.

    3. Check option \((c)\)

      \(C_6H_6 + CH_3COCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5(COCH_3) + HCl\)

      This is Friedel–Crafts acylation of benzene.

      In this reaction, benzene ring reacts with an electrophile generated from acetyl chloride in presence of Lewis acid \(AlCl_3\). One hydrogen atom of benzene is replaced by the acyl group \((COCH_3)\).

      Since an electrophile attacks the aromatic ring and a hydrogen is substituted, this is an electrophilic substitution reaction.

    4. Check option \((d)\)

      \(BrCH_2CH_2Br + Zn \xrightarrow[\Delta]{Alcohol} CH_2{=}CH_2 + ZnBr_2\)

      This is an elimination reaction (dehalogenation), not a substitution reaction.

    Therefore, the correct option is \((c)\).

    Core theory behind option \((c)\):

    • Benzene is an electron-rich aromatic ring because of its delocalized \(\pi\)-electrons.
    • So benzene is attacked by electrophiles.
    • In Friedel–Crafts acylation, acetyl chloride reacts with \(AlCl_3\) to generate the acylium ion, which acts as the electrophile.
    • The electrophile is:

      \(CH_3CO^+\)

    • This electrophile attacks benzene and finally one \(H\) of benzene is replaced by \((COCH_3)\).

    Important reaction steps:

    1. Generation of electrophile

      \(CH_3COCl + AlCl_3 \rightarrow CH_3CO^+ + AlCl_4^-\)

    2. Attack on benzene ring

      Benzene donates \(\pi\)-electrons to the electrophile and forms a sigma-complex.

    3. Loss of proton

      The sigma-complex loses \(H^+\), aromaticity is restored, and acyl benzene is formed.

    Why benzene gives substitution, not addition:

    • Benzene is highly stable due to aromaticity.
    • Addition reaction would destroy aromaticity, so benzene generally prefers substitution reactions.
    • That is why halogenation, nitration, sulphonation, Friedel–Crafts alkylation and Friedel–Crafts acylation are all electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Electrophile: electron-deficient species that accepts an electron pair.
    • Nucleophile: electron-rich species that donates an electron pair.
    • Benzene reactions: mostly electrophilic substitution reactions.
    • Common electrophilic substitution reactions of benzene:
      • Nitration
      • Sulphonation
      • Halogenation
      • Friedel–Crafts alkylation
      • Friedel–Crafts acylation
    • \(AlCl_3\) acts as a Lewis acid catalyst and helps in generation of electrophile.
    • In acylation, the attacking electrophile is the acylium ion.

    How to identify each option quickly:

    • If a reagent is added across a double bond or carbonyl, think addition.
    • If one group is replaced by \(OH^-\), \(CN^-\), \(NH_3\), etc., think nucleophilic substitution.
    • If a small molecule is removed and a double bond is formed, think elimination.
    • If benzene reacts in presence of \(AlCl_3\), \(FeCl_3\), concentrated acids, etc., think electrophilic substitution.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • In MCQs, whenever you see:

      \(Benzene + reagent \xrightarrow{AlCl_3 / FeCl_3}\)

      immediately suspect electrophilic substitution.

    • Here \(C_6H_6\) with \(CH_3COCl / AlCl_3\) is a direct clue for Friedel–Crafts acylation.
    • So you can mark \((c)\) almost instantly.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing substitution with electrophilic substitution.
    2. Thinking every substitution reaction is electrophilic. It is not.
    3. Forgetting that aldehydes and ketones usually undergo nucleophilic addition.
    4. Forgetting that benzene usually undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution.
    5. Missing the importance of \(AlCl_3\), which is a classic clue for Friedel–Crafts reaction.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – Hydrocarbons
    • Topic: Benzene and aromatic compounds
    • Subtopic: Electrophilic aromatic substitution and Friedel–Crafts acylation

    Final exam-style answer:

    Among the given reactions, option \((c)\) \(C_6H_6 + CH_3COCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5(COCH_3) + HCl\) is Friedel–Crafts acylation.

    In this reaction, acetyl chloride in presence of \(AlCl_3\) generates an electrophile which attacks the benzene ring, and one hydrogen of benzene is replaced by the acyl group.

    Therefore, it is an electrophilic substitution reaction.

    \(\boxed{(c)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Electrophilic substitution:

      Electron-rich aromatic ring + electrophile \(\rightarrow\) substitution

    • Benzene usually shows:

      Electrophilic substitution

    • Friedel–Crafts acylation:

      \(Ar{-}H + RCOCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} Ar{-}COR + HCl\)

    • Electrophile in acylation:

      \(RCO^+\)

    • Clue words:

      \(Benzene\), \(AlCl_3\), \(FeCl_3\), nitration, sulphonation

    • Aldehyde + HCN:

      Nucleophilic addition

    • Tertiary halide + water:

      Nucleophilic substitution

    • Vicinal dihalide + Zn:

      Elimination

    Answer: \((c)\) 3-ethyl-2-methylpent-2-ene

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    Ozonolysis cleaves the double bond of an alkene and converts the two doubly bonded carbon atoms into carbonyl compounds.

    1. Use the products to reconstruct the alkene

      Given products are:

      • simplest ketone \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\) (propanone / acetone)
      • 3-pentanone \(= \mathrm{CH_3CH_2COCH_2CH_3}\)

      In ozonolysis, each carbon of the original \(C=C\) becomes a carbonyl carbon.

      So we work backward:

      • \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\) means one alkene carbon was attached to two \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) groups.
      • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2COCH_2CH_3}\) means the other alkene carbon was attached to two \(\mathrm{C_2H_5}\)-type sides? Not exactly as a ketone carbonyl carbon, it comes from one alkene carbon bonded to \(\mathrm{C_2H_5}\) and \(\mathrm{CH_2CH_2CH_3}\) pattern only if wrongly reconstructed. So instead we should check the given options carefully and test them.
    2. Test option \((c)\)

      Option \((c)\): 3-ethyl-2-methylpent-2-ene

      Its structure can be written as:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3- C(CH_3)=C(CH_2CH_3)-CH_2-CH_3}\)

      Equivalently:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH_2-C(CH_3)=CH-CH_2-CH_3}\) is not the correct expanded form for this name, so we must stay careful with naming. The correct carbon skeleton from the name gives one double-bond carbon attached to two methyl groups and the other attached to two ethyl groups.

      On ozonolysis:

      \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_2C=C(C_2H_5)_2 \xrightarrow[Zn/H_2O]{O_3} (CH_3)_2CO + (C_2H_5)_2CO}\)

      That gives:

      • \((\mathrm{CH_3})_2\mathrm{CO}\) = propanone = simplest ketone
      • \((\mathrm{C_2H_5})_2\mathrm{CO}\) = 3-pentanone

      So option \((c)\) matches perfectly.

    3. Why the others are wrong
      • \((a)\) 2,3-dimethylbut-2-ene gives only propanone on both sides, not a mixture of propanone and 3-pentanone.
      • \((b)\) does not produce the required pair of ketones on cleavage.
      • \((d)\) is not the correct IUPAC form; substituents must be written alphabetically, so proper naming would place ethyl before methyl if that structure were intended.

    Core theory used here:

    • Ozonolysis is an oxidative cleavage of alkene double bond.
    • With reductive workup such as \( \mathrm{Zn/H_2O} \), the alkene splits into aldehydes and/or ketones.
    • If a double-bond carbon has:
      • two alkyl groups, it gives a ketone
      • one hydrogen and one alkyl group, it gives an aldehyde
      • two hydrogens, it gives methanal
    • Here both products are ketones, so both alkene carbons must have had no hydrogen attached.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Ozonolysis is one of the most important methods to locate and reconstruct the position of \(C=C\).
    • To solve reverse ozonolysis questions, join the two carbonyl carbons back with a double bond after removing oxygen.
    • If product is a ketone, the original alkene carbon had no H.
    • If product is an aldehyde, the original alkene carbon had one H.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Immediately identify:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\) means one alkene carbon was attached to \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) and \(\mathrm{CH_3}\).

    • Also:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2COCH_2CH_3}\) means the other alkene carbon was attached to \(\mathrm{C_2H_5}\) and \(\mathrm{C_2H_5}\).

    • So the alkene must be:

      \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_2C=C(C_2H_5)_2}\)

    • Now just match the IUPAC name from the options:

      \(\boxed{\text{3-ethyl-2-methylpent-2-ene}}\)

    How to reconstruct quickly in reverse ozonolysis:

    1. Write both carbonyl products.
    2. Mark the carbonyl carbon in each.
    3. Remove oxygen from both carbonyl groups.
    4. Join those two carbons by \(C=C\).
    5. Then name the alkene correctly.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing “simplest ketone” with ethanone. The simplest ketone is propanone, \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\).
    2. Forgetting that a ketone product means the original alkene carbon had no hydrogen.
    3. Choosing the correct structure but wrong IUPAC name.
    4. Ignoring alphabetical order in substituent naming.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
    • Topic: Alkenes
    • Subtopic: Ozonolysis and structural determination of alkenes

    Final exam-style answer:

    On ozonolysis, alkene cleavage gives two carbonyl compounds.

    Given products are simplest ketone \(=\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\) and 3-pentanone \(=\mathrm{CH_3CH_2COCH_2CH_3}\).

    Therefore the original alkene must be obtained by joining the carbonyl carbons back through a double bond:

    \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_2C=C(C_2H_5)_2}\)

    The IUPAC name of this alkene is:

    \(\boxed{\text{3-ethyl-2-methylpent-2-ene}}\)

    Hence, the correct option is \(\boxed{(c)}\).

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Ozonolysis: breaks \(C=C\) into carbonyl compounds.
    • Ketone formed: original alkene carbon had no H.
    • Aldehyde formed: original alkene carbon had one H.
    • Simplest ketone: propanone, \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\).
    • Reverse ozonolysis trick: remove O from products and join carbonyl carbons by \(C=C\).

    Answer: \((b)\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    Polymers TTR

    Solution:

    1. Identify compound \(P\)

      Ethylene reacts with cold, dilute alkaline \(KMnO_4\) at \(273\ K\) to give a vicinal diol.

      \(CH_2{=}CH_2 \xrightarrow[\ 273\ K\ ]{\text{cold, dilute alkaline }KMnO_4} HO{-}CH_2{-}CH_2{-}OH\)

      So compound \(P\) is ethylene glycol (ethane-\(1,2\)-diol).

    2. Now recall how dacron is formed

      Dacron is a polyester formed by condensation polymerisation of:

      • a diol: ethylene glycol \(HO{-}CH_2{-}CH_2{-}OH\)
      • a dicarboxylic acid: terephthalic acid \(HOOC{-}C_6H_4{-}COOH\)

      Therefore \(P\) must react with terephthalic acid.

      Among the given options, terephthalic acid is \((b)\).

    3. Polymer formed

      \(n\ HO{-}CH_2{-}CH_2{-}OH + n\ HOOC{-}C_6H_4{-}COOH \rightarrow \left[{-}O{-}CH_2{-}CH_2{-}O{-}CO{-}C_6H_4{-}CO{-}\right]_n + 2nH_2O\)

      This polymer is dacron (also called PET in polymer chemistry context).



    Why \((b)\) is correct:

    • Dacron is always obtained from a diol + dicarboxylic acid.
    • Ethylene gives ethylene glycol on hydroxylation with cold dilute alkaline \(KMnO_4\).
    • The required aromatic dicarboxylic acid for dacron is terephthalic acid, i.e. benzene-\(1,4\)-dicarboxylic acid.

    NCERT / JEE theory behind this question:

    • Cold, dilute alkaline \(KMnO_4\) is used for hydroxylation of alkenes.
    • Alkenes under these mild conditions form vicinal glycols (adjacent carbon atoms each get one \(OH\) group).
    • Thus:

      \(RCH{=}CHR \rightarrow RCH(OH)-CH(OH)R\)

    • Ethylene therefore gives ethylene glycol.
    • Polyesters are formed by condensation polymerisation between:
      • a compound with two \(-OH\) groups
      • a compound with two \(-COOH\) groups
    • Dacron is the classic polyester made from:
      • ethylene glycol
      • terephthalic acid

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See cold dilute alkaline \(KMnO_4\) \(\Rightarrow\) immediately think: alkene \(\rightarrow\) glycol.
    • Ethylene \(\rightarrow\) ethylene glycol.
    • Now see the word dacron \(\Rightarrow\) remember: ethylene glycol + terephthalic acid.
    • So directly mark the option containing \(HOOC{-}C_6H_4{-}COOH\) in para position.

    Fast memory link:

    • Dacron = Diol + Diacid
    • Ethylene + cold \(KMnO_4\) = Ethylene glycol
    • Ethylene glycol + Terephthalic acid = Dacron

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing cold dilute \(KMnO_4\) with strong oxidative cleavage conditions.
    2. Forgetting that dacron needs a dicarboxylic acid, not a mono-carboxylic acid.
    3. Choosing phthalic acid isomers without checking which one is used in dacron.
    4. Missing that terephthalic acid is the para-dicarboxylic acid.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Polymers
    • Topic: Reactions of alkenes and condensation polymers
    • Subtopic: Hydroxylation of ethene to ethylene glycol and formation of dacron

    Final exam-style answer:

    Ethylene on treatment with cold, dilute alkaline \(KMnO_4\) at \(273\ K\) gives ethylene glycol \(\left(HO{-}CH_2{-}CH_2{-}OH\right)\).

    Dacron is formed by condensation polymerisation of ethylene glycol with terephthalic acid \(\left(HOOC{-}C_6H_4{-}COOH\right)\).

    Hence the correct option is \(\boxed{(b)}\).

    Answer: \((a)\ \text{B and C only}\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    The reaction sequence involves two standard reactions from aromatic aldehydes.

    1. Identify \(X\) from toluene

      Toluene on treatment with chromyl chloride in \(\mathrm{CS_2}\) followed by hydrolysis \(\mathrm{H_3O^+}\) undergoes Etard reaction.

      So:

      \(\text{Toluene} \xrightarrow[(ii)\ \mathrm{H_3O^+}]{(i)\ \mathrm{CrO_2Cl_2/CS_2}} \text{Benzaldehyde}\)

      Hence, \(X =\) benzaldehyde \(\left(\mathrm{C_6H_5CHO}\right)\).

    2. Now identify \(Y\) and \(Z\)

      Benzaldehyde does not have any \(\alpha\)-hydrogen. Therefore, with concentrated \(\mathrm{NaOH}\), it undergoes Cannizzaro reaction.

      In Cannizzaro reaction:

      \(2\mathrm{C_6H_5CHO} + \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH} + \mathrm{C_6H_5COONa}\)

      Therefore:

      • \(Y = \mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH}\) = benzyl alcohol
      • \(Z = \mathrm{C_6H_5COONa}\) = sodium benzoate
    3. Check statement A

      Statement A says: Y is a secondary alcohol.

      But \(Y\) is benzyl alcohol, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH}\).

      The carbon bearing \(-\mathrm{OH}\) is attached to only one other carbon, so it is a primary alcohol, not secondary.

      Hence, A is false.

    4. Check statement B

      In Cannizzaro reaction, one molecule of aldehyde gets oxidized to carboxylate and the other gets reduced to alcohol.

      So benzaldehyde \((X)\) is reduced to benzyl alcohol \((Y)\).

      Hence, B is true.

    5. Check statement C

      \(Z\) is sodium benzoate, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5COONa}\).

      On heating sodium carboxylates with sodalime, decarboxylation occurs:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa + NaOH \xrightarrow[\Delta]{CaO} RH + Na_2CO_3}\)

      Therefore:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5COONa + NaOH \xrightarrow[\Delta]{CaO} C_6H_6 + Na_2CO_3}\)

      So sodium benzoate gives benzene on heating with sodalime.

      Hence, C is true.

    6. Check statement D

      Statement D says: Y does not give \(\mathrm{H_2}\) gas with Na metal.

      But alcohols do react with sodium metal to liberate hydrogen gas:

      \(2\mathrm{ROH} + 2\mathrm{Na} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{RONa} + \mathrm{H_2}\uparrow\)

      Since \(Y\) is benzyl alcohol, it does give \(\mathrm{H_2}\) with sodium.

      Hence, D is false.

    Therefore:

    Only statements B and C are correct.

    \(\boxed{(a)\ \text{B and C only}}\)

    Why this method works:

    • The first step is recognition of Etard reaction:

      \(\text{Toluene} \rightarrow \text{benzaldehyde}\)

    • The second step is recognition of Cannizzaro reaction, which is shown by:
      • an aldehyde
      • no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen
      • concentrated base
    • Once \(X =\) benzaldehyde is identified, \(Y\) and \(Z\) follow immediately as benzyl alcohol and sodium benzoate.
    • Then each statement can be tested using basic functional-group chemistry.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Etard reaction:

      \(\text{Ar-CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CrO_2Cl_2}} \text{Ar-CHO}\)

      Toluene is oxidized to benzaldehyde without full oxidation to benzoic acid.

    • Cannizzaro reaction:

      Aldehydes without \(\alpha\)-hydrogen undergo disproportionation in concentrated alkali.

      \(2\mathrm{RCHO} + \mathrm{OH^-} \rightarrow \mathrm{RCH_2OH} + \mathrm{RCOO^-}\)

    • Benzaldehyde has no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so it gives Cannizzaro reaction.
    • Sodalime decarboxylation:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa} \xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{NaOH/CaO}} \mathrm{RH}\)

    • Alcohols react with sodium metal to liberate hydrogen gas.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See \(\mathrm{CrO_2Cl_2/CS_2}\) on toluene \(\Rightarrow\) immediately think Etard reaction \(\Rightarrow X=\) benzaldehyde.
    • See benzaldehyde + conc. \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) \(\Rightarrow\) immediately think Cannizzaro reaction.
    • So directly write:

      \(Y=\) benzyl alcohol, \(Z=\) sodium benzoate

    • Then:
      • \(Y\) is primary, not secondary \(\Rightarrow A\) false
      • \(Y\) is reduction product of \(X\) \(\Rightarrow B\) true
      • \(Z\) gives benzene with sodalime \(\Rightarrow C\) true
      • Alcohol gives \(\mathrm{H_2}\) with Na \(\Rightarrow D\) false
    • Hence answer is \((a)\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing Etard oxidation with full side-chain oxidation to benzoic acid.
    2. Forgetting that benzaldehyde has no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen.
    3. Thinking Cannizzaro gives two different alcohols; it gives one alcohol and one carboxylate salt.
    4. Calling benzyl alcohol a secondary alcohol. It is a primary alcohol.
    5. Forgetting that alcohols react with sodium metal to evolve \(\mathrm{H_2}\).
    6. Missing that sodium benzoate on sodalime decarboxylation gives benzene.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Chemical reactions of aldehydes
    • Subtopic: Cannizzaro reaction of benzaldehyde

    Related chapter link:

    • The first step also connects with side-chain oxidation of aromatic compounds and named reaction:

      Etard reaction

    • The statement about \(Z\) also connects with:

      Decarboxylation of carboxylate salts using sodalime

    Final exam-style answer:
