    Toluene on treatment with \(\mathrm{CrO_2Cl_2/CS_2}\) followed by \(\mathrm{H_3O^+}\) gives benzaldehyde \((X)\) by Etard reaction.

    Benzaldehyde has no \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so with concentrated \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) it undergoes Cannizzaro reaction:

    \(2\mathrm{C_6H_5CHO} + \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH} + \mathrm{C_6H_5COONa}\)

    Thus:

    • \(Y =\) benzyl alcohol
    • \(Z =\) sodium benzoate

    Now, \(Y\) is a primary alcohol, so A is false.

    \(Y\) is the reduction product of \(X\), so B is true.

    \(Z\) on heating with sodalime gives benzene, so C is true.

    \(Y\) gives \(\mathrm{H_2}\) with Na metal, so D is false.

    Therefore, the correct statements are B and C only.

    \(\boxed{(a)\ \text{B and C only}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Etard reaction:

      \(\text{Ar-CH}_3 \rightarrow \text{Ar-CHO}\)

    • Benzaldehyde + conc. NaOH:

      \(\Rightarrow\) Cannizzaro reaction

    • Cannizzaro products:

      \(\text{alcohol} + \text{carboxylate salt}\)

    • For benzaldehyde:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2OH + C_6H_5COONa}\)

    • Sodalime rule:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa \rightarrow RH}\)

    • Alcohol + Na:

      \(\mathrm{H_2}\) gas is evolved

    • Key clue:

      \(\text{No }\alpha\text{-H} \Rightarrow \text{Cannizzaro possible}\)

    Answer: \((c)\)

    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR

    Solution:

    The reaction is solved in two clear steps: ozonolysis followed by aldol condensation.

    1. Step 1: Ozonolysis of the given alkene

      The alkene shown is tetramethylethylene, i.e. \(2,3\)-dimethyl-\(2\)-butene.

      On ozonolysis with reductive workup \(\mathrm{O_3 / Zn, H_2O}\), the double bond is cleaved and each alkene carbon becomes a carbonyl carbon.

      Since both alkene carbons are equivalent and each is attached to two methyl groups, the product formed is acetone on both sides.

      So:

      \(\text{Alkene} \xrightarrow[(ii)\ \mathrm{Zn/H_2O}]{(i)\ \mathrm{O_3}} 2\,\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\)

      Hence, \(A =\) acetone.

    2. Step 2: Reaction of acetone with \(\mathrm{Ba(OH)_2}\) and heat

      Acetone has \(\alpha\)-hydrogen atoms, so in basic medium it undergoes aldol condensation.

      First, two molecules of acetone combine to form the aldol addition product:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3 + CH_3COCH_3 \rightarrow CH_3COCH_2C(OH)(CH_3)_2}\)

      On heating, this undergoes dehydration to give the \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated ketone:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

      This matches option \((c)\).

    3. Reaction flow in one line

      \(\text{Tetramethylethylene} \xrightarrow[(ii)\ \mathrm{Zn/H_2O}]{(i)\ \mathrm{O_3}} 2\,\text{acetone} \xrightarrow[(ii)\ \Delta]{(i)\ \mathrm{Ba(OH)_2}} \text{mesityl oxide}\)

    4. Helpful reaction image


    Therefore:

    \(P = \mathrm{CH_3COCH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

    \(\boxed{(c)}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Ozonolysis always breaks the \(\mathrm{C=C}\) bond and converts the two alkene carbons into carbonyl compounds.
    • So first identify what carbonyl fragment each alkene carbon becomes.
    • Here both sides are identical, so only one carbonyl compound forms: acetone.
    • Then ask what acetone does with base and heat.
    • Ketones with \(\alpha\)-hydrogen undergo aldol condensation, and on heating give an \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated carbonyl compound.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Ozonolysis:

      \(\mathrm{R_1R_2C=CR_3R_4 \xrightarrow{O_3} carbonyl\ compounds}\)

    • Reductive workup \(\mathrm{Zn/H_2O}\) stops at aldehydes/ketones and prevents further oxidation.
    • Aldol condensation:

      Aldehydes and ketones having \(\alpha\)-hydrogen react in dilute/base medium to form \(\beta\)-hydroxy carbonyl compounds.

    • On heating, aldol products dehydrate to give \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated aldehydes or ketones.
    • Acetone self-condensation gives first diacetone alcohol, then on dehydration gives mesityl oxide.

    Key theory behind the second step:

    1. Formation of enolate

      Base removes an \(\alpha\)-hydrogen from acetone to form an enolate ion.

    2. Nucleophilic addition

      This enolate attacks the carbonyl carbon of another acetone molecule.

    3. Aldol product

      A \(\beta\)-hydroxy ketone is formed.

    4. Dehydration on heating

      Loss of water gives the conjugated enone, which is more stable.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See a highly symmetrical alkene in ozonolysis \(\Rightarrow\) check whether both halves are identical.
    • Here both alkene carbons are the same, so ozonolysis must give two identical carbonyl compounds.
    • That carbonyl is acetone.
    • Then immediately recall:

      \(\text{acetone} \xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{base}} \text{mesityl oxide}\)

    • Among the options, choose the \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated ketone, not the aldol addition product.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Stopping after ozonolysis and not using the second reagent step.
    2. Writing aldehyde instead of ketone after ozonolysis.
    3. Choosing the aldol addition product instead of the dehydrated aldol condensation product.
    4. Forgetting that heating favors dehydration and formation of conjugation.
    5. Missing the symmetry of the starting alkene and unnecessarily creating two different carbonyl compounds.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Reactions of carbonyl compounds
    • Subtopic: Aldol condensation and ozonolysis-based product prediction

    Final exam-style answer:

    On ozonolysis, the given alkene undergoes cleavage of the double bond to give two molecules of acetone.

    \(\mathrm{O_3/Zn,H_2O}\) therefore forms \(A = \mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\).

    Acetone has \(\alpha\)-hydrogen, so with \(\mathrm{Ba(OH)_2}\) it undergoes aldol condensation, and on heating the aldol product dehydrates to form:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

    This is mesityl oxide.

    Hence, the correct product is option \((c)\).

    \(\boxed{(c)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Ozonolysis: breaks \(\mathrm{C=C}\) into carbonyl compounds
    • Reductive workup: \(\mathrm{Zn/H_2O}\)
    • Acetone + base: aldol reaction possible
    • Heating after aldol: dehydration occurs
    • Final product type: \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated ketone
    • Name of final product: mesityl oxide
    • Correct option: \((c)\)

    Answer: \((b)\ \mathrm{CCl}\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    We identify each compound in the reaction sequence step by step and then find the empirical formula of the final product \(D\).

    1. Formation of compound \(A\) from ethene

      Ethene reacts with bromine in \(\mathrm{CCl_4}\) by addition across the double bond.

      \(\mathrm{CH_2{=}CH_2 + Br_2 \xrightarrow{CCl_4} BrCH_2{-}CH_2Br}\)

      So, compound \(A\) is \(\mathrm{C_2H_4Br_2}\), that is, 1,2-dibromoethane.

      • This is a standard reaction of alkenes.
      • \(\pi\)-bond breaks and two new \(\mathrm{C{-}Br}\) bonds are formed.
    2. Conversion of \(A\) into compound \(B\)

      The reagent sequence is:

      \((i)\ \mathrm{alc.\ KOH} \quad (ii)\ \mathrm{NaNH_2}\)

      This indicates successive dehydrohalogenation.

      First elimination:

      \(\mathrm{BrCH_2{-}CH_2Br \xrightarrow{alc.\ KOH} CH_2{=}CHBr}\)

      Second elimination:

      \(\mathrm{CH_2{=}CHBr \xrightarrow{NaNH_2} HC{\equiv}CH}\)

      So, compound \(B\) is \(\mathrm{C_2H_2}\), that is, ethyne (acetylene).

      • NCERT/JEE theory: vicinal dihalides on strong elimination can give alkynes.
      • \(\mathrm{alc.\ KOH}\) usually removes one molecule of \(\mathrm{HX}\).
      • \(\mathrm{NaNH_2}\) is a much stronger base and can remove the second \(\mathrm{HX}\) from a vinyl halide.
    3. Conversion of \(B\) into compound \(C\)

      The question says cyclic polymerisation.

      A very important standard reaction is:

      \(\mathrm{3HC{\equiv}CH \rightarrow C_6H_6}\)

      Thus, compound \(C\) is benzene, with molecular formula \(\mathrm{C_6H_6}\).

      • JEE memory point: three molecules of ethyne undergo cyclic polymerisation to form benzene.


      • Useful reaction-chain view: 
        CH2=CH2  --Br2/CCl4-->  BrCH2-CH2Br  --alc. KOH-->  CH2=CHBr
                                                  --NaNH2-->  HC≡CH
3(HC≡CH)  --cyclic polymerisation-->  C6H6
C6H6  --Cl2 (excess), dry AlCl3, dark, cold-->  C6Cl6
    4. Conversion of \(C\) into compound \(D\)

      According to the given textbook pathway, benzene reacts further with excess chlorine in presence of dry \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) to give:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + 3Cl_2 \rightarrow C_6Cl_6 + 6HCl}\)

      So, compound \(D\) has molecular formula \(\mathrm{C_6Cl_6}\).

    5. Find the empirical formula of \(D\)

      Empirical formula means the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms.

      For \(\mathrm{C_6Cl_6}\), divide subscripts by \(6\):

      \(\mathrm{C_6Cl_6 \div 6 = CCl}\)

      Hence, the empirical formula is \(\mathrm{CCl}\).

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(b)\ \mathrm{CCl}}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Each reagent in the sequence gives a very standard transformation in hydrocarbon chemistry.
    • \(\mathrm{Br_2/CCl_4}\) on an alkene means halogen addition.
    • \(\mathrm{alc.\ KOH}\) followed by \(\mathrm{NaNH_2}\) on a vicinal dihalide strongly suggests alkyne formation.
    • Cyclic polymerisation of ethyne is a famous conversion to benzene.
    • Once molecular formula of \(D\) is known, empirical formula is obtained by reducing subscripts to the simplest ratio.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Addition of bromine to alkene:

      \(\mathrm{RCH{=}CHR + Br_2 \rightarrow RCHBr{-}CHBrR}\)

    • Vicinal dihalide \(\rightarrow\) alkyne:

      \(\mathrm{BrCH_2{-}CH_2Br \rightarrow CH_2{=}CHBr \rightarrow HC{\equiv}CH}\)

    • Cyclic polymerisation of ethyne:

      \(\mathrm{3C_2H_2 \rightarrow C_6H_6}\)

    • Empirical formula: simplest ratio of atoms, not the actual molecular formula.
    • Example:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 \rightarrow CH}\)

      \(\mathrm{C_6Cl_6 \rightarrow CCl}\)

    Detailed theory behind each step:

    1. Alkenes undergo electrophilic addition

      Ethene contains a \(\pi\)-bond, which is electron-rich and easily attacked by halogens. Therefore bromine adds across the double bond.

    2. Successive dehydrohalogenation gives an alkyne

      When a vicinal dihalide is treated with base, one molecule of \(\mathrm{HX}\) is removed first to give an alkene. With a stronger base, a second molecule of \(\mathrm{HX}\) is removed to give an alkyne.

    3. Ethyne can trimerise to benzene

      This is a very important hydrocarbon conversion. Three linear \(\mathrm{C_2H_2}\) units combine to form the six-membered benzene ring.

    4. Condition-based identification matters

      In multi-step organic conversions, JEE often tests whether you can identify the product from the reagent and reaction type without doing a full mechanism.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See \(\mathrm{Br_2/CCl_4}\) on ethene \(\Rightarrow\) immediately think dibromo compound.
    • See \(\mathrm{alc.\ KOH}\) then \(\mathrm{NaNH_2}\) \(\Rightarrow\) think double elimination \(\Rightarrow\) alkyne.
    • See cyclic polymerisation of ethyne \(\Rightarrow\) instantly write benzene.
    • Then from textbook-supported final step, write \(\mathrm{D = C_6Cl_6}\).
    • Since the question asks empirical formula, directly reduce:

      \(\mathrm{C_6Cl_6 \rightarrow CCl}\)

    Fast mental chain:

    • \(\mathrm{C_2H_4 \rightarrow C_2H_4Br_2 \rightarrow C_2H_2 \rightarrow C_6H_6 \rightarrow C_6Cl_6}\)
    • Then:

      \(\mathrm{C_6Cl_6 \rightarrow CCl}\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Stopping at molecular formula \(\mathrm{C_6Cl_6}\) and forgetting to convert to empirical formula.
    2. Not recognizing that vicinal dihalides can give alkynes on strong elimination.
    3. Forgetting the standard result:

      \(\mathrm{3HC{\equiv}CH \rightarrow C_6H_6}\)

    4. Confusing empirical formula with structural formula.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
    • Topic: Reactions of alkenes, alkynes, and benzene
    • Subtopic: Halogen addition, dehydrohalogenation, cyclic polymerisation of ethyne, and empirical formula-based product identification

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(\mathrm{C_2H_4 \xrightarrow{Br_2/CCl_4} C_2H_4Br_2}\)

    \(\mathrm{C_2H_4Br_2 \xrightarrow[(ii)\ NaNH_2]{(i)\ alc.\ KOH} HC{\equiv}CH}\)

    \(\mathrm{3HC{\equiv}CH \xrightarrow{cyclic\ polymerisation} C_6H_6}\)

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 \xrightarrow[dark,\ cold]{Cl_2\ (excess),\ dry\ AlCl_3} C_6Cl_6}\)

    Therefore, molecular formula of \(D\) is \(\mathrm{C_6Cl_6}\).

    Hence empirical formula:

    \(\mathrm{C_6Cl_6 \div 6 = CCl}\)

    \(\boxed{(b)\ \mathrm{CCl}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Alkene + \(\mathrm{Br_2/CCl_4}\):

      vicinal dibromide

    • Vicinal dihalide + strong base:

      alkyne formation by dehydrohalogenation

    • Key memory reaction:

      \(\mathrm{3C_2H_2 \rightarrow C_6H_6}\)

    • Empirical formula:

      simplest whole-number ratio of atoms

    • Shortcut:

      \(\mathrm{C_6Cl_6 \rightarrow CCl}\)

    Answer: \((c)\ \mathrm{Aniline + H_2O}\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    Steam distillation is used to separate a water-insoluble or sparingly water-soluble organic liquid which is volatile in steam and usually has a high boiling point or may decompose on direct heating.

    1. Why \(\mathrm{Aniline + H_2O}\) is correct

      Aniline is slightly soluble in water and is steam volatile.

      So, when steam is passed, water and aniline distil together at a temperature lower than the normal boiling point of aniline.

      The distillate can then be collected and the two layers can be separated.

      The key idea is that the organic compound should be immiscible or nearly immiscible with water and should have appreciable vapour pressure at the boiling temperature.

      For such liquids:

      \(P_{\text{total}} = P^\circ_{\text{water}} + P^\circ_{\text{organic}}\)

      Boiling occurs when:

      \(P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{atm}}\)

      Since both vapour pressures add up, the mixture boils at a temperature lower than the boiling point of either pure component.

      Hence, a high-boiling liquid like aniline can be distilled with steam below its normal boiling point of about \(184^\circ\mathrm{C}\).

    2. Core theory of steam distillation

      For two immiscible liquids, each liquid exerts its own vapour pressure independently.

      Therefore, the total vapour pressure is:

      \(P_{\text{total}} = P^\circ_{\text{water}} + P^\circ_{\text{organic}}\)

      The mixture boils when this total vapour pressure becomes equal to atmospheric pressure:

      \(P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{atm}}\)

      This is why the boiling occurs earlier than expected and why steam distillation is useful for:

      • high-boiling organic compounds
      • compounds that may decompose on strong heating
      • organic compounds insoluble in water but volatile with steam

      Typical examples include:

      • aniline
      • nitrobenzene
      • essential oils
    3. Why the other options are wrong
      • (a) \(n\)-Hexane + \(n\)-Heptane

        These are miscible organic liquids.

        Such mixtures are separated by fractional distillation, not by steam distillation.

        They are both volatile and completely miscible, and their separation depends mainly on difference in boiling points.

      • (b) \(\mathrm{CHCl_3 + Aniline}\)

        This is not the standard type of pair separated by steam distillation.

        Steam distillation specifically involves separation using water/steam with a suitable organic liquid.

        The classic suitable case here is \(\mathrm{Aniline + H_2O}\), where the pair is largely immiscible and steam distillation directly applies.

      • (d) Glucose + \(\mathrm{NaCl}\)

        Both are non-volatile solids.

        Steam distillation works only for volatile substances, so this cannot be separated by this method.

    Why this method works:

    • Steam distillation works for an organic liquid that is steam volatile and insoluble or sparingly soluble in water.
    • For immiscible liquids, vapour pressures add:

      \(P_{\text{total}} = P^\circ_{\text{water}} + P^\circ_{\text{organic}}\)

    • So the mixture boils when the sum becomes equal to atmospheric pressure:

      \(P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{atm}}\)

    • Therefore, distillation occurs at a temperature lower than the normal boiling point of the organic liquid.

    Important JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Steam distillation is commonly used for purification of organic compounds.
    • It is especially useful for compounds that are:
      • immiscible with water
      • high boiling
      • steam volatile
      • liable to decompose on direct heating
    • The real reason is not merely different boiling points.
    • Many mixtures have different boiling points, but they are not all separated by steam distillation.
    • The correct theory is that the organic liquid and water are largely immiscible, and their vapour pressures add.

    Textbook theory integrated properly:

    • The textbook points toward vapour formation and separation.
    • A more accurate statement is:

      \(\mathrm{Aniline}\) is steam volatile and nearly immiscible with \(\mathrm{H_2O}\), so both contribute separately to vapour pressure.

    • Thus the mixture boils and distils together below the boiling point of pure aniline.

    Diagram for intuition:

    • Water layer      - gives its own vapour pressure
Organic layer    - gives its own vapour pressure

So,
P(total) = P(water) + P(organic)

When this sum reaches atmospheric pressure,
the mixture boils earlier.


    1-minute solving trick:

    • First ask: Is the substance volatile?
    • If it is a non-volatile solid, reject immediately.

      So \(\mathrm{Glucose + NaCl}\) is rejected.

    • Next ask: Are the two liquids just ordinary miscible liquids?
    • If yes, think of fractional distillation instead.

      So \(n\)-hexane + \(n\)-heptane is rejected.

    • Then ask: Is it a water + organic liquid pair where the organic compound is sparingly soluble and steam volatile?
    • If yes, choose steam distillation.

      So \(\mathrm{Aniline + H_2O}\) is the correct option.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking that steam distillation depends only on difference in boiling points.
    2. Forgetting that the liquids should be immiscible or nearly immiscible.
    3. Applying steam distillation to non-volatile solids.
    4. Confusing fractional distillation with steam distillation.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry / Purification of Organic Compounds
    • Topic: Methods of purification
    • Subtopic: Steam distillation

    Final exam-style answer:

    Steam distillation is used for organic liquids that are steam volatile and insoluble or sparingly soluble in water.

    In the pair \(\mathrm{Aniline + H_2O}\), aniline is slightly soluble in water and distils with steam.

    For immiscible liquids:

    \(P_{\text{total}} = P^\circ_{\text{water}} + P^\circ_{\text{organic}}\)

    When this sum becomes equal to atmospheric pressure, the mixture boils.

    Therefore, \(\mathrm{Aniline + H_2O}\) can be separated by steam distillation.

    \(\boxed{(c)\ \mathrm{Aniline + H_2O}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Used for: steam-volatile, water-insoluble organic liquids
    • Main condition:

      liquids should be immiscible or nearly immiscible

    • Key relation:

      \(P_{\text{total}} = P^\circ_{\text{water}} + P^\circ_{\text{organic}}\)

    • Boiling condition:

      \(P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{atm}}\)

    • Best use: high-boiling organic compounds that may decompose on heating
    • Do not use for: miscible liquid pairs like \(n\)-hexane + \(n\)-heptane
    • Do not use for: non-volatile solids like glucose and \(\mathrm{NaCl}\)
    • Memory line:

      Steam distillation = water + steam-volatile, water-insoluble organic liquid

    Answer: \((b)\ \mathrm{2\text{-}ethyl\text{-}5\text{-}methylhept\text{-}1\text{-}en\text{-}6\text{-}yn\text{-}4\text{-}ol}\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    This is an IUPAC nomenclature question involving an alcohol group, one double bond, one triple bond, and alkyl substituents.

    The correct name is:

    \(\boxed{\mathrm{2\text{-}ethyl\text{-}5\text{-}methylhept\text{-}1\text{-}en\text{-}6\text{-}yn\text{-}4\text{-}ol}}\)

    1. Identify the principal functional group

      The compound contains \(\mathrm{-OH}\), so the principal functional group is alcohol.

      Therefore, the parent chain must contain the carbon bearing the \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group.

      In numbering priority, the principal functional group gets preference over double bond, triple bond, and substituents.

    2. Choose the correct parent chain

      The correct parent chain must:

      • contain the \(\mathrm{-OH}\)-bearing carbon,
      • contain the maximum number of multiple bonds,
      • and then be chosen appropriately according to IUPAC rules.

      The correct parent chain here has 7 carbons.

      So the parent hydrocarbon is:

      \(\mathrm{hept\text{-}\cdots}\)

    4. Number the parent chain correctly

      Now number the chain so that the principal functional group \(\mathrm{-OH}\) gets the lowest possible locant.

      On correct numbering:

      • \(\mathrm{-OH}\) is at carbon \(4\)
      • double bond is at carbon \(1\)
      • triple bond is at carbon \(6\)

      Therefore the parent name becomes:

      \(\mathrm{hept\text{-}1\text{-}en\text{-}6\text{-}yn\text{-}4\text{-}ol}\)

    5. Why \(\mathrm{en}\) gets lower locant than \(\mathrm{yn}\)

      When both a double bond and a triple bond are present, and the principal functional group locant is the same from both directions, the double bond gets the lower locant.

      So in the tie between:

      • \(\mathrm{hept\text{-}1\text{-}en\text{-}6\text{-}yn\text{-}4\text{-}ol}\)
      • \(\mathrm{hept\text{-}6\text{-}en\text{-}1\text{-}yn\text{-}4\text{-}ol}\)

      the correct one is:

      \(\mathrm{hept\text{-}1\text{-}en\text{-}6\text{-}yn\text{-}4\text{-}ol}\)

      So between bond types for numbering preference:

      \(\mathrm{double\ bond\ >\ triple\ bond}\)

    6. Identify the substituents

      The remaining side chains are:

      • an ethyl group at carbon \(2\)
      • a methyl group at carbon \(5\)

      Therefore, the substituent part becomes:

      \(\mathrm{2\text{-}ethyl\text{-}5\text{-}methyl}\)

    7. Write the full IUPAC name

      Combining substituents and parent chain:

      \(\mathrm{2\text{-}ethyl\text{-}5\text{-}methylhept\text{-}1\text{-}en\text{-}6\text{-}yn\text{-}4\text{-}ol}\)

      Hence, the correct option is:

      \((b)\)

    Answer: \((d)\ 4\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    We need the number of 1-alkyne isomers for the molecular formula:

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\)

    1. Understand what “1-alkyne” means

      A 1-alkyne has the triple bond at the first carbon, so its general form is:

      \(\mathrm{RC \equiv CH}\)

      That means the alkyne is terminal.

      For \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\), we can write:

      \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C - R}\)

      The fragment \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C}\) already contains \(2\) carbons, so the remaining group \(R\) must contain:

      \(6 - 2 = 4\) carbons

    2. Reduce the question to counting alkyl groups

      So the problem becomes:

      How many different \(\mathrm{C_4H_9}\) groups can be attached to \(\mathrm{-C \equiv CH}\)?

      There are \(4\) different butyl groups, so there are \(4\) different 1-alkyne isomers.

    3. Write all possible isomers
      • 1-hexyne

        \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3}\)

      • 3-methyl-1-pentyne

        \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-CH(CH_3)-CH_2-CH_3}\)

      • 4-methyl-1-pentyne

        \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-CH_2-CH(CH_3)-CH_3}\)

      • 3,3-dimethyl-1-butyne

        \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-C(CH_3)_2-CH_3}\)

      Therefore, the number of 1-alkyne isomers is:

      \(\boxed{4}\)

    Why this method works:

    • An open-chain alkyne has general formula:

      \(\mathrm{C}_{n}\mathrm{H}_{2n-2}\)

    • For \(n = 6\):

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\)

    • A 1-alkyne must have terminal triple bond:

      \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-R}\)

    • Since \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C}\) contributes \(2\) carbons, the remaining part must be a \(4\)-carbon alkyl group.
    • So counting 1-alkyne isomers becomes the same as counting distinct \(\mathrm{C_4H_9}\) groups.
    • Number of distinct \(\mathrm{C_4H_9}\) groups \(= 4\), so number of 1-alkyne isomers \(= 4\).

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • General formula of open-chain alkynes:

      \(\mathrm{C}_{n}\mathrm{H}_{2n-2}\)

    • A terminal alkyne has the form:

      \(\mathrm{RC \equiv CH}\)

    • In isomer-counting questions, fixing the functional group first makes the counting much easier.
    • Here the triple bond position is fixed at carbon-1, so only the alkyl group attached to it can vary.
    • This is a standard structural isomerism question from hydrocarbons.

    Useful diagram for intuition:

    • 1-alkyne of C6H10:

HC≡C — R

2 carbons are fixed in HC≡C
So R must be a 4-carbon alkyl group

Different C4H9 groups = 4
Therefore different 1-alkynes = 4


    1-minute solving trick:

    • The moment you see 1-alkyne, write:

      \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-R}\)

    • Then subtract \(2\) carbons from the total carbon count.
    • For \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\):

      \(6 - 2 = 4\)

    • Now just ask: How many different \(\mathrm{C_4H_9}\) groups are possible?
    • Answer:

      \(4\)

    • So directly:

      \(\boxed{(d)\ 4}\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Counting all alkyne isomers instead of only 1-alkyne isomers.
    2. Including internal alkynes like 2-hexyne or 3-hexyne.
    3. Missing one branched butyl group.
    4. Counting the same structure twice by writing it from opposite ends.
    5. Getting confused by the phrase “excluding stereoisomers”; here only structural isomers matter.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
    • Topic: Alkynes
    • Subtopic: Structural isomerism of terminal alkynes / counting isomers

    Final exam-style answer:

    A 1-alkyne has the form \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-R}\).

    For \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\), the group \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C}\) contains \(2\) carbons, so \(R\) must contain \(4\) carbons.

    Thus, the number of 1-alkyne isomers is equal to the number of distinct \(\mathrm{C_4H_9}\) groups.

    There are \(4\) such groups, giving the following isomers:

    • \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3}\)
    • \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-CH(CH_3)-CH_2-CH_3}\)
    • \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-CH_2-CH(CH_3)-CH_3}\)
    • \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-C(CH_3)_2-CH_3}\)

    Hence, the number of 1-alkyne isomers is:

    \(\boxed{(d)\ 4}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • General formula of alkyne:

      \(\mathrm{C}_{n}\mathrm{H}_{2n-2}\)

    • 1-alkyne form:

      \(\mathrm{HC \equiv C-R}\)

    • Fixed carbons in terminal alkyne unit:

      \(2\)

    • For \(\mathrm{C_6H_{10}}\):

      \(R = \mathrm{C_4H_9}\)

    • Different \(\mathrm{C_4H_9}\) groups:

      \(4\)

    • Answer:

      \(\boxed{4}\)

    • Memory line:

      Count the alkyl groups attached to \(\mathrm{-C \equiv CH}\).

    Answer: \((b)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen TTR
    Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    The reaction sequence has three standard named/conceptual steps. We identify each intermediate one by one.

    1. Step 1: Diazonium salt to chlorobenzene

      The starting compound is benzenediazonium chloride, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5N_2^+Cl^-}\).

      With \(\mathrm{Cu_2Cl_2/HCl}\), the diazonium group is replaced by chlorine.

      This is the Sandmeyer reaction.

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5N_2^+Cl^- \xrightarrow[HCl]{Cu_2Cl_2} C_6H_5Cl + N_2}\)

      Therefore, \(X =\) chlorobenzene.

    2. Step 2: Chlorobenzene to toluene

      Now chlorobenzene reacts with \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl}\) in presence of \(\mathrm{Na/dry\ ether}\).

      This is the Wurtz-Fittig reaction, in which an aryl halide and an alkyl halide couple together.

      General form:

      \(\mathrm{ArX + RX + 2Na \rightarrow ArR + 2NaX}\)

      So here:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5Cl + CH_3Cl + 2Na \rightarrow C_6H_5CH_3 + 2NaCl}\)

      Thus, \(Y =\) toluene.

    3. Step 3: Chlorination of toluene in dark with \(\mathrm{Cl_2/Fe}\)

      The reagent \(\mathrm{Cl_2/Fe}\) in dark indicates electrophilic aromatic substitution on the benzene ring, not free-radical side-chain chlorination.

      The \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) group is an activating, ortho-para directing group.

      Therefore chlorination occurs mainly at the ortho and para positions, with the para product preferred here.

      So:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_3 \xrightarrow[\text{dark}]{Cl_2/Fe} p\text{-}chlorotoluene}\)

      Hence \(Z =\) \(p\)-chlorotoluene.

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(b)\ \text{\(p\)-chlorotoluene}}\)

    Complete reaction sequence:

    • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5N_2^+Cl^- \xrightarrow[HCl]{Cu_2Cl_2} C_6H_5Cl + N_2}\)
    • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5Cl \xrightarrow[\text{dry ether}]{CH_3Cl/2Na} C_6H_5CH_3}\)
    • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_3 \xrightarrow[\text{dark}]{Cl_2/Fe} p\text{-}chlorotoluene}\)

    Reaction map:

    Why the last step gives ring chlorination and not side-chain chlorination:

    • \(\mathrm{Cl_2/Fe}\) or \(\mathrm{Cl_2/FeCl_3}\) is the condition for electrophilic substitution on the aromatic ring.
    • Side-chain chlorination of toluene occurs under free-radical conditions, usually \(\mathrm{Cl_2/h\nu}\) or sunlight.
    • Since the question clearly mentions dark, photochemical radical substitution is ruled out.
    • So the chlorine must enter the ring, giving mainly ortho and para products.

    JEE / NCERT theory to remember:

    • Sandmeyer reaction:

      Aryldiazonium salts react with cuprous salts such as \(\mathrm{CuCl}\), \(\mathrm{CuBr}\), \(\mathrm{CuCN}\) to replace the diazonium group by \(\mathrm{Cl}\), \(\mathrm{Br}\), or \(\mathrm{CN}\).

      \(\mathrm{ArN_2^+X^- \rightarrow ArCl,\ ArBr,\ ArCN}\)

    • Wurtz-Fittig reaction:

      Coupling of an aryl halide and an alkyl halide using sodium in dry ether.

      \(\mathrm{ArX + RX + 2Na \rightarrow ArR + 2NaX}\)

    • Toluene as an ortho-para director:

      The methyl group donates electron density to the benzene ring by \(+I\) effect and hyperconjugation, making the ring more reactive and directing incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions.

    • Chlorination of benzene ring:

      \(\mathrm{Cl_2/Fe}\) or \(\mathrm{Cl_2/FeCl_3}\) gives electrophilic aromatic substitution.

    • Side-chain halogenation:

      \(\mathrm{Cl_2/h\nu}\) acts at the benzylic position by radical substitution.

    How to think of this question quickly:

    1. See diazonium salt + \(\mathrm{Cu_2Cl_2/HCl}\) and immediately think:

      \(\mathrm{-N_2^+Cl^- \rightarrow -Cl}\)

    2. Then see aryl halide + \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl/Na/dry\ ether}\) and recall:

      Wurtz-Fittig \(\Rightarrow\) aryl + methyl coupling \(\Rightarrow\) toluene

    3. Then see \(\mathrm{Cl_2/Fe,\ dark}\) and think:

      Ring chlorination, not side-chain chlorination

    4. Since \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) is ortho-para directing, choose the para-substituted ring product from the options.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Keyword 1: diazonium + \(\mathrm{CuCl}\) \(\Rightarrow\) chlorobenzene
    • Keyword 2: aryl halide + \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl/Na}\) \(\Rightarrow\) toluene
    • Keyword 3: \(\mathrm{Cl_2/Fe,\ dark}\) \(\Rightarrow\) ring chlorination
    • Keyword 4: \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) directs to ortho/para
    • So directly select \(p\)-chlorotoluene.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing Sandmeyer reaction with diazonium hydrolysis or fluorination.
    2. Forgetting that \(\mathrm{Na/dry\ ether}\) with aryl halide + alkyl halide is Wurtz-Fittig, not ordinary Wurtz reaction.
    3. Treating \(\mathrm{Cl_2}\) as side-chain chlorination without noticing the condition \(\mathrm{Fe}\), dark.
    4. Choosing benzyl chloride or benzal chloride type products, which would arise in radical side-chain substitution, not here.
    5. Missing the directive effect of \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\).

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Amines
    • Topic: Diazonium salts and their reactions
    • Subtopic: Sandmeyer reaction

    Linked revision topics:

    • Chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
    • Topic: Coupling reactions of halo compounds
    • Subtopic: Wurtz-Fittig reaction
    • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
    • Topic: Electrophilic substitution in alkyl benzenes
    • Subtopic: Orientation in toluene chlorination

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_5N_2^+Cl^-}\) with \(\mathrm{Cu_2Cl_2/HCl}\) gives chlorobenzene by Sandmeyer reaction. Chlorobenzene on treatment with \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl/Na/dry\ ether}\) undergoes Wurtz-Fittig reaction to form toluene. Toluene on chlorination with \(\mathrm{Cl_2/Fe}\) in dark undergoes electrophilic substitution on the ring. Since \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) is ortho-para directing, the product \(Z\) is \(p\)-chlorotoluene.

    \(\boxed{(b)}\)

    Answer: \((c)\) Ethanal, propanone


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    We first identify the starting carbonyl compound and then follow the reaction sequence step by step.

    1. Identify the starting ketone

      The given ketone is \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2CH_3}\), that is butan-2-one.

    2. Action of Grignard reagent \(\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}\)

      Grignard reagents add an alkyl group to the carbonyl carbon.

      So, \(\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}\) adds a \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) group to butan-2-one, and after hydrolysis \(\mathrm{(H_3O^+)}\), a tertiary alcohol is formed.

      Thus, \(A\) is:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2C(OH)(CH_3)CH_3}\)

      This is a tertiary alcohol.

    3. Dehydration of the alcohol

      On heating with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(358\ \mathrm{K}\), the alcohol undergoes elimination to form an alkene.

      By Saytzeff rule, the more substituted alkene is the major product.

      Therefore, \(B\) is:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

      This is the major alkene formed.

    4. Ozonolysis of the alkene

      Ozonolysis with \(\mathrm{O_3}\), followed by reductive workup \(\mathrm{Zn/H_2O}\), cleaves the double bond and converts each doubly bonded carbon into a carbonyl compound.

      For:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

      the cleavage gives:

      • Left carbon \(\mathrm{(CH_3CH=)}\) has one H, so it gives an aldehyde:

        \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) = ethanal

      • Right carbon \(\mathrm{(=C(CH_3)_2)}\) has two \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) groups, so it gives a ketone:

        \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_2CO}\) = propanone

      Hence:

      \(C = \mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) and \(D = \mathrm{(CH_3)_2CO}\)

    Reaction flow:



    Compact reaction summary:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow[\ (ii)\ H_3O^+]{\ (i)\ CH_3MgBr\ } CH_3CH_2C(OH)(CH_3)CH_3}\)

    \(\mathrm{\xrightarrow[\ 358\,K]{20\%\ H_3PO_4} CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

    \(\mathrm{\xrightarrow[\ Zn/H_2O]{O_3} CH_3CHO + (CH_3)_2CO}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Grignard reagent \(\mathrm{RMgX}\) behaves like \(\mathrm{R^-}\) and attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon.
    • Ketone \(+\) Grignard reagent \(\rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol after hydrolysis.
    • Acidic dehydration of alcohol usually gives the more substituted alkene as the major product.
    • Ozonolysis breaks the \(\mathrm{C=C}\) bond into carbonyl fragments.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Grignard reagent: \(\mathrm{RMgX}\) adds to aldehydes and ketones.
    • With ketone:

      \(\mathrm{R_2CO + R'MgX \xrightarrow{ether} R_2C(OMgX)R' \xrightarrow{H_3O^+} R_2C(OH)R'}\)

    • Alcohol dehydration: generally gives alkene; major product follows Saytzeff rule.
    • Ozonolysis: cleaves \(\mathrm{C=C}\) bond.
    • Carbon of double bond having at least one H usually gives an aldehyde on reductive ozonolysis.
    • Carbon of double bond attached to two carbon groups gives a ketone.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • First identify the starting ketone as butan-2-one: \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2CH_3}\).
    • \(\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}\) adds one \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) to the carbonyl carbon \(\Rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol.
    • Dehydration of that alcohol gives the most substituted alkene:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

    • Now split the double bond directly in ozonolysis:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=} \Rightarrow \mathrm{CH_3CHO}\)

      \(\mathrm{=C(CH_3)_2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{(CH_3)_2CO}\)

    • So the answer is immediately:

      \((c)\) Ethanal, propanone

    Very fast carbonyl-fragment trick:

    • In ozonolysis, mentally cut the double bond:
    • CH3CH = C(CH3)2
  |         |
CHO      C=O
    • The left alkene carbon has one H \(\Rightarrow\) aldehyde.
    • The right alkene carbon has two alkyl groups \(\Rightarrow\) ketone.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Forgetting that ketone \(+\) Grignard reagent gives a tertiary alcohol, not a secondary alcohol.
    2. Missing the major alkene during dehydration.
    3. Confusing oxidative ozonolysis with reductive ozonolysis.
    4. Writing ethanoic acid instead of ethanal. Here \(\mathrm{Zn/H_2O}\) gives reductive ozonolysis, so aldehyde is obtained.
    5. Not checking whether the alkene carbon bears hydrogen before deciding aldehyde vs ketone.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
    • Topic: Reactions of carbonyl compounds and ozonolysis of alkenes
    • Subtopic: Grignard addition to ketones, dehydration of alcohols, reductive ozonolysis

    Final exam-style answer:

    The starting compound is butan-2-one, \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_2CH_3}\).

    On reaction with \(\mathrm{CH_3MgBr}\) followed by \(\mathrm{H_3O^+}\), it forms the tertiary alcohol:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2C(OH)(CH_3)CH_3}\)

    On dehydration with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(358\ \mathrm{K}\), the major alkene formed is:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2}\)

    Reductive ozonolysis of this alkene gives:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3CHO}\) and \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_2CO}\)

    Therefore, \(C =\) ethanal and \(D =\) propanone.

    \(\boxed{(c)\ \text{Ethanal, propanone}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Ketone \(+\) Grignard:

      \(\mathrm{R_2CO \rightarrow R_3COH}\)

    • Dehydration of alcohol:

      more substituted alkene is major

    • Reductive ozonolysis:

      \(\mathrm{C=C \rightarrow}\) aldehydes / ketones

    • Alkene carbon with H:

      gives aldehyde

    • Alkene carbon with two alkyl groups:

      gives ketone

    • Here:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=C(CH_3)_2 \rightarrow CH_3CHO + (CH_3)_2CO}\)

    Answer: \((d)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    1. We must find the incorrect reagent match

      The question asks which reagent cannot distinguish the given pair of compounds.

    2. Check option (a)

      \(CH_3OH\) and \(CH_3CH_2OH\) with \(I_2 + NaOH\)

      This is the iodoform test. Ethanol gives iodoform test, but methanol does not.

      • \(CH_3CH_2OH \rightarrow CH_3CHO\) on oxidation
      • Compounds containing or forming the group \(CH_3CO-\) or \(CH_3CH(OH)-\) give iodoform test
      • So this reagent can distinguish them
    3. Check option (b)

      \(CH_3CH_2OH\) and \((CH_3)_3COH\) with anhydrous \(ZnCl_2 +\) conc. \(HCl\)

      This is Lucas reagent, used to distinguish primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols.

      • Primary alcohol: very slow / no turbidity at room temperature
      • Tertiary alcohol: immediate turbidity
      • Therefore ethanol and tert-butyl alcohol are easily distinguished
    4. Check option (c)

      \(CH_3-C{\equiv}CH\) and \(CH_3-C{\equiv}C-CH_3\) with \(Na\)

      \(CH_3-C{\equiv}CH\) is a terminal alkyne. It has an acidic hydrogen attached to the triple-bonded carbon.

      \(CH_3-C{\equiv}C-CH_3\) is an internal alkyne. It does not have such acidic hydrogen.

      • Terminal alkynes react with active metals like \(Na\) due to acidic hydrogen
      • Internal alkynes do not show this behavior
      • So this reagent can distinguish them
    5. Check option (d)

      \(CH_3CHO\) and \((CH_3)_2CO\) with \(2,4\)-dinitrophenylhydrazine

      \(2,4\)-DNP reagent tests for the presence of the carbonyl group, \(C{=}O\).

      • Aldehydes give the test
      • Ketones also give the test
      • Since both \(CH_3CHO\) and \((CH_3)_2CO\) contain carbonyl group, both respond positively
      • Hence this reagent cannot distinguish these two compounds

      Therefore, this is the incorrect match.

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(d)}\)

    Core theory to remember:

    • Iodoform test: Given by compounds containing \(CH_3CO-\) or \(CH_3CH(OH)-\), and also by ethanol because it gets oxidized to ethanal.
    • Lucas reagent: anhydrous \(ZnCl_2 +\) conc. \(HCl\); used to distinguish alcohol classes by rate of turbidity formation.
    • Terminal alkynes: contain acidic hydrogen and react with strong metals / suitable reagents, unlike internal alkynes.
    • \(2,4\)-DNP test: confirms presence of aldehyde or ketone carbonyl group, but does not distinguish aldehyde from ketone.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Questions on qualitative organic analysis are often solved by remembering what functional group the reagent detects.
    • If a reagent gives positive test for both compounds, it cannot be used for distinction.
    • \(2,4\)-DNP is a test for carbonyl compounds in general, not specifically aldehydes only.
    • To distinguish aldehydes and ketones, reagents like Tollens' reagent or Fehling's solution are usually more useful.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Immediately spot option (d): aldehyde + ketone with \(2,4\)-DNP.
    • Ask: “Does \(2,4\)-DNP detect only one of them?” No, it detects both.
    • So without checking the full details, you can often mark \(\boxed{(d)}\) quickly.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking \(2,4\)-DNP distinguishes aldehydes and ketones. It does not; it only confirms carbonyl presence.
    2. Forgetting that ethanol gives iodoform test.
    3. Forgetting that terminal alkynes have acidic hydrogen but internal alkynes do not.
    4. Mixing up Lucas reagent behavior of primary and tertiary alcohols.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
    • Topic: Qualitative analysis of organic compounds
    • Subtopic: Distinguishing functional groups using named reagents and characteristic tests

    Quick reagent cheatsheet:

    • Iodoform test:

      \(CH_3CO-\), \(CH_3CH(OH)-\), ethanol

    • Lucas reagent:

      distinguishes \(1^\circ\), \(2^\circ\), \(3^\circ\) alcohols

    • Terminal alkyne test idea:

      presence of acidic hydrogen on \(sp\)-hybridized carbon

    • \(2,4\)-DNP:

      aldehydes + ketones \(\rightarrow\) positive test

    • Tollens' / Fehling's:

      generally useful to distinguish aldehydes from ketones

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(2,4\)-dinitrophenylhydrazine reacts with the carbonyl group \((C{=}O)\), which is present in both \(CH_3CHO\) and \((CH_3)_2CO\). Therefore, it cannot distinguish the given pair of compounds.

    Hence, the incorrect match is \(\boxed{(d)}\).

    Answer: \((a)\ D > C > A > B\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    We have to arrange the given alkanes in decreasing order of boiling points.

    • \(A =\) 2-methylbutane
    • \(B =\) 2,2-dimethylpropane
    • \(C =\) Pentane
    • \(D =\) Hexane

    For alkanes, boiling point mainly depends on:

    • Molecular mass / size
    • Extent of branching
    1. Compare molecular mass first

      Hexane \(\mathrm{(C_6H_{14})}\) has higher molar mass than the other three compounds, which are isomers of pentane \(\mathrm{(C_5H_{12})}\).

      Greater molar mass means stronger London dispersion forces, so \(\mathrm{hexane}\) has the highest boiling point.

      Therefore:

      \(D > A, B, C\)

    2. Now compare the \(\mathrm{C_5H_{12}}\) isomers

      Among isomeric alkanes, more branching means lower boiling point.

      Reason: branching makes the molecule more compact, decreases surface area, and reduces intermolecular van der Waals attraction.

      • \(C =\) Pentane \( \rightarrow \) straight chain
      • \(A =\) 2-methylbutane \( \rightarrow \) one branch
      • \(B =\) 2,2-dimethylpropane \( \rightarrow \) maximum branching

      So for these three:

      \(C > A > B\)

    3. Combine both results

      Putting everything together:

      \(D > C > A > B\)

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(a)\ D > C > A > B}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Boiling point depends on how strongly molecules attract each other in the liquid state.
    • In alkanes, the relevant intermolecular force is mainly London dispersion force.
    • Bigger molecules have more electrons and larger temporary induced dipoles, so dispersion forces become stronger.
    • For isomers with the same formula, the less branched molecule has greater surface contact, so intermolecular attraction is stronger and boiling point is higher.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • In a homologous series of alkanes, boiling point generally increases with molecular mass.
    • Among isomeric alkanes, boiling point generally decreases with branching.
    • Straight-chain alkanes usually have higher boiling points than their branched isomers.
    • Highly symmetrical and compact molecules have lower surface area and hence lower boiling points.
    • This is a standard application of physical properties of hydrocarbons.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • First separate by carbon number:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}} > \mathrm{C_5H_{12}}\) in boiling point

    • So hexane \((D)\) must be highest.
    • Then among pentane isomers, use:

      straight chain \(>\) one branch \(>\) more branching

    • Hence:

      \(\mathrm{Pentane > 2\!-\!methylbutane > 2,2\!-\!dimethylpropane}\)

    • Final order:

      \(D > C > A > B\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking more branching increases boiling point. It actually decreases boiling point for alkane isomers.
    2. Forgetting to compare molecular mass before comparing branching.
    3. Confusing boiling point with stability. A more branched alkane may be more stable, but that does not mean it has a higher boiling point.
    4. Mixing up melting point and boiling point; symmetry can affect melting point differently.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
    • Topic: Physical properties of alkanes
    • Subtopic: Effect of molecular mass and branching on boiling point

    Final exam-style answer:

    Hexane has higher molar mass than the other three compounds, so it has the highest boiling point. Among the isomers of pentane, boiling point decreases with increase in branching. Therefore:

    \(\mathrm{Pentane > 2\!-\!methylbutane > 2,2\!-\!dimethylpropane}\)

    Hence, the overall decreasing order of boiling points is:

    \(\boxed{D > C > A > B}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Higher molar mass \(\Rightarrow\) higher boiling point
    • More branching \(\Rightarrow\) lower boiling point
    • Straight chain \(\Rightarrow\) maximum surface area
    • Compact branched molecule \(\Rightarrow\) weaker dispersion force
    • For pentane isomers:

      \(n\text{-pentane} > \text{isopentane} > \text{neopentane}\)

    Answer: \((a)\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    To decide whether a species is aromatic, we check the standard aromaticity conditions.

    1. Conditions for aromaticity
      • The species must be cyclic.
      • It must be planar.
      • It must have a continuous ring of overlapping \(p\)-orbitals.
      • It must contain \(\boldsymbol{(4n+2)}\) \(\pi\)-electrons \((n = 0,1,2,\dots)\). This is Hückel’s rule.
    2. Check option \((b)\)

      This is the cyclopentadienyl anion.

      It is cyclic and planar, and all five carbon atoms participate in a continuous conjugated system.

      Electron count:

      Two double bonds contribute \(4\ \pi\)-electrons, and the negative charge contributes \(2\ \pi\)-electrons.

      Total \(= 6\ \pi\)-electrons.

      Since \(6 = 4n+2\) for \(n=1\), this species is aromatic.

    3. Check option \((c)\)

      This is the cycloheptatrienyl cation \((\text{tropylium ion})\).

      It is cyclic, planar and fully conjugated.

      Electron count:

      Three double bonds contribute \(6\ \pi\)-electrons.

      The positively charged carbon has an empty \(p\)-orbital and becomes part of the conjugated ring.

      Thus it has \(6\ \pi\)-electrons and obeys Hückel’s rule, so it is aromatic.

    4. Check option \((d)\)

      This is naphthalene.

      Naphthalene is a fused planar cyclic system with continuous conjugation.

      It contains \(10\ \pi\)-electrons.

      Since \(10 = 4n+2\) for \(n=2\), naphthalene is aromatic.

    5. Check option \((a)\)

      This is the cyclohexadienyl cation.

      Although it is cyclic, it does not have a continuous cyclic conjugated system over all six carbons.

      One carbon is not participating properly in the uninterrupted \(\pi\)-delocalisation required for aromaticity.

      So it fails the basic condition of continuous overlap of \(p\)-orbitals.

      Therefore, it is not aromatic.

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(a)}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Aromaticity is not decided only by electron count.
    • First check whether the ring is fully conjugated.
    • Only after that should you apply the \((4n+2)\) rule.
    • A species that is cyclic but not fully conjugated is simply non-aromatic.
    • A species that is cyclic, planar, fully conjugated, but has \(4n\ \pi\)-electrons is anti-aromatic.
    • This distinction is very important in exams.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Aromatic: cyclic, planar, fully conjugated, \((4n+2)\ \pi\)-electrons.
    • Anti-aromatic: cyclic, planar, fully conjugated, \(4n\ \pi\)-electrons.
    • Non-aromatic: if cyclicity, planarity, or continuous conjugation is absent.
    • Cyclopentadienyl anion is a classic \(6\pi\)-electron aromatic species.
    • Tropylium ion is another classic \(6\pi\)-electron aromatic cation.
    • Naphthalene is aromatic with \(10\pi\)-electrons.

    Quick electron-count summary:

    • Option \((b)\): \(4 + 2 = 6\ \pi\)-electrons \(\Rightarrow\) aromatic
    • Option \((c)\): \(6\ \pi\)-electrons \(\Rightarrow\) aromatic
    • Option \((d)\): \(10\ \pi\)-electrons \(\Rightarrow\) aromatic
    • Option \((a)\): conjugation is interrupted \(\Rightarrow\) not aromatic

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Memorise the three famous aromatic species:
      • cyclopentadienyl anion
      • tropylium cation
      • naphthalene
    • If these three are present together in options, the odd one out is usually the non-fully-conjugated ring.
    • In this question, \((b)\), \((c)\) and \((d)\) are standard aromatic species, so \((a)\) must be the non-aromatic one.

    Intuitive way to think about it:

    • Aromaticity needs a complete circular electron highway.
    • In option \((a)\), that highway is broken, so electrons cannot delocalise around the entire ring.
    • In \((b)\), \((c)\) and \((d)\), the highway is continuous.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Applying Hückel’s rule without first checking full conjugation.
    2. Confusing non-aromatic with anti-aromatic.
    3. Forgetting that a lone pair on a negatively charged carbon can contribute \(2\ \pi\)-electrons if it occupies a \(p\)-orbital.
    4. Forgetting that a positively charged carbon can still participate in conjugation through an empty \(p\)-orbital.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – General Organic Chemistry
    • Topic: Aromaticity
    • Subtopic: Hückel’s rule and aromatic / anti-aromatic / non-aromatic species

    Final exam-style answer:

    For aromaticity, the species must be cyclic, planar, fully conjugated and must contain \((4n+2)\) \(\pi\)-electrons.

    Cyclopentadienyl anion, tropylium cation and naphthalene satisfy these conditions and are aromatic.

    The cyclohexadienyl cation does not possess continuous cyclic conjugation, so it is not aromatic.

    \(\boxed{(a)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • \(6\pi\) electrons \(\Rightarrow\) often aromatic
    • \(10\pi\) electrons \(\Rightarrow\) often aromatic if fully conjugated
    • Fully conjugated ring required first
    • cyclopentadienyl anion \(\Rightarrow\) aromatic
    • tropylium cation \(\Rightarrow\) aromatic
    • naphthalene \(\Rightarrow\) aromatic
    • cyclohexadienyl cation \(\Rightarrow\) non-aromatic

    Answer: \((b)\ \mathrm{C_6H_5NH_2}\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    In Kjeldahl’s method, the nitrogen present in the organic compound is converted into \(\mathrm{NH_3}\). That ammonia is absorbed by a known amount of acid, and the leftover acid is found by back titration.

    1. Find total moles of \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) taken initially

      Volume of \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4} = 60\ \mathrm{mL} = 0.060\ \mathrm{L}\)

      Molarity \(= 0.1\ \mathrm{M}\)

      Therefore:

      \(\text{moles of } \mathrm{H_2SO_4} = 0.060 \times 0.1 = 0.006\ \mathrm{mol}\)

    2. Find unreacted \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) from NaOH back titration

      Volume of \(\mathrm{NaOH} = 20\ \mathrm{mL} = 0.020\ \mathrm{L}\)

      Molarity of \(\mathrm{NaOH} = 0.1\ \mathrm{M}\)

      So:

      \(\text{moles of } \mathrm{NaOH} = 0.020 \times 0.1 = 0.002\ \mathrm{mol}\)

      Neutralisation relation:

      \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4 + 2NaOH \rightarrow Na_2SO_4 + 2H_2O}\)

      Thus unreacted \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\):

      \(\text{moles of unreacted } \mathrm{H_2SO_4} = \frac{0.002}{2} = 0.001\ \mathrm{mol}\)

    3. Find moles of \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) that reacted with ammonia

      \(\text{reacted } \mathrm{H_2SO_4} = 0.006 - 0.001 = 0.005\ \mathrm{mol}\)

    4. Find moles of \(\mathrm{NH_3}\) evolved

      Reaction between ammonia and sulphuric acid:

      \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4 + 2NH_3 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4}\)

      So \(1\) mole of \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) reacts with \(2\) moles of \(\mathrm{NH_3}\).

      Therefore:

      \(\text{moles of } \mathrm{NH_3} = 2 \times 0.005 = 0.010\ \mathrm{mol}\)

    5. Find mass of nitrogen in the sample

      Each mole of \(\mathrm{NH_3}\) contains \(1\) mole of nitrogen.

      So moles of nitrogen \(= 0.010\ \mathrm{mol}\)

      Mass of nitrogen:

      \(m_N = 0.010 \times 14.01 \approx 0.1401\ \mathrm{g}\)

    6. Find percentage of nitrogen in compound \(X\)

      Mass of sample \(= 0.933\ \mathrm{g}\)

      \[ \%N = \frac{0.1401}{0.933}\times 100 \approx 15.0\% \]

      Using inline format:

      \(\%N = \dfrac{0.1401}{0.933}\times 100 \approx 15.0\%\)

    7. Match with the options
      • \((a)\ \mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2NH_2}\) Molar mass \(= 107\) \(\%N = \dfrac{14}{107}\times 100 \approx 13.1\%\)
      • \((b)\ \mathrm{C_6H_5NH_2}\) Molar mass \(= 93\) \(\%N = \dfrac{14}{93}\times 100 \approx 15.05\%\)
      • \((c)\ \mathrm{CH_3CH_2NH_2}\) Molar mass \(= 45\) \(\%N = \dfrac{14}{45}\times 100 \approx 31.1\%\)
      • \((d)\ \mathrm{CH_3CONH_2}\) Molar mass \(= 59\) \(\%N = \dfrac{14}{59}\times 100 \approx 23.7\%\)

      The calculated value \(\approx 15.0\%\) matches option \((b)\).

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(b)\ \mathrm{C_6H_5NH_2}}\)

    Why this method works:

    • In Kjeldahl’s method, nitrogen in the compound is ultimately measured through the amount of \(\mathrm{NH_3}\) produced.
    • The \(\mathrm{NH_3}\) is trapped in a known excess of acid.
    • The acid left unused is found by back titration with base.
    • So:

      acid initially taken \(-\) acid left unreacted \(=\) acid used by ammonia

    • From that, moles of \(\mathrm{NH_3}\), then mass of nitrogen, and finally percentage nitrogen are obtained.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Kjeldahl’s method is used to estimate nitrogen in many organic compounds.
    • Nitrogen is converted into ammonium sulphate during digestion, then on treatment with alkali, ammonia is liberated.
    • The liberated ammonia is absorbed in a known excess of standard acid.
    • The remaining acid is estimated by back titration with standard base.
    • Kjeldahl’s method is not applicable to nitrogen present in nitro compounds, azo compounds, diazonium salts, ring nitrogen and some other special cases where nitrogen is not converted into ammonium sulphate under the usual conditions.

    Main reaction pattern to remember:

    • Back titration:

      \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4 + 2NaOH \rightarrow Na_2SO_4 + 2H_2O}\)

    • Ammonia absorption:

      \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4 + 2NH_3 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4}\)

    1-minute solving trick:

    • First calculate moles of acid taken:

      \(0.060\times 0.1 = 0.006\)

    • Then from NaOH, find leftover acid:

      \(0.020\times 0.1 = 0.002\ \mathrm{mol\ NaOH}\)

      \(\Rightarrow \mathrm{H_2SO_4} = 0.001\ \mathrm{mol}\)

    • Acid used by ammonia:

      \(0.006 - 0.001 = 0.005\ \mathrm{mol}\)

    • Since \(1\ \mathrm{H_2SO_4} : 2\ \mathrm{NH_3}\),

      \(\mathrm{NH_3} = 0.010\ \mathrm{mol}\)

    • So nitrogen mass:

      \(0.010\times 14 = 0.14\ \mathrm{g}\)

    • Then:

      \(\%N \approx \dfrac{0.14}{0.933}\times 100 \approx 15\%\)

    • Now compare quickly with options. Only aniline fits.

    Intuitive shortcut:

    • Once you get \(\%N \approx 15\%\), the compound must have moderate molar mass with one nitrogen atom.
    • For one nitrogen atom:

      \(\%N \approx \dfrac{14}{M}\times 100\)

    • So if \(\%N \approx 15\%\), then:

      \(M \approx \dfrac{14\times 100}{15} \approx 93\)

    • Among the options, molar mass \(93\) corresponds to aniline, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5NH_2}\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Forgetting that \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) is dibasic, so NaOH moles must be divided by \(2\) to get moles of leftover \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\).
    2. Forgetting that \(1\) mole of \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) absorbs \(2\) moles of \(\mathrm{NH_3}\).
    3. Using total acid directly without subtracting leftover acid.
    4. Matching the option before computing percentage nitrogen properly.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – Practical Organic Analysis
    • Topic: Estimation of nitrogen
    • Subtopic: Kjeldahl’s method and back titration

    Final exam-style answer:

    Total \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) taken \(= 0.060\times 0.1 = 0.006\ \mathrm{mol}\).

    \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) used \(= 0.020\times 0.1 = 0.002\ \mathrm{mol}\).

    Since \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4 + 2NaOH}\), unreacted \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4} = 0.001\ \mathrm{mol}\).

    Hence \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) that reacted with \(\mathrm{NH_3}\):

    \(0.006 - 0.001 = 0.005\ \mathrm{mol}\)

    Since \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4 + 2NH_3}\), moles of \(\mathrm{NH_3} = 0.010\ \mathrm{mol}\).

    Therefore mass of nitrogen \(= 0.010\times 14.01 = 0.1401\ \mathrm{g}\).

    \(\%N = \dfrac{0.1401}{0.933}\times 100 \approx 15.0\%\)

    This matches aniline, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5NH_2}\).

    \(\boxed{(b)\ \mathrm{C_6H_5NH_2}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Total acid:

      \(n = MV\)

    • Leftover acid from base:

      \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4} = \dfrac{\mathrm{NaOH}}{2}\)

    • Acid used by ammonia:

      \(n_{\text{initial acid}} - n_{\text{left acid}}\)

    • Ammonia relation:

      \(1\ \mathrm{H_2SO_4} : 2\ \mathrm{NH_3}\)

    • Nitrogen moles:

      \(n_N = n_{\mathrm{NH_3}}\)

    • Mass of nitrogen:

      \(m_N = n_N \times 14\)

    • Percentage nitrogen:

      \(\%N = \dfrac{m_N}{m_{\text{sample}}}\times 100\)

    Answer: \((b)\ \mathrm{CH_2=CH^+}\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    We are asked to identify the least stable carbocation.

    1. Check the nature of each carbocation
      • (a) \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH_2^+}\) is an ethyl carbocation, a primary carbocation.
      • (b) \(\mathrm{CH_2=CH^+}\) is a vinyl carbocation.
      • (c) \(\mathrm{CH_2=CH-CH_2^+}\) is an allyl carbocation.
      • (d) \(\mathrm{C_6H_5-CH_2^+}\) is a benzyl carbocation.
    2. Now compare their stabilisation
      • (a) Ethyl carbocation: Though primary carbocations are not very stable, this one still gets some stabilisation from the \(+I\) effect of the methyl group and from hyperconjugation.
      • (c) Allyl carbocation: It is resonance-stabilised. The positive charge is delocalised over two carbon atoms.
      • (d) Benzyl carbocation: It is also strongly resonance-stabilised. The positive charge is delocalised into the benzene ring.
      • (b) Vinyl carbocation: This is exceptionally unstable. Here the positive charge is on a vinylic carbon, that is, a carbon of the double bond itself.
    3. Why is the vinyl carbocation least stable?
      • In \(\mathrm{CH_2=CH^+}\), the positively charged carbon is \(sp\)-hybridised.
      • An \(sp\)-hybridised carbon has greater \(s\)-character than \(sp^2\) or \(sp^3\) carbon, so it is more electronegative.
      • A more electronegative carbon is less willing to bear a positive charge.
      • Also, the positive charge here is not effectively stabilised by resonance in the way allylic and benzylic carbocations are.
      • So the vinylic carbocation is much less stable than ordinary alkyl, allylic, and benzylic carbocations.

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{(b)\ \mathrm{CH_2=CH^+}}\)

    Key theory to remember:

    • Carbocation stability generally increases with inductive effect, hyperconjugation, and resonance.
    • Benzylic and allylic carbocations are especially stable because the positive charge is delocalised.
    • Ordinary alkyl carbocations are stabilised in the order:

      \(3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ > \mathrm{CH_3^+}\)

    • Vinylic carbocations are highly unstable because the positive charge lies on a carbon involved in a double bond.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Immediately look for allylic and benzylic carbocations. These are usually stabilised by resonance, so eliminate them first.
    • A simple primary carbocation is unstable, but a vinylic carbocation is even worse.
    • The phrase “positive charge on double-bond carbon” should instantly signal very unstable.
    • So in exam conditions:

      \(\mathrm{benzyl,\ allyl > alkyl \gg vinyl}\)

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Assuming every carbocation near a double bond is resonance-stabilised.
    2. Confusing allylic carbocation with vinylic carbocation.
    3. Forgetting that in a vinylic carbocation, the positive charge is on the double-bond carbon itself, which is the key reason for its instability.
    4. Ignoring hybridisation. Carbocations are more stable when the positively charged carbon is less electronegative.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – General Organic Chemistry
    • Topic: Reaction intermediates
    • Subtopic: Stability of carbocations

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Resonance stabilisation dominates over simple inductive stabilisation.
    • Allylic and benzylic carbocations are especially important and very commonly tested.
    • Vinylic carbocations are among the least stable carbocations.
    • A quick stability idea:

      \(\mathrm{Benzylic \approx Allylic > 3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ > Methyl > Vinyl}\)

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(\mathrm{CH_2=CH^+}\) is the least stable carbocation because it is a vinylic carbocation in which the positive charge is on an \(sp\)-hybridised carbon. Such a carbon is more electronegative and cannot accommodate positive charge effectively. Also, it does not get the strong resonance stabilisation available to allylic and benzylic carbocations.

    \(\boxed{(b)\ \mathrm{CH_2=CH^+}}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Hyperconjugation stabilises: alkyl carbocations
    • Resonance stabilises strongly: allylic, benzylic carbocations
    • Least stable: vinylic carbocation
    • Watch for: position of the positive charge
    • Golden clue:

      \(\text{positive charge on double-bond carbon} \Rightarrow \text{very unstable}\)

    Answer: \((d)\)


    Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    The starting compound is anisole, \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OCH_3}\). We have to find the final product \(Z\).

    1. Reaction of anisole with \(\mathrm{HI}\)

      Anisole is an aryl-alkyl ether. On heating with \(\mathrm{HI}\), ether cleavage occurs at the alkyl-oxygen bond, not at the aryl-oxygen bond.

      Therefore:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OCH_3 + HI \rightarrow C_6H_5OH + CH_3I}\)

      So:

      • \(X = \mathrm{C_6H_5OH}\) (phenol)
      • \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3I}\) (methyl iodide)
    2. Why cleavage happens at \(\mathrm{O-CH_3}\) and not at \(\mathrm{O-C_6H_5}\)

      The \(\mathrm{C_6H_5-O}\) bond in anisole has partial double-bond character due to resonance, so it is difficult to break.

      Also, nucleophilic substitution at an \(\mathrm{sp^2}\)-hybridised aryl carbon does not occur easily.

      Hence \(\mathrm{I^-}\) attacks the methyl group and forms \(\mathrm{CH_3I}\).

    3. Reaction of \(X\) with Zn / \(\Delta\)

      Phenol on distillation with zinc dust gives benzene:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OH \xrightarrow{Zn,\ \Delta} C_6H_6}\)

      This matches the reaction shown, so our identification of \(X = \mathrm{phenol}\) is confirmed.

    4. Reaction of \(Y\) with benzene in presence of anhydrous \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\)

      Since \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3I}\), it undergoes Friedel-Crafts alkylation with benzene:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + CH_3I \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5CH_3 + HI}\)

      The product formed is toluene.

    Therefore, \(Z\) is:

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_3}\)

    Hence, the correct option is \((d)\).

    Why this method works:

    • In aryl-alkyl ethers, cleavage with \(\mathrm{HI}\) or \(\mathrm{HBr}\) occurs at the alkyl side.
    • Phenol formation from anisole is a standard NCERT reaction.
    • Methyl iodide formed in the first step is an excellent substrate for Friedel-Crafts alkylation.
    • The Zn-dust step is a useful confirmation because \(\mathrm{phenol \rightarrow benzene}\) is a classic conversion.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Cleavage of ethers with \(\mathrm{HI}\):

      \(\mathrm{R-O-R' + HI \rightarrow R-OH + R'-I}\)

    • In anisole:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OCH_3 + HI \rightarrow C_6H_5OH + CH_3I}\)

    • Phenol with Zn dust:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OH \xrightarrow{Zn} C_6H_6}\)

    • Friedel-Crafts alkylation: alkyl halide + benzene \(\rightarrow\) alkylbenzene
    • \(\mathrm{CH_3I}\) with benzene gives toluene.

    Reaction flow:

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Immediately remember:

      \(\mathrm{anisole + HI \rightarrow phenol + CH_3I}\)

    • Then use the clue:

      \(\mathrm{phenol \xrightarrow{Zn} benzene}\)

    • So \(X\) must be phenol and \(Y\) must be methyl iodide.
    • Then:

      \(\mathrm{benzene + CH_3I / AlCl_3 \rightarrow toluene}\)

    • So choose the structure with a \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) group on benzene: option \((d)\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking anisole gives phenyl iodide with \(\mathrm{HI}\).

      The aryl-oxygen bond is not cleaved easily.

    2. Confusing \(\mathrm{CH_3I}\) with \(\mathrm{C_2H_5I}\), leading to ethylbenzene instead of toluene.
    3. Forgetting the standard reaction:

      \(\mathrm{phenol \xrightarrow{Zn} benzene}\)

    4. Choosing benzyl chloride instead of toluene in Friedel-Crafts step.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
    • Topic: Reactions of ethers and phenols
    • Subtopic: Cleavage of anisole by \(\mathrm{HI}\), phenol to benzene conversion, and Friedel-Crafts alkylation

    Final exam-style answer:

    Anisole reacts with \(\mathrm{HI}\) to give phenol and methyl iodide:

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_5OCH_3 + HI \rightarrow C_6H_5OH + CH_3I}\)

    Thus \(X = \mathrm{phenol}\) and \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3I}\).

    Since phenol on heating with zinc dust gives benzene, this confirms \(X\).

    Then methyl iodide reacts with benzene in presence of anhydrous \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) to form toluene:

    \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + CH_3I \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5CH_3}\)

    Therefore \(Z = \mathrm{toluene}\).

    \(\boxed{(d)}\)

    Quick cheatsheet:

    • Anisole + \(\mathrm{HI}\):

      \(\mathrm{phenol + CH_3I}\)

    • Phenol + Zn dust:

      \(\mathrm{benzene}\)

    • \(\mathrm{CH_3I + benzene / AlCl_3}\):

      \(\mathrm{toluene}\)

    • Key bond cleavage rule: cleavage occurs at alkyl-O bond in anisole
    • Final product \(Z\):

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_3}\)

    • Correct answer: (b) propanone
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: The given vicinal dibromo compound first undergoes double dehydrohalogenation to form propyne. Hydration of propyne in the presence of \(\mathrm{Hg^{2+}/H^+}\) gives an enol, which immediately isomerises by keto-enol tautomerism to propanone. Therefore, \(Z\) is propanone.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The starting compound is \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH(Br)-CH_2Br}\), a dibromo derivative of propane.
      • With alcoholic KOH and then \(\mathrm{NaNH_2}\), two molecules of HBr are removed in sequence.
      • This converts the dibromo compound into an alkyne:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH(Br)-CH_2Br \rightarrow CH_3-C{\equiv}CH}\)
      • So, \(X =\) propyne.
      • Now propyne undergoes hydration in the presence of \(\mathrm{Hg^{2+}/H^+}\) at \(333\ \mathrm{K}\).
      • This is acid-catalysed mercuric ion assisted hydration of a terminal alkyne, which follows Markovnikov addition.
      • Water adds across the triple bond to first give an enol:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-C(OH){=}CH_2}\)
      • This enol is unstable and undergoes keto-enol tautomerism.
      • The keto form formed is:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-CO-CH_3}\)
      • Therefore, \(Y\) is the enol and \(Z\) is propanone.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Vicinal or geminal dihalides on strong dehydrohalogenation give alkynes.
      • Alcoholic KOH usually removes one molecule of HX to form an alkene.
      • A stronger base like \(\mathrm{NaNH_2}\) removes the second molecule of HX to form the alkyne.
      • Terminal alkynes on hydration in the presence of \(\mathrm{Hg^{2+}/H^+}\) give Markovnikov addition products.
      • The initially formed alcohol is not stable as such when it is attached to a doubly bonded carbon. It exists as an enol and then rearranges to the more stable carbonyl compound.
      • This process is called keto-enol tautomerism.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Propyne on mercuric ion catalysed hydration does not stop at the enol stage.
      • It finally gives the ketone \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\).
      • \(\mathrm{CH_3COCH_3}\) is propanone.
      • Hence option (b) is correct.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • (a) propanal: Terminal alkynes under \(\mathrm{Hg^{2+}/H^+}\) hydration give methyl ketones, not aldehydes.
      • (c) propanoic acid: No oxidation conditions are given. Hydration does not produce a carboxylic acid here.
      • (d) propanamide: No nitrogen-containing reagent is involved that can form an amide.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Remember this standard pattern:
        • vicinal/geminal dihalide \(\rightarrow\) alkyne
        • terminal alkyne \(+\ \mathrm{H_2O,\ Hg^{2+}/H^+}\ \rightarrow\) methyl ketone
      • So, whenever you see propyne under these hydration conditions, you should immediately think of propanone.
      • This is a very common exam pattern.
    • Final result: \(Z =\) propanone.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Organic Chemistry / Hydrocarbons / Preparation of alkynes and hydration of alkynes
    • Correct answer: (c) 6-amino-2-bromo-5-hydroxy-3-oxohexanenitrile
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: In IUPAC naming, nitrile has higher priority than ketone, alcohol and amino groups, so the parent chain must include the nitrile carbon and is named as hexanenitrile. Numbering from the nitrile carbon gives bromo at C-2, oxo at C-3, hydroxy at C-5 and amino at C-6. Hence the correct name is 6-amino-2-bromo-5-hydroxy-3-oxohexanenitrile.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • First identify the principal functional group.
      • The structure contains:
        • a nitrile group \((\mathrm{-CN})\)
        • a ketone group \((\mathrm{>C{=}O})\)
        • hydroxy group \((\mathrm{-OH})\)
        • an amino group \((\mathrm{-NH_2})\)
        • a bromo substituent \((\mathrm{-Br})\)
      • Among these, nitrile has the highest priority, so it becomes the suffix.
      • Therefore the parent chain must include the nitrile carbon as carbon number 1.
      • Now count the longest chain including the nitrile carbon:
        • \(\mathrm{C_1(CN)-C_2(Br)-C_3(=O)-C_4H_2-C_5(OH)-C_6H_2NH_2}\)
      • So the parent name is hexanenitrile.
      • Now place substituents and prefixes according to positions:
        • C-2: bromo
        • C-3: oxo
        • C-5: hydroxy
        • C-6: amino
      • Hence the full name becomes:
        • 6-amino-2-bromo-5-hydroxy-3-oxohexanenitrile
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Functional group priority is very important in IUPAC nomenclature.
      • Nitrile \((\mathrm{-C{\equiv}N})\) has higher priority than ketone, alcohol and amino groups.
      • Therefore:
        • nitrile is written as the suffix \(\mathrm{-nitrile}\)
        • ketone is written as prefix oxo
        • alcohol is written as prefix hydroxy
        • amino is written as prefix amino
      • When nitrile is the principal group, its carbon is counted as part of the main chain and gets position 1.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (c) correctly:
        • takes nitrile as the principal functional group
        • uses a 6-carbon chain, so hexanenitrile
        • assigns locants as 2-bromo, 3-oxo, 5-hydroxy and 6-amino
      • So it matches the structure perfectly.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • (a): This treats the molecule in a non-standard way and does not correctly choose nitrile as the principal suffix with proper parent chain naming.
      • (b): Uses pentanenitrile, but the correct parent chain has 6 carbons including the nitrile carbon.
      • (d): Again incorrectly uses pentanenitrile instead of hexanenitrile.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • In nitrile-based naming questions, first ask:
        • Is the nitrile carbon included in the main chain?
      • If yes, start numbering from that carbon as C-1.
      • This single step often eliminates wrong options immediately.
      • Also remember: ketone loses suffix priority in the presence of nitrile and becomes oxo.
    • Final result: The correct IUPAC name is 6-amino-2-bromo-5-hydroxy-3-oxohexanenitrile.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Organic Chemistry / IUPAC nomenclature / Functional group priority and naming of nitriles
    • Correct answer: (b) Distillation under reduced pressure
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: High boiling organic liquids that decompose at or below their normal boiling point are purified by distillation under reduced pressure, also called vacuum distillation. Lowering the external pressure lowers the boiling point, so the liquid distils at a safer temperature without decomposing.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The question describes a liquid with two important features:
        • it has a high boiling point
        • it decomposes before reaching that normal boiling point
      • If we try ordinary distillation, we would have to heat it too much.
      • That much heating would cause decomposition, so ordinary distillation is unsuitable.
      • To avoid this, we reduce the external pressure.
      • When pressure is lowered, the boiling point of the liquid decreases.
      • Now the liquid can boil and be distilled at a temperature much lower than its normal boiling point.
      • Therefore, the correct method is distillation under reduced pressure.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • A liquid boils when its vapour pressure becomes equal to the external pressure.
      • If external pressure is reduced, this equality is reached at a lower temperature.
      • That is why vacuum distillation is used for heat-sensitive or high-boiling compounds.
      • Typical examples include substances like glycerol, aniline and nitrobenzene, which are better purified without heating them to their full normal boiling points.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (b) directly matches the condition given in the question.
      • It is the standard laboratory method for purification of high-boiling liquids that may decompose on strong heating.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • (a) Distillation: Ordinary distillation is used when the substance can safely boil at its normal boiling point.
      • (c) Steam distillation: This is mainly used for water-immiscible volatile compounds, especially natural oils or temperature-sensitive volatile substances, not for every high-boiling decomposing liquid.
      • (d) Fractional distillation: This is used for separating miscible liquids with close boiling points, not specifically for avoiding decomposition.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever the question says:
        • high boiling
        • decomposes on heating
        think immediately of vacuum distillation or distillation under reduced pressure.
      • If the question instead mentions volatile liquids mixed together with close boiling points, then think of fractional distillation.
    • Final result: The generally used method is distillation under reduced pressure.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Organic Chemistry / Purification of organic compounds / Vacuum distillation
    • Correct answer: (a) A-II, B-IV, C-I, D-III
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: Each reaction can be matched by recalling one standard named or highly repeated transformation from hydrocarbons. Propyne on hydration gives propanone, sodium acetate on Kolbe electrolysis gives ethane, propene on acid-catalysed hydration gives isopropyl alcohol, and propene with cold dilute \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) gives the vicinal diol. So the correct matching is A-II, B-IV, C-I, D-III.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • A. \(\mathrm{CH_3-C\equiv CH \xrightarrow[\ Hg^{2+}/H^+,\ 333\,K\ ]{H_2O}}\)
        • This is hydration of a terminal alkyne in presence of \(\mathrm{Hg^{2+}/H^+}\).
        • Water adds according to Markovnikov rule.
        • First an enol is formed, which rapidly tautomerises to a ketone.
        • So:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3-C\equiv CH \rightarrow CH_3-CO-CH_3}\)
