        • Hence, A matches II.
      • B. \(\mathrm{CH_3COONa \xrightarrow[\ Electrolysis\ ]{H_2O,\ Pt}}\)
        • This is Kolbe’s electrolysis.
        • At the anode, acetate ion loses \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) to form methyl radicals.
        • Two methyl radicals couple to give ethane.
        • Overall major organic product:
          • \(\mathrm{2CH_3COO^- \rightarrow C_2H_6 + 2CO_2 + 2e^-}\)
        • So the product is \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH_3}\).
        • Hence, B matches IV.
      • C. \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH_2 \xrightarrow{H_2O/H^+}}\)
        • This is acid-catalysed hydration of propene.
        • Water adds in Markovnikov manner.
        • The \(\mathrm{OH}\) group goes to the more substituted carbon.
        • So the product is:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_3}\)
        • This is propan-2-ol.
        • Hence, C matches I.
      • D. \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH_2 \xrightarrow[\ 273\,K\ ]{H_2O,\ dil\ KMnO_4}}\)
        • Cold dilute alkaline or neutral \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) adds two \(\mathrm{OH}\) groups across the double bond.
        • This is hydroxylation of alkene.
        • So propene forms propane-1,2-diol:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_2OH}\)
        • Hence, D matches III.
      • Therefore the full matching is A-II, B-IV, C-I, D-III.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Hydration of terminal alkynes
        • In presence of \(\mathrm{Hg^{2+}/H^+}\), terminal alkynes give Markovnikov addition of water.
        • The initially formed enol is unstable and changes into a carbonyl compound by keto-enol tautomerism.
        • For propyne, the final product is propanone.
      • Kolbe electrolysis
        • Sodium or potassium salts of carboxylic acids on electrolysis lose \(\mathrm{CO_2}\).
        • The resulting alkyl radicals couple to form alkanes.
        • Acetate gives methyl radical, and coupling gives ethane.
      • Hydration of alkenes
        • Acid-catalysed hydration follows Markovnikov addition.
        • So propene gives propan-2-ol, not propan-1-ol.
      • Baeyer-type hydroxylation
        • Cold dilute \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) converts alkene to vicinal glycol.
        • Hence propene gives propane-1,2-diol.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • A gives propanone \(\Rightarrow\) II.
      • B gives ethane \(\Rightarrow\) IV.
      • C gives propan-2-ol \(\Rightarrow\) I.
      • D gives propane-1,2-diol \(\Rightarrow\) III.
      • Only option (a) contains exactly this sequence.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • Option (b): B is wrongly matched with V. Kolbe electrolysis of sodium acetate gives ethane, not methane.
      • Option (c): A is wrongly matched with III and C is wrongly matched with II. Hydration of propyne gives ketone, while hydration of propene gives alcohol.
      • Option (d): B and C are both mismatched. Kolbe electrolysis does not give a diol, and acid-catalysed hydration of propene does not give ethane.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Remember these four rapid triggers:
        • terminal alkyne + \(\mathrm{H_2O/Hg^{2+}/H^+}\) \(\rightarrow\) ketone
        • Kolbe electrolysis of acetate \(\rightarrow\) ethane
        • alkene + \(\mathrm{H_2O/H^+}\) \(\rightarrow\) Markovnikov alcohol
        • alkene + cold dil \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) \(\rightarrow\) vicinal diol
      • In one minute, solve the unique ones first:
        • B must be ethane, so B-IV immediately.
        • D with cold dilute \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) must be glycol, so D-III immediately.
      • Then only one option remains consistent: (a).
    • Final result: The correct matching is \(\mathrm{A-II,\ B-IV,\ C-I,\ D-III}\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
      • Topic: Reactions of alkenes and alkynes; Kolbe electrolysis
      • Subtopic: Hydration, hydroxylation and electrochemical decarboxylation
    • Correct answer: (b) I, II, III.
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: In figure I, \(n\)-butane is written in condensed form as \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_3}\). Figure II shows the bond-line or skeletal formula. Figure III shows the complete displayed formula with all carbon and hydrogen atoms and bonds. Hence the correct order is I, II, III.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The question is asking us to identify three different ways of representing the same compound, \(n\)-butane.
      • \(n\)-Butane is the straight-chain alkane with four carbon atoms.
      • Its molecular formula is:
        • \(\mathrm{C_4H_{10}}\)
      • Now check each labeled form.
      • I is written as:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_3}\)
      • This is the condensed formula.
      • II is shown as a zig-zag chain line.
      • This is the bond-line formula, also called skeletal formula.
      • III shows every carbon, hydrogen and bond explicitly.
      • This is the complete displayed formula.
      • So the correct order is:
        • Condensed formula \(\rightarrow\) I
        • Bond-line formula \(\rightarrow\) II
        • Complete formula \(\rightarrow\) III
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Organic compounds can be represented in different notations.
      • Condensed formula groups atoms together without showing every bond separately.
      • Bond-line formula shows only the carbon skeleton as zig-zag lines; carbon and attached hydrogens are understood.
      • Complete displayed formula shows all atoms and all covalent bonds explicitly.
      • For alkanes, bond-line formulas are especially useful because they make long carbon chains easy to read quickly.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (b) gives the order I, II, III.
      • This exactly matches condensed, bond-line and complete formulae respectively.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) is wrong because II is not condensed form.
      • (c) is wrong because III is not bond-line form.
      • (d) is wrong because II is neither condensed nor III bond-line in that order.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • If all atoms are written groupwise like \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_3}\), it is condensed form.
      • If only zig-zag lines are shown, it is bond-line form.
      • If every H and every bond is drawn, it is complete displayed formula.
    • Final result
      • Condensed formula \(\rightarrow\) I
      • Bond-line formula \(\rightarrow\) II
      • Complete formula \(\rightarrow\) III
      • Hence, option (b) is correct.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry - Some Basic Principles and Techniques
      • Topic: Representation of organic compounds
      • Subtopic: Condensed, bond-line and complete structural formulae
    • Correct answer: (d) Wurtz reaction of 2-bromopropane with Na in dry ether.
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: An isomer of \(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}}\) having four primary carbons and two tertiary carbons is 2,3-dimethylbutane. This structure is obtained by Wurtz coupling of two molecules of 2-bromopropane in dry ether.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • We need an isomer of \(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}}\) with:
        • 4 primary carbons
        • 2 tertiary carbons
      • Let us think structurally.
      • The isomer 2,3-dimethylbutane is:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH(CH_3)-CH(CH_3)-CH_3}\)
      • In this molecule:
        • the four \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) carbons are primary
        • the two middle \(\mathrm{CH}\) carbons are each attached to three other carbons, so they are tertiary
      • So \(X\) must be 2,3-dimethylbutane.
      • Now see which reaction can give this structure.
      • In Wurtz reaction, two alkyl halide molecules couple in presence of sodium and dry ether.
      • For 2-bromopropane:
        • \(2(CH_3)_2CHBr + 2Na \rightarrow (CH_3)_2CH-CH(CH_3)_2 + 2NaBr\)
      • The product formed is exactly 2,3-dimethylbutane.
      • Hence option (d) is correct.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Primary carbon is attached to only one other carbon atom.
      • Tertiary carbon is attached to three other carbon atoms.
      • Wurtz reaction couples two alkyl halides:
        • \(2R-X + 2Na \rightarrow R-R + 2NaX\)
      • When the same secondary alkyl halide is used, symmetric coupling can produce a branched alkane.
      • This question combines carbon classification with reaction-based synthesis.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (d) uses 2-bromopropane.
      • Coupling of two isopropyl groups gives:
        • \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_2CH-CH(CH_3)_2}\)
      • This is 2,3-dimethylbutane, which has exactly four primary and two tertiary carbons.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) hydrogenation of the given alkene gives a different branched hexane, not the required one.
      • (b) Wurtz reaction of 1-bromobutane gives \(n\)-octane or related longer-chain product, not a \(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}}\) isomer.
      • (c) reduction of the given alkyl chloride gives a hexane isomer with a different carbon classification.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • For \(\mathrm{C_6H_{14}}\), if the question says 4 primary + 2 tertiary carbons, think immediately of 2,3-dimethylbutane.
      • Then ask which reagent can join two \(\mathrm{C_3}\) units symmetrically.
      • That directly points to Wurtz reaction of 2-bromopropane.
    • Final result
      • \(X =\) 2,3-dimethylbutane
      • It is prepared by Wurtz reaction of 2-bromopropane.
      • Hence, option (d) is correct.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
      • Topic: Alkanes and their preparation
      • Subtopic: Wurtz reaction; classification of carbon atoms in alkanes
    • Correct answer: (c) X is 2-methyl-2-butanol and Y is 2-methyl-2-butene.
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Oxidation of isopentane under the given condition gives the corresponding tertiary alcohol, 2-methyl-2-butanol. On dehydration with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(358\ \mathrm{K}\), this alcohol gives the more substituted alkene as major product, namely 2-methyl-2-butene.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • Start with isopentane, whose structure is:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH(CH_3)-CH_2-CH_3}\)
      • The carbon at position 2 is a tertiary-type center for oxidation/substitution context in this branched framework, and the product shown in the key is the tertiary alcohol:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-C(CH_3)(OH)-CH_2-CH_3}\)
      • So:
        • \(X =\) 2-methyl-2-butanol
      • Now treat this alcohol with \(20\%\ \mathrm{H_3PO_4}\) at \(358\ \mathrm{K}\).
      • This is a dehydration reaction, meaning water is removed and an alkene is formed.
      • From 2-methyl-2-butanol, possible alkenes can form by removing a \(\beta\)-hydrogen.
      • According to Saytzeff rule, the more substituted alkene is the major product.
      • Therefore the major alkene is:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-C(CH_3)=CH-CH_3}\)
      • This is 2-methyl-2-butene.
      • So:
        • \(Y =\) 2-methyl-2-butene
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This question combines oxidation/substitution at branched alkane center with dehydration of alcohol.
      • Tertiary alcohols on dehydration generally form alkenes through elimination.
      • Saytzeff rule says the major alkene is usually the more substituted, more stable alkene.
      • So once \(X\) is recognized as 2-methyl-2-butanol, the major product of dehydration is expected to be 2-methyl-2-butene.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (c) gives:
        • \(X =\) 2-methyl-2-butanol
        • \(Y =\) 2-methyl-2-butene
      • This exactly matches the reaction sequence shown in the solution key.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) gives the wrong alcohol and a less appropriate alkene product.
      • (b) has the correct alcohol but wrong alkene; dehydration major product is not 2-methyl-1-butene here.
      • (d) gives a primary alcohol, which does not match the given transformation.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • When acid-catalyzed dehydration of an alcohol is asked, look for the more substituted alkene as major product.
      • So among 2-methyl-1-butene and 2-methyl-2-butene, the latter is more substituted and more stable.
      • That helps eliminate options quickly.
    • Final result
      • \(X =\) 2-methyl-2-butanol
      • \(Y =\) 2-methyl-2-butene
      • Hence, option (c) is correct.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Alcohols
      • Topic: Oxidation and dehydration reactions
      • Subtopic: Dehydration of alcohols and alkene major-product prediction
    • Correct answer: (a) \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl,\ CH_3COCl}\).
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: Benzene gives toluene by Friedel-Crafts alkylation using \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl/AlCl_3}\). Benzene gives acetophenone by Friedel-Crafts acylation using acetyl chloride \(\mathrm{CH_3COCl/AlCl_3}\). Therefore \(X = \mathrm{CH_3Cl}\) and \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3COCl}\).
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The question shows benzene being converted into two different products in presence of anhydrous \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\).
      • Anhydrous \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) is the classic Lewis acid catalyst used in Friedel-Crafts reactions.
      • First conversion:
        • Benzene \(\rightarrow\) toluene
      • To introduce a \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\) group on benzene, we use methyl chloride in Friedel-Crafts alkylation:
        • \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + CH_3Cl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5CH_3 + HCl}\)
      • So:
        • \(X = \mathrm{CH_3Cl}\)
      • Second conversion:
        • Benzene \(\rightarrow\) acetophenone
      • Acetophenone is \(\mathrm{C_6H_5COCH_3}\), so we must introduce the acetyl group \(\mathrm{-COCH_3}\).
      • This is done by Friedel-Crafts acylation using acetyl chloride:
        • \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + CH_3COCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} C_6H_5COCH_3 + HCl}\)
      • So:
        • \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3COCl}\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Friedel-Crafts alkylation introduces an alkyl group onto benzene using alkyl halide and \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\).
      • Friedel-Crafts acylation introduces an acyl group \(\mathrm{RCO-}\) onto benzene using acyl chloride and \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\).
      • \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl}\) gives methyl substitution and forms toluene.
      • \(\mathrm{CH_3COCl}\) gives acetyl substitution and forms acetophenone.
      • This is a direct named-reaction recognition question.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (a) gives:
        • \(X = \mathrm{CH_3Cl}\)
        • \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3COCl}\)
      • These reagents exactly produce toluene and acetophenone respectively.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (b) would give ethylbenzene instead of toluene in the first reaction.
      • (c) reverses the required reagents.
      • (d) would give propiophenone instead of acetophenone in the second reaction.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • If the product is toluene, the introduced group must be \(\mathrm{-CH_3}\), so think \(\mathrm{CH_3Cl/AlCl_3}\).
      • If the product is acetophenone, the introduced group must be \(\mathrm{-COCH_3}\), so think \(\mathrm{CH_3COCl/AlCl_3}\).
      • Alkylation adds \(\mathrm{-R}\); acylation adds \(\mathrm{-COR}\).
    • Final result
      • \(X = \mathrm{CH_3Cl}\)
      • \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3COCl}\)
      • Hence, option (a) is correct.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
      • Topic: Aromatic electrophilic substitution
      • Subtopic: Friedel-Crafts alkylation and Friedel-Crafts acylation
    • Correct answer: (c) \(Y > X > Z\).
      Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: In an \(S_N1\) reaction, the rate depends mainly on the stability of the carbocation formed after the leaving group departs. Here, \(Y\) gives the most stable tertiary benzylic-type carbocation, \(X\) gives a secondary one, and \(Z\) gives a primary one. Therefore the order is \(Y > X > Z\).
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • First identify what \(X\), \(Y\), and \(Z\) are after addition of HBr.
      • From the given reaction pattern:
        • \(X\) is \(1\)-bromo-\(1\)-phenylethane
        • \(Y\) is \(2\)-bromo-\(2\)-phenylpropane
        • \(Z\) is \(1\)-bromo-\(2\)-phenylethane
      • Now think about \(S_N1\) mechanism.
      • In \(S_N1\), the slow step is:
        • breaking of the \(C-Br\) bond to form a carbocation
      • So the more stable the carbocation, the faster the \(S_N1\) reaction.
      • For \(Y\):
        • loss of \(Br^-\) gives a highly stable tertiary carbocation attached to a phenyl-bearing carbon framework
        • this is the most stable among the three
      • For \(X\):
        • loss of \(Br^-\) gives a secondary carbocation
        • it is less stable than that from \(Y\), but more stable than a primary carbocation
      • For \(Z\):
        • loss of \(Br^-\) gives a primary carbocation
        • this is the least stable
      • Therefore the decreasing order of \(S_N1\) reactivity is:
        • \(3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ\)
        • so \(Y > X > Z\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This is an organic chemistry question combining:
        • addition of HBr to alkenes
        • peroxide effect
        • \(S_N1\) reactivity
      • Without peroxide, HBr usually adds by the ionic Markovnikov route.
      • With peroxide \((C_6H_5COO)_2\), HBr adds by the radical anti-Markovnikov route.
      • After forming the bromides, the next part of the question asks about their \(S_N1\) behaviour.
      • \(S_N1\) is favoured when the substrate can form a stable carbocation.
      • General carbocation stability rule:
        • \(3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ\)
      • Resonance and alkyl substitution both help stabilize carbocations.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • \(Y\) forms the most stable carbocation, so it reacts fastest by \(S_N1\).
      • \(X\) forms a less stable secondary carbocation, so it comes next.
      • \(Z\) forms the least stable primary carbocation, so it is slowest.
      • Hence \(Y > X > Z\).
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Any option placing \(Z\) above \(X\) is wrong because primary carbocations are less stable than secondary ones.
      • Any option placing \(X\) above \(Y\) is wrong because a tertiary carbocation is more stable than a secondary carbocation.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • For \(S_N1\), do not overcomplicate the first step.
      • Simply ask:
        • what carbocation is formed after \(Br^-\) leaves?
      • Then compare stability directly:
        • \(3^\circ > 2^\circ > 1^\circ\)
      • This solves the MCQ quickly.
    • Final result
      • The correct order of reactivity towards \(S_N1\) reaction is \(Y > X > Z\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
      • Topic: Addition of HBr and nucleophilic substitution
      • Subtopic: Carbocation stability and \(S_N1\) reactivity order
    • Correct answer: (d) CrO3 + (CH3CO)2O, H3O+; KMnO4 - KOH/\( \Delta \), H3O+.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: \(X\) must be benzaldehyde because it gives 2,4-DNP test and also reduces ammoniacal silver nitrate solution (Tollen’s reagent). \(Y\) must be benzoic acid because it reacts with NaHCO3 giving CO2. So reagent \(A\) must convert toluene into benzaldehyde, and reagent \(B\) must oxidise toluene fully into benzoic acid.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The product \(X\) forms a 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazone. This tells us \(X\) contains a carbonyl group, so it must be either an aldehyde or a ketone.
      • \(X\) also reduces ammoniacal silver nitrate solution. That is the Tollen’s test, which is given by aldehydes, not ordinary ketones.
      • So \(X\) is an aldehyde derived from toluene, namely benzaldehyde, \(C_6H_5CHO\).
      • Toluene is converted to benzaldehyde by controlled side-chain oxidation using chromyl reagent conditions, here represented by CrO3 + (CH3CO)2O, followed by hydrolysis.
      • Now look at \(Y\): it dissolves in NaHCO3 with evolution of CO2. That is the characteristic reaction of a carboxylic acid.
      • Oxidation of toluene with strong oxidising agent KMnO4 in alkaline medium on heating, followed by acidification, converts the side chain completely to benzoic acid, \(C_6H_5COOH\).
      • Hence \(A\) is CrO3 + (CH3CO)2O, H3O+ and \(B\) is KMnO4 - KOH/\( \Delta \), H3O+.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • 2,4-DNP test identifies aldehydes and ketones because both have the \(>C=O\) group.
      • Tollen’s reagent distinguishes aldehydes from ketones. Aldehydes are easily oxidised to acids, so Ag+ is reduced to metallic silver.
      • NaHCO3 test distinguishes carboxylic acids from many other organic compounds because acids stronger than carbonic acid release CO2.
      • Alkyl side chains on benzene having benzylic hydrogen usually undergo complete oxidation with hot alkaline KMnO4 to give benzoic acid.
      • Controlled oxidation of toluene to benzaldehyde is a standard exam pattern. Full oxidation gives benzoic acid; controlled oxidation stops at aldehyde.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Its first reagent set gives benzaldehyde, which fits both 2,4-DNP and Tollen’s test.
      • Its second reagent set gives benzoic acid, which reacts with NaHCO3 to evolve CO2.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Any option that uses KMnO4 for making \(X\) is wrong because hot permanganate does not stop at aldehyde; it usually oxidises the side chain fully to benzoic acid.
      • Any option that uses the benzaldehyde-forming reagent for \(Y\) is wrong because \(Y\) must be a carboxylic acid, not an aldehyde.
      • So the two reagent roles are not interchangeable here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • 2,4-DNP + Tollen’s together almost immediately means aldehyde.
      • NaHCO3 with CO2 almost immediately means carboxylic acid.
      • For alkyl benzene side chain oxidation, KMnO4 usually means benzoic acid, not aldehyde.
    • Final result:
      • \(X =\) benzaldehyde
      • \(Y =\) benzoic acid
      • \(A =\) CrO3 + (CH3CO)2O, H3O+
      • \(B =\) KMnO4 - KOH/\( \Delta \), H3O+
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Hydrocarbons and Aldehydes, Ketones, Carboxylic Acids
      • Subtopic: Side-chain oxidation of alkyl benzene, qualitative tests of aldehydes and carboxylic acids
    • Correct answer: (a) \(X =\) propanone and \(Y =\) ethanal.
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: \(C_5H_{12}O\) on passing over Cu at 573 K must first give an alkene \(C_5H_{10}\). The alkene formed is 2-methylbut-2-ene, and on ozonolysis it splits into propanone and ethanal.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting compound has molecular formula \(C_5H_{12}O\).
      • On heating alcohol vapours with copper at 573 K:
        • primary alcohols usually give aldehydes,
        • secondary alcohols usually give ketones,
        • tertiary alcohols cannot be dehydrogenated, so they undergo dehydration to give alkenes.
      • Since the product given is \(C_5H_{10}\), it must be an alkene. So the alcohol is a tertiary alcohol.
      • The suitable tertiary alcohol with formula \(C_5H_{12}O\) is 2-methylbutan-2-ol.
      • On heating with Cu at 573 K, it gives 2-methylbut-2-ene.
      • Now apply ozonolysis:
        • Ozone breaks the \(C=C\) double bond.
        • Each doubly bonded carbon becomes a carbonyl carbon.
      • For 2-methylbut-2-ene, the structure can be written as \(CH_3 - C(CH_3)=CH - CH_3\).
      • On cleavage of the double bond:
        • the carbon attached to two \(CH_3\) groups gives propanone, \(CH_3COCH_3\),
        • the other carbon attached to \(H\) and \(CH_3\) gives ethanal, \(CH_3CHO\).
      • Therefore, \(X =\) propanone and \(Y =\) ethanal.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Copper at 573 K is a standard reagent used in alcohol conversions.
      • Tertiary alcohols lack the necessary hydrogen on the carbon bearing OH for dehydrogenation, so they preferentially undergo elimination.
      • Ozonolysis is a very important method for locating the position of the double bond, because the alkene splits into smaller carbonyl compounds.
      • If a double-bond carbon has:
        • two alkyl groups, it gives a ketone,
        • one alkyl group and one H, it gives an aldehyde.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • The alkene produced is 2-methylbut-2-ene.
      • Its ozonolysis products are exactly propanone + ethanal.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (b) gives products that do not arise from cleavage of 2-methylbut-2-ene.
      • (c) would require both fragments to become aldehydes, which is not possible here because one double-bond carbon has no hydrogen.
      • (d) is wrong because ozonolysis with reductive workup \((Zn + H_2O)\) gives aldehydes/ketones, not carboxylic acids like methanoic acid.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • In ozonolysis, first identify how many alkyl groups are attached to each double-bond carbon.
      • Two carbon groups \(\rightarrow\) ketone; one H present \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde.
      • Also remember: \(Zn + H_2O\) is reductive workup, so do not expect acids.
    • Final result:
      • \(X = CH_3COCH_3\) (propanone)
      • \(Y = CH_3CHO\) (ethanal)
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Alcohols and Hydrocarbons
      • Subtopic: Behaviour of alcohols with Cu at 573 K, ozonolysis of alkenes

    2024

    Answer: \((b)\ -R\) effect


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    The structure shown is benzaldehyde, where the \(\mathrm{-CHO}\) group is attached to a benzene ring.

    1. Identify the effect being shown

      The curved arrows indicate that electron density is being withdrawn from the benzene ring toward the carbonyl group \(\mathrm{(-CHO)}\) through conjugation.

      This is a resonance effect, also called the mesomeric effect.

    2. Why it is \(-R\) effect

      In the given resonance form, electrons move from the ring toward the \(\mathrm{C=O}\) group, and the oxygen gains negative charge while the ring develops positive charge.

      So the substituent is withdrawing electrons by resonance.

      Therefore, it shows \(-R\) effect.

    3. What \(-R\) effect means

      A group shows \(-R\) effect when it pulls electron density from a conjugated system through resonance.

      Common \(-R\) groups are:

      • \(\mathrm{-CHO}\)
      • \(\mathrm{-COR}\)
      • \(\mathrm{-COOH}\)
      • \(\mathrm{-COOR}\)
      • \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\)
      • \(\mathrm{-CN}\)
    4. Why other options are wrong
      • \((a)\ +R\) effect: In \(+R\) effect, a group donates electrons by resonance into the ring. That is not happening here.
      • \((c)\) Electromeric effect: Electromeric effect is a temporary effect seen only in the presence of an attacking reagent, usually involving complete transfer of \(\pi\)-electrons. Here the figure shows resonance, not electromeric effect.
      • \((d)\ -I\) effect: Inductive effect operates through \(\sigma\)-bonds due to electronegativity difference. The figure is showing electron displacement by delocalisation, so this is not \(-I\) effect.

    NCERT / JEE theory takeaway:

    • Resonance effect: Electron displacement through a chain of conjugated \(\pi\)-bonds.
    • \(+R\) effect: Electron donation by resonance.
    • \(-R\) effect: Electron withdrawal by resonance.
    • Groups containing multiple bonds to electronegative atoms, like \(\mathrm{C=O}\), usually show \(-R\) effect.
    • In aromatic compounds, \(-R\) groups reduce electron density on the ring, especially at ortho and para positions.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • Look at the arrow direction.
    • If electrons move from ring to substituent, it is usually \(-R\).
    • If electrons move from substituent to ring, it is usually \(+R\).
    • Here the \(\mathrm{-CHO}\) group is pulling electrons, so directly mark \((b)\ -R\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Confusing resonance effect with inductive effect.
    2. Marking carbonyl-containing groups as \(+R\). They generally show \(-R\) effect.
    3. Choosing electromeric effect just because curved arrows are shown.
    4. Ignoring the positive charge developed on the ring in the resonance form.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry - General Organic Chemistry
    • Topic: Electronic effects in organic compounds
    • Subtopic: Resonance effect / Mesomeric effect \((+R,\,-R)\)

    Final exam-style answer:

    In the given structure, electron displacement takes place from the benzene ring toward the \(\mathrm{-CHO}\) group by delocalisation. Hence the substituent withdraws electrons by resonance. Therefore, the effect observed is \(-R\) effect.

    \(\boxed{(b)\ -R\text{ effect}}\)

    Answer: \((d)\ \mathrm{CH_4}\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    We are told that alkene \(X\) with molecular formula \(\mathrm{C_4H_8}\) exhibits geometrical isomerism.

    1. Identify alkene \(X\)

      Among alkenes having formula \(\mathrm{C_4H_8}\), the one that shows geometrical isomerism is but-\(2\)-ene:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=CHCH_3}\)

      This is because each carbon of the double bond is attached to two different groups, so cis-trans isomerism is possible.

    2. Oxidation of but-\(2\)-ene

      On oxidation with \(\mathrm{KMnO_4/H^+}\), the double bond undergoes oxidative cleavage.

      But-\(2\)-ene is symmetrical, so cleavage gives two molecules of ethanoic acid:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=CHCH_3 \xrightarrow[\ [O]\ ]{KMnO_4/H^+} 2CH_3COOH}\)

      Therefore, \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3COOH}\).

    3. Formation of sodium salt of \(Y\)

      The sodium salt of ethanoic acid is sodium ethanoate:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_3COONa}\)

    4. Heating with soda lime

      When sodium carboxylates are heated with soda lime \(\mathrm{(NaOH + CaO)}\), decarboxylation occurs.

      General reaction:

      \(\mathrm{RCOONa \xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOH + CaO} RH + Na_2CO_3}\)

      Applying this here:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3COONa \xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOH + CaO} CH_4 + Na_2CO_3}\)

      Hence, \(Z = \mathrm{CH_4}\).



    Reaction flow:

    • \(\mathrm{X = CH_3CH=CHCH_3}\)
    • \(\mathrm{X \xrightarrow{KMnO_4/H^+} Y = 2CH_3COOH}\)
    • \(\mathrm{Y \rightarrow CH_3COONa}\)
    • \(\mathrm{CH_3COONa \xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOH + CaO} CH_4}\)

    Why this method works:

    • Alkenes with restricted rotation can show geometrical isomerism only when each double-bond carbon has two different substituents.
    • Hot acidic \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) causes oxidative cleavage of the \(\mathrm{C=C}\) bond.
    • Sodium salts of carboxylic acids on heating with soda lime lose \(\mathrm{CO_2}\) as \(\mathrm{Na_2CO_3}\), giving an alkane with one carbon less.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Geometrical isomerism in alkenes: possible when both carbons of \(\mathrm{C=C}\) carry two different groups.
    • But-\(2\)-ene: shows cis-trans isomerism.
    • But-\(1\)-ene: does not show geometrical isomerism because one double-bond carbon has two hydrogens.
    • Oxidative cleavage: strong oxidising agents like hot acidic \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) break the double bond and convert fragments into carbonyl compounds or acids depending on substitution.
    • Soda lime decarboxylation: \(\mathrm{RCOONa \xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOH + CaO} RH}\)
    • The product alkane always has one carbon less than the parent carboxylate chain.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • \(\mathrm{C_4H_8}\) alkene with geometrical isomerism \(\Rightarrow\) think directly of but-\(2\)-ene.
    • Symmetrical alkene on oxidative cleavage \(\Rightarrow\) same acid on both sides.
    • But-\(2\)-ene gives \(\mathrm{CH_3COOH}\).
    • \(\mathrm{CH_3COONa}\) with soda lime always gives \(\mathrm{CH_4}\).
    • So the answer can be marked very quickly as \((d)\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Choosing but-\(1\)-ene as \(X\). It does not show geometrical isomerism.
    2. Forgetting that hot acidic \(\mathrm{KMnO_4}\) causes cleavage of the double bond.
    3. Missing that but-\(2\)-ene is symmetrical, so both fragments are identical.
    4. Forgetting soda lime decarboxylation gives an alkane with one less carbon.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
    • Topic: Alkenes and their reactions
    • Subtopic: Geometrical isomerism, oxidative cleavage of alkenes, and soda lime decarboxylation

    Final exam-style answer:

    The alkene \(\mathrm{C_4H_8}\) that exhibits geometrical isomerism is but-\(2\)-ene, \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=CHCH_3}\).

    On oxidation with \(\mathrm{KMnO_4/H^+}\), it undergoes oxidative cleavage to give \(\mathrm{2CH_3COOH}\), so \(Y\) is ethanoic acid.

    Its sodium salt is \(\mathrm{CH_3COONa}\).

    On heating with soda lime:

    \(\mathrm{CH_3COONa \xrightarrow[\Delta]{NaOH + CaO} CH_4 + Na_2CO_3}\)

    Therefore, \(Z = \mathrm{CH_4}\).

    \(\boxed{(d)\ \mathrm{CH_4}}\)

    Answer: \((a)\ 2,\,3\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    We have to count how many of the given substituents are activating and how many are deactivating toward electrophilic substitution in benzene.

    The groups given are:

    \(\mathrm{-OCH_2CH_3,\ -COCH_3,\ -NHCOCH_3,\ -COOCH_3,\ -SO_3H}\)

    1. Core idea used in this question

      A group attached to benzene is:

      • Activating if it increases electron density in the ring, especially by \(+R\) or \(+M\) effect.
      • Deactivating if it withdraws electron density from the ring, especially by \(-R\) or \(-M\) effect.

      So this question is mainly about electronic effects of substituents on benzene.

    2. Identify the activating groups

      \(\mathrm{-OCH_2CH_3}\): Oxygen has a lone pair and donates electron density into the ring by resonance.

      So \(\mathrm{-OCH_2CH_3}\) shows \(+R\) effect and is activating.

      \(\mathrm{-NHCOCH_3}\): The nitrogen attached to the ring still has a lone pair that can participate in resonance with the benzene ring.

      So \(\mathrm{-NHCOCH_3}\) also shows \(+R\) effect and is activating.

      Therefore, activating groups are:

      • \(\mathrm{-OCH_2CH_3}\)
      • \(\mathrm{-NHCOCH_3}\)

      Number of activating groups \(= 2\).

    3. Identify the deactivating groups

      \(\mathrm{-COCH_3}\): Carbonyl group withdraws electron density from the ring by resonance \(( -R )\).

      So it is deactivating.

      \(\mathrm{-COOCH_3}\): Ester group also contains a carbonyl carbon directly connected to the ring side through the acyl system, so it withdraws electrons by resonance.

      So it is deactivating.

      \(\mathrm{-SO_3H}\): Sulphonic acid group strongly withdraws electron density by resonance and inductive effects.

      So it is deactivating.

      Therefore, deactivating groups are:

      • \(\mathrm{-COCH_3}\)
      • \(\mathrm{-COOCH_3}\)
      • \(\mathrm{-SO_3H}\)

      Number of deactivating groups \(= 3\).

    4. Final count

      Activating groups \(= 2\)

      Deactivating groups \(= 3\)

      Hence the correct answer is:

      \((a)\ 2,\,3\)



    Why this method works:

    • In electrophilic aromatic substitution, benzene reacts faster when the substituent donates electron density to the ring.
    • Groups with lone pairs directly connected through an atom attached to the ring often show \(+R\) effect and activate the ring.
    • Groups containing \(\mathrm{C=O}\), \(\mathrm{SO_3H}\), \(\mathrm{NO_2}\), \(\mathrm{CN}\) usually withdraw electron density by resonance and deactivate the ring.
    • So the quickest route is to classify each substituent as electron-donating or electron-withdrawing.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Activating groups: increase electron density in benzene and generally increase the rate of electrophilic substitution.
    • Deactivating groups: decrease electron density in benzene and generally decrease the rate of electrophilic substitution.
    • Groups like \(\mathrm{-OH}\), \(\mathrm{-OR}\), \(\mathrm{-NH_2}\), \(\mathrm{-NHR}\), \(\mathrm{-NHCOR}\) are usually activating because of \(+R\) effect.
    • Groups like \(\mathrm{-CHO}\), \(\mathrm{-COR}\), \(\mathrm{-COOH}\), \(\mathrm{-COOR}\), \(\mathrm{-SO_3H}\), \(\mathrm{-NO_2}\), \(\mathrm{-CN}\) are usually deactivating because of \(-R\) effect.
    • \(\mathrm{-NHCOCH_3}\) is less activating than \(\mathrm{-NH_2}\), but it is still activating.
    • \(\mathrm{-OCH_2CH_3}\) behaves like an alkoxy group \(\mathrm{-OR}\), so it is activating.

    Useful classification for 1-minute solving:

    • Lone pair donor attached to ring \(\Rightarrow\) usually activating
    • Carbonyl-containing withdrawing group \(\Rightarrow\) usually deactivating
    • \(\mathrm{-SO_3H}\) \(\Rightarrow\) strongly deactivating

    1-minute solving trick:

    • First look for atoms with lone pairs that can donate into the ring.
    • \(\mathrm{-OCH_2CH_3}\) and \(\mathrm{-NHCOCH_3}\) immediately go into the activating group list.
    • Then look for carbonyl-based and strongly electron-withdrawing groups.
    • \(\mathrm{-COCH_3}\), \(\mathrm{-COOCH_3}\), and \(\mathrm{-SO_3H}\) immediately go into the deactivating group list.
    • So without writing any resonance structures, you can directly count \(2\) activating and \(3\) deactivating.

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Thinking every group containing oxygen is deactivating. \(\mathrm{-OR}\) groups are actually activating because oxygen donates by resonance.
    2. Missing that \(\mathrm{-NHCOCH_3}\) still has a nitrogen lone pair available for resonance with the ring.
    3. Forgetting that carbonyl-containing substituents such as \(\mathrm{-COCH_3}\) and \(\mathrm{-COOCH_3}\) are deactivating.
    4. Confusing inductive effect with the dominant resonance effect in aromatic substitution questions.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Organic Chemistry - Aromatic Compounds / Haloalkanes, Arenes and Electrophilic Substitution concepts
    • Topic: Effect of substituents on benzene ring
    • Subtopic: Activating and deactivating groups; resonance effects in electrophilic aromatic substitution

    Final exam-style answer:

    Among the given groups, \(\mathrm{-OCH_2CH_3}\) and \(\mathrm{-NHCOCH_3}\) donate electron density to the benzene ring by resonance and are activating groups.

    The groups \(\mathrm{-COCH_3}\), \(\mathrm{-COOCH_3}\), and \(\mathrm{-SO_3H}\) withdraw electron density from the ring by resonance and are deactivating groups.

    Therefore, the number of activating and deactivating groups respectively is:

    \(\boxed{2,\,3}\)

    \(\boxed{(a)}\)

    Answer: \((c)\ \mathrm{HBr/ROOR}\) and cumene \(\big(\mathrm{isopropylbenzene}\big)\)


    Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR

    Solution:

    We are given the sequence:

    \(\mathrm{C_3H_6 \xrightarrow{X} Y \xrightarrow[\mathrm{AlCl_3}]{C_6H_6} Z}\)

    1. Identify \(\mathrm{C_3H_6}\)

      \(\mathrm{C_3H_6}\) here is propene:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH_2}\)

    2. Find reagent \(X\) that converts propene into a suitable alkyl halide \(Y\)

      In the next step, \(Y\) reacts with benzene in presence of \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\), so \(Y\) must be an alkyl halide suitable for Friedel-Crafts alkylation.

      If propene reacts with \(\mathrm{HBr}\) in the presence of peroxide \(\mathrm{(ROOR)}\), anti-Markovnikov addition takes place.

      This is called the Kharasch effect or peroxide effect.

      So:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=CH_2 \xrightarrow{HBr/ROOR} CH_3CH_2CH_2Br}\)

      Thus:

      \(X = \mathrm{HBr/ROOR}\)

      and \(Y = \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br}\) \(\big(\mathrm{1\text{-}bromopropane}\big)\).

    3. Now apply Friedel-Crafts alkylation

      \(\mathrm{1\text{-}bromopropane}\) reacts with benzene in presence of \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\).

      Although the alkyl halide is normal propyl bromide, the initially formed primary carbocation is unstable and rearranges to the more stable secondary carbocation:

      \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2^+ \rightarrow CH_3CH^+CH_3}\)

      This secondary carbocation then attacks benzene.

      Therefore the major product is:

      \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH(CH_3)_2}\)

      This compound is cumene or isopropylbenzene.

    4. Hence identify \(Z\)

      \(Z = \mathrm{isopropylbenzene}\) \(\big(\mathrm{cumene}\big)\).

      So the correct pair is:

      \((c)\ \mathrm{HBr/ROOR}\) and cumene.



    Compact reaction flow:

    • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=CH_2 \xrightarrow{HBr/ROOR} CH_3CH_2CH_2Br}\)
    • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br \xrightarrow[\mathrm{AlCl_3}]{C_6H_6} C_6H_5CH(CH_3)_2}\)
    • Major product \(= \mathrm{cumene}\)

    Why this method works:

    • \(\mathrm{HBr}\) in presence of peroxide adds to an unsymmetrical alkene by the free-radical mechanism.
    • Because of this, bromine goes to the carbon having more hydrogens, giving the anti-Markovnikov product.
    • In Friedel-Crafts alkylation, alkyl halides generate carbocation-like electrophiles in presence of \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\).
    • If a more stable carbocation can form by rearrangement, the reaction usually gives the product derived from the more stable rearranged carbocation.
    • So normal propyl halide often gives isopropylbenzene as the major product, not \(n\)-propylbenzene.

    JEE / NCERT takeaway:

    • Peroxide effect: shown only by \(\mathrm{HBr}\), not by \(\mathrm{HCl}\) or \(\mathrm{HI}\).
    • Without peroxide: \(\mathrm{HBr}\) adds by Markovnikov rule.
    • With peroxide: \(\mathrm{HBr}\) adds by anti-Markovnikov rule.
    • Friedel-Crafts alkylation: \(\mathrm{ArH + RCl \xrightarrow{AlCl_3} ArR}\)
    • Carbocation rearrangement: common in Friedel-Crafts alkylation when a more stable carbocation can form.
    • Cumene: \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH(CH_3)_2}\) is isopropylbenzene.

    Useful intuition for this question:

    • The second step uses benzene and \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\), so immediately think Friedel-Crafts alkylation.
    • That means \(Y\) should be an alkyl halide.
    • From propene, \(\mathrm{HBr/ROOR}\) gives \(\mathrm{1\text{-}bromopropane}\), while plain \(\mathrm{HBr}\) gives \(\mathrm{2\text{-}bromopropane}\).
    • Then remember: even \(n\)-propyl halide in Friedel-Crafts often gives cumene because of rearrangement.

    1-minute solving trick:

    • See \(\mathrm{C_6H_6/AlCl_3}\) \(\Rightarrow\) Friedel-Crafts alkylation.
    • Major product with propyl system is usually cumene, due to carbocation rearrangement.
    • Among the options, the one containing cumene is the likely answer.
    • That matches option \((c)\), where \(X = \mathrm{HBr/ROOR}\).

    Common mistakes to avoid:

    1. Assuming \(\mathrm{HBr}\) always gives Markovnikov product. In presence of peroxide, it gives anti-Markovnikov addition.
    2. Forgetting that peroxide effect is specific to \(\mathrm{HBr}\).
    3. Ignoring carbocation rearrangement in Friedel-Crafts alkylation.
    4. Choosing \(n\)-propylbenzene as major product directly from propyl halide.

    Chapter / Topic / Subtopic:

    • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
    • Topic: Reactions of alkenes and aromatic hydrocarbons
    • Subtopic: Peroxide effect of \(\mathrm{HBr}\), Friedel-Crafts alkylation, carbocation rearrangement

    Final exam-style answer:

    \(\mathrm{C_3H_6}\) is propene, \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=CH_2}\).

    With \(\mathrm{HBr/ROOR}\), it undergoes anti-Markovnikov addition to give \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br}\).

    This alkyl halide reacts with benzene in presence of \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) by Friedel-Crafts alkylation. During the reaction, rearrangement leads to formation of the more stable isopropyl carbocation, so the major product is cumene \(\big(\mathrm{isopropylbenzene}\big)\).

    Therefore:

    \(\boxed{X = \mathrm{HBr/ROOR},\quad Z = \mathrm{cumene}}\)

    \(\boxed{(c)}\)

    !--Start Q137-->
      • Correct answer: (c) 5-ethyl-9-methyldec-6-en-4-ol.
        Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      • Explanation: In IUPAC naming, the chain must be chosen so that the principal functional group gets the lowest possible number first. Here, the principal functional group is the alcohol group \((\mathrm{-OH})\), so numbering must start from the end nearer to \((\mathrm{-OH})\), not from the double-bond side.
      • Step-by-step explanation
        • First identify the longest carbon chain that contains both the alcohol group and the double bond. That chain has 10 carbons, so the parent word is dec.
        • Now number the chain from the end closest to the \((\mathrm{-OH})\) group because alcohol has higher naming priority than a double bond.
        • On numbering this way, the alcohol group comes at carbon 4, so we get dec-6-en-4-ol.
        • The double bond then lies between carbons 6 and 7, so it is written as 6-en.
        • Now identify the side chains: there is an ethyl substituent at carbon 5 and a methyl substituent at carbon 9.
        • Substituents are written in alphabetical order, so ethyl is written before methyl.
      • Concept used / theory behind it
        • This question belongs to IUPAC nomenclature of organic compounds, specifically naming of unsaturated alcohols.
        • When more than one feature is present, IUPAC priority rules are very important.
        • Among \((\mathrm{-OH})\) and \((\mathrm{C=C})\), the alcohol group is the principal functional group, so it gets the suffix ol and must receive the lowest locant.
        • After fixing the numbering based on the principal functional group, the position of the double bond and substituents is written.
        • If multiple substituents are present, their names are arranged alphabetically in the final name.
      • Why the correct option is right
        • Option (c) gives the alcohol the lower locant \((4)\), which is correct.
        • It also correctly places the double bond at carbon 6.
        • It lists the substituents as 5-ethyl and 9-methyl, and writes them in alphabetical order.
        • So the full correct name is 5-ethyl-9-methyldec-6-en-4-ol.
      • Why other options are wrong
        • Options (a) and (b) number the chain from the wrong side and place the alcohol at carbon 7, which violates the priority rule.
        • Option (d) has the correct numbering positions, but it writes methyl before ethyl, which is incorrect because substituents must be arranged alphabetically.
      • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
        • In nomenclature questions, first find the principal functional group before doing anything else.
        • If \((\mathrm{-OH})\), \((\mathrm{-CHO})\), \((\mathrm{-COOH})\), etc. are present along with double or triple bonds, always give the suffix functional group the lowest number first.
        • After that, check alphabetical order of substituents. Many MCQs are designed to trap students exactly here.
      • Final result
        • The IUPAC name is 5-ethyl-9-methyldec-6-en-4-ol.
      • Chapter / topic / subtopic
        • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
        • Topic: IUPAC Nomenclature
        • Subtopic: Naming of unsaturated alcohols with substituents
    • Correct answer: (b) \(\mathrm{-OH,\ -CO-}\).
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: The given compound is an aryl ester, and in the presence of \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) it undergoes Fries rearrangement. In this rearrangement, the acyl group migrates from oxygen to the benzene ring, producing a hydroxy aryl ketone. So the final product contains a phenolic \((\mathrm{-OH})\) group and a ketone carbonyl \((\mathrm{-CO-})\) group.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The starting compound is phenyl benzoate-type structure, where an acyl group is attached through oxygen to a benzene ring.
      • When such aryl esters are treated with a Lewis acid like \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\), they undergo Fries rearrangement.
      • In Fries rearrangement, the acyl group \((\mathrm{-COPh})\) shifts from the oxygen atom onto the aromatic ring.
      • As a result, the aromatic ring now gets two important groups:
        • a phenolic \((\mathrm{-OH})\) group
        • an acyl group directly attached to the ring, which behaves as a ketone \((\mathrm{-CO-})\)
      • Therefore, the product contains \(\mathrm{-OH}\) and \(\mathrm{-CO-}\).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This is a named reaction question from aromatic compounds.
      • Fries rearrangement converts phenolic esters into o- and p-hydroxy aryl ketones using \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\).
      • The bond between oxygen and acyl fragment is reorganized so that the acyl group finally attaches to the benzene ring.
      • Because the oxygen remains connected to the ring as \(\mathrm{-OH}\), the product is a phenol derivative.
      • Because the migrated acyl group is now directly attached to the ring carbon, the carbonyl behaves as a ketone functional group.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (b) correctly identifies the two functional groups formed after Fries rearrangement.
      • The product shown is hydroxy benzophenone-type, which clearly contains one \(\mathrm{-OH}\) group and one ketonic carbonyl \(\mathrm{-CO-}\).
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Option (a) is wrong because no aldehyde group \((\mathrm{-CHO})\) is formed.
      • Option (c) is wrong because the \(\mathrm{-O-ph}\) ester linkage does not remain in the final rearranged product.
      • Option (d) is wrong because no carboxylic acid group \((\mathrm{-COOH})\) is formed.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see an aryl ester plus \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\), immediately think of Fries rearrangement.
      • Then remember the standard product pattern: hydroxy aryl ketone.
      • So without doing the full mechanism, you can quickly mark \(\mathrm{-OH}\) and \(\mathrm{-CO-}\).
    • Why the rearrangement happens
      • \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) is a strong Lewis acid and coordinates with the carbonyl oxygen.
      • This makes the acyl part more reactive and helps generate an acylium-like electrophilic species.
      • That electrophilic acyl fragment attacks the aromatic ring, usually at ortho and para positions relative to oxygen.
      • After workup, the oxygen appears as phenolic \(\mathrm{-OH}\), and the acyl group remains on the ring as a ketone.
    • Final result
      • The functional groups present in product \(X\) are \(\mathrm{-OH}\) and \(\mathrm{-CO-}\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Aromatic Compounds / Ethers and Phenols
      • Subtopic: Fries rearrangement of aryl esters
    • Relevant answer images
    • Correct answer: (a) \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr}\).
      Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: Tert-butyl bromide first undergoes elimination with alcoholic \(\mathrm{KOH}\) on heating to form 2-methylpropene. Then \(\mathrm{HBr}\) adds across the double bond by Markovnikov addition, regenerating tert-butyl bromide as the major product.
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The starting compound is tert-butyl bromide, \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr}\).
      • Alcoholic \(\mathrm{KOH}\) with heat generally promotes elimination rather than substitution.
      • So tert-butyl bromide loses \(\mathrm{HBr}\) and forms the alkene:
        • \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr \rightarrow (CH_3)_2C=CH_2}\)
      • This alkene is 2-methylpropene.
      • Now \(\mathrm{HBr}\) is added to this alkene.
      • Since no peroxide is mentioned, the addition of \(\mathrm{HBr}\) follows normal Markovnikov rule.
      • Hydrogen adds to the carbon already having more hydrogens, and bromine adds to the more substituted carbon.
      • That gives:
        • \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_2C=CH_2 \xrightarrow{HBr} (CH_3)_3CBr}\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This question combines two standard organic concepts:
        • \(\beta\)-elimination using alcoholic \(\mathrm{KOH}\)
        • electrophilic addition of \(\mathrm{HBr}\) to an alkene
      • Tert-butyl halides easily undergo elimination under strong basic and heated conditions because a stable substituted alkene is formed.
      • Then alkenes react with hydrogen halides by addition across the double bond.
      • Without peroxide, \(\mathrm{HBr}\) adds by the ionic pathway and follows Markovnikov rule.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (a) matches the product obtained after elimination followed by Markovnikov addition.
      • The sequence takes tert-butyl bromide to 2-methylpropene and then back to tert-butyl bromide.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Option (b) is isobutyl bromide, which would require anti-Markovnikov type placement of bromine or a different skeleton outcome. That does not happen here.
      • Option (c) has four carbons in a straight-chain butyl bromide arrangement, which is not formed from this branched alkene by simple \(\mathrm{HBr}\) addition.
      • Option (d) is an unsaturated bromo compound and does not match the direct addition product of \(\mathrm{HBr}\) to 2-methylpropene.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see alcoholic \(\mathrm{KOH}\), think elimination.
      • Whenever you see \(\mathrm{HBr}\) added to an alkene and no peroxide is given, think Markovnikov addition.
      • In this question, the whole sequence is a neat reverse-and-return pattern: alkyl bromide \(\rightarrow\) alkene \(\rightarrow\) alkyl bromide again.
    • Final result
      • The major product \(P\) is \(\mathrm{(CH_3)_3CBr}\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Haloalkanes and Alkenes
      • Subtopic: Elimination with alcoholic \(\mathrm{KOH}\) and Markovnikov addition of \(\mathrm{HBr}\)
    • Correct answer: (c) 90.6.
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: Benzene reacts with ethyl chloride in the presence of anhydrous \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\) by Friedel-Crafts alkylation to form ethylbenzene. After finding the molecular formula of ethylbenzene, we calculate the mass percentage of carbon using \(\dfrac{\text{mass of carbon}}{\text{molar mass of compound}} \times 100\).
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The reaction is:
        • \(\mathrm{C_6H_6 + C_2H_5Cl \xrightarrow{anhyd.\ AlCl_3} C_6H_5C_2H_5 + HCl}\)
      • So product \(X\) is ethylbenzene.
      • Its molecular formula is:
        • \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH_2CH_3 = C_8H_{10}}\)
      • Now calculate molar mass of ethylbenzene:
        • Mass of 8 carbon atoms \(= 8 \times 12 = 96\)
        • Mass of 10 hydrogen atoms \(= 10 \times 1 = 10\)
        • Total molar mass \(= 96 + 10 = 106\)
      • Percentage of carbon:
        • \(\dfrac{96}{106} \times 100 = 90.6\%\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This is a combined organic plus basic composition calculation question.
      • The organic concept is Friedel-Crafts alkylation, where an alkyl group is introduced into benzene using an alkyl halide and anhydrous \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\).
      • Once the product is known, the numerical part is simple percentage composition by mass.
      • The general formula used is:
        • \(\mathrm{\% \ element = \dfrac{mass\ of\ that\ element\ in\ one\ mole\ of\ compound}{molar\ mass\ of\ compound} \times 100}\)
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (c) matches the calculated carbon percentage in ethylbenzene.
      • \(\mathrm{C_8H_{10}}\) contains 96 units of carbon mass out of total 106, giving \(90.6\%\).
    • Why other options are wrong
      • The other options arise from either choosing the wrong product, writing the wrong molecular formula, or making an arithmetic mistake in molar-mass calculation.
      • A common mistake is forgetting that benzene becomes ethylbenzene, not a chlorinated product.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever benzene reacts with an alkyl halide in presence of anhydrous \(\mathrm{AlCl_3}\), first identify it as Friedel-Crafts alkylation.
      • Then write the product formula before doing any percentage calculation.
      • For hydrocarbons rich in carbon like ethylbenzene, the carbon percentage will be quite high, so an answer near 90 is reasonable.
    • Final result
      • The percentage of carbon in product \(X\) is 90.6%.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Aromatic Hydrocarbons
      • Subtopic: Friedel-Crafts alkylation and percentage composition
    • Correct answer: (d) II > III > IV > I
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: The main factor controlling the boiling points here is intermolecular force. Alcohols have hydrogen bonding, so they boil at much higher temperatures than ether and alkane. Between the two alcohols, the less branched one has a higher boiling point. Between pentane and diethyl ether, pentane has the slightly higher boiling point here. Hence the order is II > III > IV > I.
    • Boiling point comparison of the given compounds:
      • The image helps identify the same final order: II > III > IV > I.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Let us identify the four compounds first:
        • I \(=\) diethyl ether, \((C_2H_5)_2O\)
        • II \(=\) \(n\)-butyl alcohol, \(CH_3(CH_2)_3OH\)
        • III \(=\) isobutyl alcohol, \(CH_3CH(CH_3)CH_2OH\)
        • IV \(=\) \(n\)-pentane, \(CH_3(CH_2)_3CH_3\)
      • Alcohols have hydrogen bonding, so both II and III must have higher boiling points than I and IV.
      • Now compare the two alcohols.
      • II is a straight-chain alcohol, while III is branched.
      • Branching reduces surface area and weakens intermolecular attraction, so: II > III.
      • Now compare I and IV.
      • I is an ether, so it has dipole-dipole interactions but no hydrogen bonding.
      • IV is pentane, which is non-polar, but it has a larger molecular size and stronger dispersion forces than diethyl ether in this comparison.
      • So: IV > I.
      • Combining all results: II > III > IV > I.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Boiling point depends mainly on the strength of intermolecular forces.
      • Common order of intermolecular force strength here is: hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > dispersion, though molecular size also matters.
      • Alcohols show hydrogen bonding because of the \(O-H\) bond.
      • Ethers do not show intermolecular hydrogen bonding among themselves, so their boiling points are lower than those of comparable alcohols.
      • Among isomeric alcohols, increased branching usually lowers boiling point.
      • Among hydrocarbons, higher molecular mass and larger surface area generally increase boiling point.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) matches all the correct pairwise comparisons:
        • II > III because straight-chain alcohol has stronger intermolecular attraction than branched alcohol.
        • III > IV because alcohol has hydrogen bonding but alkane does not.
        • IV > I in this set because pentane has the slightly higher boiling point.
      • So option (d) is the correct overall order.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) puts ether at the highest boiling point, which is impossible here.
      • (b) incorrectly places I above III.
      • (c) incorrectly places branched alcohol above straight-chain alcohol.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For such questions, first separate the compounds by functional group:
        • alcohols highest
        • ethers and alkanes lower
      • Then use branching rule: straight chain > branched for boiling point.
      • That quickly gives II > III at the top.
    • Final result:
      • The correct order of boiling points is II > III > IV > I.
      • Hence, the correct answer is (d).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry - Some Basic Principles / Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Topic: Intermolecular forces and physical properties
      • Subtopic: Boiling point comparison of alcohols, ethers and hydrocarbons
    • Correct answer: (c) 2-bromo -3-methylbutane
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Acetone reacts with ethylmagnesium bromide followed by hydrolysis to give a tertiary alcohol \(X\). Conversion with \(SOCl_2\) followed by elimination using \(CH_3ONa\) gives the alkene \(Y\), namely \(2\)-methyl-\(2\)-butene. Addition of HBr in the presence of peroxide takes place by the anti-Markovnikov radical pathway, leading to \(2\)-bromo-\(3\)-methylbutane as the major product \(Z\).
    • Reaction sequence and product flow:
      • The image shows the same pathway: acetone \(\rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) alkene \(\rightarrow\) bromoalkane.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting compound is acetone, \((CH_3)_2CO\).
      • Grignard reagent \(C_2H_5MgBr\) adds an ethyl group to the carbonyl carbon.
      • After acidic work-up, we get the tertiary alcohol: \(X = (CH_3)_2C(OH)C_2H_5\)
      • This is \(2\)-methyl-\(2\)-butanol.
      • Now \(SOCl_2\) converts the alcohol into the corresponding chloride.
      • Treatment with \(CH_3ONa\) and heat gives elimination, forming the more substituted alkene: \(Y = CH_3-C(CH_3)=CH-CH_3\)
      • So \(Y\) is \(2\)-methyl-\(2\)-butene.
      • Now HBr is added in the presence of peroxide.
      • Peroxide causes free-radical anti-Markovnikov addition of HBr.
      • Therefore bromine attaches to the less substituted carbon of the double bond.
      • The product becomes: \(CH_3-CH(CH_3)-CH(Br)-CH_3\)
      • This is named \(2\)-bromo-\(3\)-methylbutane.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Step 1: Grignard addition
        • Grignard reagents add carbon groups to carbonyl compounds.
        • Ketone + Grignard reagent \(\rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol after hydrolysis.
      • Step 2: Elimination
        • Alcohol is converted to a better leaving group, then base removes \(HX\) to form alkene.
        • More substituted alkene is usually favored.
      • Step 3: Peroxide effect
        • HBr in the presence of peroxide follows the radical mechanism.
        • This gives anti-Markovnikov addition.
        • This effect is characteristic for HBr, not generally for HCl or HI.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Only option (c) matches the product obtained by anti-Markovnikov addition of HBr to \(2\)-methyl-\(2\)-butene.
      • The final structure corresponds to \(2\)-bromo-\(3\)-methylbutane.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) is an unsaturated bromo compound and does not match the addition product.
      • (b) is an ether, not formed anywhere in this sequence.
      • (d) places bromine at the wrong carbon position.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever you see:
        • Grignard + ketone \(\rightarrow\) alcohol
        • \(SOCl_2\), base, \(\Delta\) \(\rightarrow\) alkene
        • \(HBr\) + peroxide \(\rightarrow\) anti-Markovnikov addition
      • you can solve the whole sequence stepwise without memorising the exact final structure in advance.
      • Also remember: peroxide effect is most commonly used with HBr.
    • Final result:
      • The major product \(Z\) is \(2\)-bromo-\(3\)-methylbutane.
      • Hence, the correct answer is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids / Hydrocarbons
      • Topic: Grignard reaction, elimination, peroxide effect
      • Subtopic: Multi-step conversion from ketone to alkene to bromoalkane
    • Correct answer: (d) \( \mathrm{Na_3PO_4,\ (NH_4)_3PO_4 \cdot 12MoO_3} \)
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: On oxidation with \( \mathrm{Na_2O_2} \), an organic phosphorus compound is converted into sodium phosphate, \( \mathrm{Na_3PO_4} \). On acidification with \( \mathrm{HNO_3} \), this gives phosphoric acid, and with ammonium molybdate in nitric acid medium it produces the characteristic yellow precipitate of ammonium phosphomolybdate, \( \mathrm{(NH_4)_3PO_4 \cdot 12MoO_3} \).
    • Phosphate test sequence
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The question is based on the qualitative test for phosphorus in an organic compound.
      • When an organic compound containing phosphorus is strongly oxidised with \( \mathrm{Na_2O_2} \), the phosphorus present in it is converted into phosphate.
      • That phosphate is obtained as \( \mathrm{Na_3PO_4} \). So, \( \mathrm{X = Na_3PO_4} \).
      • Now this \( \mathrm{Na_3PO_4} \) is boiled with nitric acid:
        • \( \mathrm{Na_3PO_4 + 3HNO_3 \rightarrow H_3PO_4 + 3NaNO_3} \)
      • So in acidic medium, phosphate is effectively converted to phosphoric acid.
      • Next, this is treated with ammonium molybdate in the presence of nitric acid.
      • Phosphoric acid gives a yellow precipitate of ammonium phosphomolybdate:
        • \( \mathrm{H_3PO_4 + 12(NH_4)_2MoO_4 + 21HNO_3 \rightarrow (NH_4)_3PO_4 \cdot 12MoO_3 \downarrow + 21NH_4NO_3 + 12H_2O} \)
      • Thus, \( \mathrm{Y = (NH_4)_3PO_4 \cdot 12MoO_3} \).
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This is from the elemental analysis of organic compounds.
      • In Lassaigne-type analysis or related detection methods, heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulphur, halogens and phosphorus are converted into inorganic ionic forms that can be tested easily.
      • Phosphorus is converted to phosphate on oxidation.
      • Phosphate is then identified by the classic ammonium molybdate test in nitric acid medium, which gives a canary yellow precipitate.
      • This yellow precipitate is a very standard confirmatory test for phosphate or phosphorus-containing compounds after oxidation.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (d) correctly gives:
        • \( \mathrm{X = Na_3PO_4} \)
        • \( \mathrm{Y = (NH_4)_3PO_4 \cdot 12MoO_3} \)
      • This matches the standard phosphorus detection sequence exactly.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) is wrong because ammonium molybdate itself is not the yellow precipitate; it is the reagent used for the test.
      • (b) is wrong for the same reason. \( \mathrm{(NH_4)_2MoO_4} \) is the reagent, not the product Y.
      • (c) is wrong because \( \mathrm{H_3PO_4} \) is formed after treatment with \( \mathrm{HNO_3} \), but X is the product obtained directly after oxidation with \( \mathrm{Na_2O_2} \), which is sodium phosphate.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Memorise this directly:
        • Phosphorus \( \rightarrow \) phosphate \( \rightarrow \) ammonium molybdate test \( \rightarrow \) yellow ammonium phosphomolybdate ppt
      • If the question mentions yellow precipitate with ammonium molybdate in nitric acid, immediately think phosphate.
      • Also remember that the yellow precipitate is not ammonium molybdate itself; it is a separate phosphate-containing complex.
    • Final result: \( \mathrm{X = Na_3PO_4} \) and \( \mathrm{Y = (NH_4)_3PO_4 \cdot 12MoO_3} \), so the correct option is (d).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Organic Chemistry / Detection of elements in organic compounds / Test for phosphorus
    • Correct answer: (c) 5-amino-1-hydroxy-3-methylhex-3-en-2-one
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: The structure contains ketone, double bond, hydroxyl and amino groups. Among these, ketone gets higher priority than alcohol and amine, so the parent name is written as an enone. Numbering is done from the end nearer the carbonyl group, giving the name \( \mathrm{5\text{-}amino\text{-}1\text{-}hydroxy\text{-}3\text{-}methylhex\text{-}3\text{-}en\text{-}2\text{-}one} \).
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • First identify the longest carbon chain containing the highest priority functional group and the double bond.
      • The longest suitable chain has six carbons, so the parent word is hex-.
      • The structure also contains:
        • one ketone group \( \mathrm{(>C=O)} \),
        • one double bond,
        • one hydroxyl group \( \mathrm{(-OH)} \),
        • one amino group \( \mathrm{(-NH_2)} \),
        • one methyl substituent.
      • Now decide the principal functional group. Priority order tells us:
        • ketone has higher priority than alcohol and amine.
      • So the suffix will be based on ketone, not alcohol or amine.
      • Hence the parent will be an en-one system.
      • Now numbering should give the ketone the lowest possible locant.
      • When numbered from the end nearer the carbonyl:
        • carbonyl becomes at carbon 2,
        • double bond lies at carbon 3,
        • methyl substituent is at carbon 3,
        • hydroxy group is at carbon 1,
        • amino group is at carbon 5.
      • So the name becomes:
        • 5-amino-1-hydroxy-3-methylhex-3-en-2-one
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • IUPAC naming depends on:
        • choosing the correct parent chain,
        • identifying the principal functional group,
        • giving lowest number to the principal functional group,
        • then locating multiple bond and substituents.
      • Functional group priority is crucial here.
      • Since ketone outranks alcohol and amine, the suffix is -one, while hydroxy and amino are written as prefixes.
      • The double bond is included in the parent as -en-.
      • This is why the name is not written as an alcohol-based or amine-based parent compound.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (c) is the only one that correctly:
        • chooses ketone as the principal functional group,
        • numbers the chain from the carbonyl end,
        • places the double bond at 3,
        • puts hydroxy and amino as prefixes,
        • and includes the methyl substituent correctly.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (a) is not the preferred IUPAC style here because it treats the ketone as an oxo substituent and alcohol as the suffix, even though ketone has higher priority.
      • (b) is completely wrong because the given compound is neither a pentanoic acid nor does it contain a carboxylic acid group.
      • (d) is not the correct IUPAC format. Expressions like “keto” and “hydroxy” are not being used properly here with respect to principal functional group priority.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • In mixed functional group naming, do this in order:
        • find the highest priority group,
        • give it the suffix,
        • number from that side,
        • write all lower-priority groups as prefixes.
      • Here, the moment you see ketone + alcohol + amine, you should know:
        • ketone wins
      • That immediately eliminates options that use alcohol as the main suffix.
    • Final result: The correct IUPAC name is 5-amino-1-hydroxy-3-methylhex-3-en-2-one, so the answer is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Organic Chemistry / IUPAC nomenclature / Functional group priority and naming of multifunctional compounds
    • Correct answer: (a) \( \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br} \)
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: \( \mathrm{C_3H_7OH} \) on dehydration with concentrated \( \mathrm{H_2SO_4} \) at \( \mathrm{443\,K} \) gives propene. Addition of \( \mathrm{HBr} \) in the presence of peroxide follows the anti-Markovnikov route, so bromine attaches to the terminal carbon, giving \( \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br} \).
    • Reaction sequence
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The given alcohol is \( \mathrm{C_3H_7OH} \), which here behaves as propanol.
      • On heating with concentrated \( \mathrm{H_2SO_4} \) at \( \mathrm{443\,K} \), alcohol undergoes dehydration to form an alkene.
      • Thus:
        • \( \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2OH \xrightarrow[\ 443\,K\ ]{Conc.H_2SO_4} CH_3CH=CH_2 + H_2O} \)
      • So, \( \mathrm{X = CH_3CH=CH_2} \), which is propene.
      • Next, propene reacts with \( \mathrm{HBr} \) in the presence of peroxide \( \mathrm{((C_6H_5CO)_2O_2)} \).
      • This is the famous peroxide effect or Kharasch effect.
      • In the presence of peroxide, \( \mathrm{HBr} \) adds to an unsymmetrical alkene by a free-radical mechanism and gives the anti-Markovnikov product.
      • So bromine goes to the less substituted carbon:
        • \( \mathrm{CH_3CH=CH_2 \xrightarrow[\ peroxide\ ]{HBr} CH_3CH_2CH_2Br} \)
      • Therefore \( \mathrm{Y} \) is 1-bromopropane.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Alcohols undergo elimination on strong acid heating to form alkenes.
      • For primary alcohols, dehydration under these conditions gives the corresponding alkene.
      • Normally, \( \mathrm{HBr} \) adds to alkenes by Markovnikov addition.
      • But in the presence of peroxide, only \( \mathrm{HBr} \) shows the peroxide effect and follows a radical pathway.
      • That radical pathway gives the anti-Markovnikov product.
      • This is an extremely common exam concept.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (a) is correct because the sequence is:
        • propanol \( \rightarrow \) propene
        • propene \( \xrightarrow{HBr/peroxide} \) 1-bromopropane
      • This gives \( \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br} \).
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable
      • (b) \( \mathrm{CH_3CH(Br)CH_3} \) would be the Markovnikov product, formed if peroxide were absent.
      • (c) \( \mathrm{CH_3COC_6H_5} \) is unrelated to this reaction sequence.
      • (d) \( \mathrm{C_6H_5COBr} \) is benzoyl bromide, also unrelated as a product here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see:
        • \( \mathrm{HBr + peroxide} \)
      • immediately think:
        • anti-Markovnikov addition
      • And remember:
        • \( \mathrm{HCl} \) and \( \mathrm{HI} \) do not normally show this effect in standard exam chemistry.
      • This one clue is enough to avoid choosing option (b).
    • Final result: The major product \( \mathrm{Y} \) is \( \mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2Br} \), so the correct option is (a).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Organic Chemistry / Alcohols and Hydrocarbons / Dehydration of alcohols and peroxide effect of HBr
    • Correct answer: (c) \(5\)
      Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: The molecular formula \(C_4H_9Br\) gives four structural isomers of bromobutane. Among them, 2-bromobutane is chiral and exists as two stereoisomers \((d\) and \(l)\). So total isomers including stereoisomers \(= 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 5\).
    • Isomers of \(C_4H_9Br\) including the chiral pair
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • First, write all possible structural isomers for \(C_4H_9Br\).
      • These are:
        • 1-bromobutane
        • 2-bromobutane
        • 1-bromo-2-methylpropane
        • 2-bromo-2-methylpropane
      • So structurally there are 4 isomers.
      • Now check for stereoisomerism.
      • 2-bromobutane has one chiral carbon because the carbon attached to Br is connected to four different groups:
        • \(\mathrm{Br}\)
        • \(\mathrm{H}\)
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3}\)
        • \(\mathrm{C_2H_5}\)
      • Therefore 2-bromobutane exists as two enantiomers:
        • \(d\)-form
        • \(l\)-form
      • The other three structures are achiral, so each counts only once.
      • Total isomers including stereoisomers:
        • 1-bromobutane \(\rightarrow 1\)
        • 2-bromobutane \(\rightarrow 2\)
        • 1-bromo-2-methylpropane \(\rightarrow 1\)
        • 2-bromo-2-methylpropane \(\rightarrow 1\)
      • Hence total \(= 5\).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Structural isomers have the same molecular formula but different connectivity.
      • Stereoisomers have the same connectivity but differ in spatial arrangement.
      • An organic compound shows optical isomerism if it contains a chiral carbon attached to four different groups.
      • In haloalkanes, this is a very common exam pattern:
        • first count chain and position isomers
        • then check whether any one of them is chiral
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • There are 4 structural isomers, but one of them, 2-bromobutane, has 2 optical forms.
      • That raises the total count to 5.
      • So option (c) is correct.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • (a) 3 is too small because even structural isomers alone are 4.
      • (b) 4 ignores the optical isomerism of 2-bromobutane.
      • (d) 2 is far too small and misses both position and chain isomerism.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For questions saying including stereoisomers, never stop after counting structural isomers.
      • Always inspect each structure for a chiral center.
      • Here the key trap is 2-bromobutane.
    • Final result: The possible number of isomers including stereoisomers for \(C_4H_9Br\) is \(5\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic: Organic Chemistry / Isomerism / Structural and optical isomerism in haloalkanes
    • Correct answer: (a) I, IV only
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: The alkane next to methane in the homologous series is ethane. Reaction I gives ethane by Wurtz reaction of methyl bromide. Reaction IV gives ethane by reduction of ethyl bromide. Reactions II and III do not produce ethane.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The homologous series of alkanes begins:
        • methane \((\mathrm{CH_4})\)
        • ethane \((\mathrm{C_2H_6})\)
        • propane \((\mathrm{C_3H_8})\)
      • So the alkane next to methane is ethane.
      • Now test each reaction.
      • Reaction I:
        • \(2\mathrm{CH_3Br} \xrightarrow[\text{dry ether}]{2\mathrm{Na}} \mathrm{CH_3-CH_3} + 2\mathrm{NaBr}\)
        • This is Wurtz reaction.
        • Product is ethane.
        • So I is correct.
      • Reaction II:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3COOH}\) with soda lime \((\mathrm{NaOH/CaO})\) gives methane by decarboxylation, not ethane.
        • So II is incorrect.
      • Reaction III:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH{=}CH_2}\) on hydrogenation gives propane \((\mathrm{C_3H_8})\), not ethane.
        • So III is incorrect.
      • Reaction IV:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2Br}\) on reduction gives \(\mathrm{C_2H_6}\).
        • So IV is correct.
      • Hence the correct set is I and IV only.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
