On hydration with \(\mathrm{H_2O/H^+}\), it gives butan-2-ol, and on passing its vapours over Cu at \(573\ \mathrm{K}\), the secondary alcohol is dehydrogenated to butan-2-one. Since butan-2-one is a methyl ketone \((\mathrm{CH_3CO-})\), it gives the iodoform test. Hence option (c) is correct.
    • Conversion of \(X\) into the iodoform-positive product \(Y\)
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • First identify the alkene \(X\).
      • The molecular formula is \(\mathrm{C_4H_8}\), so possible alkenes are:
        • but-1-ene \(\mathrm{(CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3)}\)
        • but-2-ene \(\mathrm{(CH_3CH{=}CHCH_3)}\)
        • 2-methylpropene \(\mathrm{((CH_3)_2C{=}CH_2)}\)
      • The question says \(X\) does not exhibit geometrical isomerism.
      • But-2-ene shows cis-trans isomerism, so it is ruled out.
      • From the attached solution and the reaction flow, the intended alkene is but-1-ene:
        • \(\mathrm{X = CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3}\)
      • Now test the reagent sequences.
      • Option (c): \(\mathrm{H_2O/H^+}\) followed by \(\mathrm{Cu/573\ K}\)
        • Acid-catalysed hydration of but-1-ene follows Markovnikov addition.
        • So:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{H_2O/H^+} CH_3CH(OH)CH_2CH_3}\)
        • The product is butan-2-ol, a secondary alcohol.
        • Secondary alcohols on heating with copper at \(573\ \mathrm{K}\) undergo dehydrogenation to ketones.
        • Thus:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)CH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{Cu/573\,K} CH_3COCH_2CH_3}\)
        • The final product \(Y\) is butan-2-one.
        • Butan-2-one contains the \(\mathrm{CH_3CO-}\) group, so it gives the iodoform test.
      • Why the other options fail
        • Option (a): Hydroboration-oxidation gives butan-1-ol from but-1-ene. Oxidation with PCC converts primary alcohol into butanal:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{BH_3,\ H_2O_2/OH^-} CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2OH \xrightarrow{PCC} CH_3CH_2CH_2CHO}\)
        • Butanal does not give the iodoform test.
        • Option (b): Hydration gives butan-2-ol, but \(\mathrm{ZnCl_2/HCl}\) converts alcohol into alkyl chloride:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)CH_2CH_3 \xrightarrow{ZnCl_2/HCl} CH_3CHClCH_2CH_3}\)
        • An alkyl chloride does not give the iodoform test.
        • Option (d): Hydroboration-oxidation again gives butan-1-ol, and Cu at \(573\ \mathrm{K}\) converts a primary alcohol into an aldehyde:
          • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CH_2CH_2OH \xrightarrow{Cu/573\,K} CH_3CH_2CH_2CHO}\)
        • Butanal does not give the iodoform test.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Iodoform test is given by:
        • methyl ketones \(\mathrm{(RCOCH_3)}\)
        • and alcohols of the type \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)R}\), because they can be oxidised to methyl ketones
      • Acid-catalysed hydration of an unsymmetrical alkene follows Markovnikov’s rule.
      • Hydroboration-oxidation follows anti-Markovnikov addition and gives the alcohol at the less substituted carbon.
      • Alcohols with Cu at \(573\ \mathrm{K}\):
        • primary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) aldehyde
        • secondary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) ketone
        • tertiary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) generally dehydration
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Only option (c) converts but-1-ene into butan-2-one.
      • Butan-2-one is a methyl ketone.
      • Therefore \(Y\) gives the iodoform test.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Options (a) and (d) give butanal, not a methyl ketone.
      • Option (b) gives an alkyl chloride, which does not satisfy the iodoform clue.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see “gives iodoform test,” quickly look for formation of:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3CO-}\) group, or
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3CH(OH)-}\) group
      • Here, the fastest route is:
        • alkene \(\rightarrow\) secondary alcohol \(\rightarrow\) methyl ketone
      • That immediately points to hydration followed by Cu/\(573\ \mathrm{K}\).
    • Final result
      • \(\mathrm{X = CH_2{=}CHCH_2CH_3}\)
      • \(\mathrm{Y = CH_3COCH_2CH_3}\), which gives the iodoform test.
      • So the correct sequence is (c) \(\mathrm{H_2O/H^+ ,\ Cu/573\ K}\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers / Aldehydes and Ketones
      • Topic: Alkene reactions and functional group interconversion
      • Subtopic: Hydration of alkenes, dehydrogenation of alcohols, iodoform test
    • Correct answer: (d) 2-ethyl-5-hydroxy-3-methylhept-3-enal
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: The compound contains an aldehyde group, so the parent chain must include the carbonyl carbon of the \(-CHO\) group and numbering must start from that carbon. After choosing the longest chain containing the aldehyde carbon and the double bond, the name becomes 2-ethyl-5-hydroxy-3-methylhept-3-enal.
    • Structure used for naming
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The highest priority functional group here is the aldehyde group \((-CHO)\).
      • So the carbon of the aldehyde must be carbon number 1.
      • Now choose the longest chain that includes:
        • the aldehyde carbon,
        • the carbon-carbon double bond, and
        • the maximum possible number of carbons.
      • That parent chain has 7 carbons, so the base name is hept.
      • The double bond starts at carbon 3, so the parent becomes hept-3-enal.
      • Now identify the substituents on this parent chain:
        • At carbon 2, there is an ethyl group.
        • At carbon 3, there is a methyl group.
        • At carbon 5, there is a hydroxy group.
      • Now arrange the substituents in alphabetical order: ethyl, hydroxy, methyl.
      • So the full IUPAC name is 2-ethyl-5-hydroxy-3-methylhept-3-enal.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • In IUPAC nomenclature, the principal functional group gets the suffix and also gets the lowest possible number.
      • Aldehyde has higher priority than alcohol and double bond for suffix naming.
      • Therefore:
        • \(-CHO\) is written as the suffix -al,
        • \(-OH\) is written as the prefix hydroxy,
        • the double bond is written as -en-.
      • The aldehyde carbon is always part of the parent chain and is numbered as carbon 1.
      • Substituent names are written in alphabetical order in the final name.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (d) correctly uses:
        • a 7-carbon parent chain,
        • aldehyde suffix as enal,
        • double bond at carbon 3,
        • ethyl at carbon 2,
        • hydroxy at carbon 5,
        • methyl at carbon 3.
      • It also lists the prefixes in the correct alphabetical order.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Option (a) is wrong because it treats the aldehyde group as a formyl substituent instead of the principal functional group. That is not correct here.
      • Option (b) is wrong because the positions of substituents are not assigned correctly after proper numbering from the aldehyde carbon.
      • Option (c) is wrong because although some locants look close, the substituent order is not alphabetical.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see \(-CHO\), start numbering from that carbon first. This avoids most naming mistakes.
      • After fixing the parent chain, check three things in order:
        • chain length,
        • position of multiple bond,
        • positions and alphabetical order of substituents.
      • In such questions, many wrong options are designed to trap students on alphabetical order or wrong parent-chain choice.
    • Final result
      • The IUPAC name is 2-ethyl-5-hydroxy-3-methylhept-3-enal.
      • Therefore, the correct answer is (d).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – IUPAC Nomenclature
      • Topic: Naming of polyfunctional organic compounds
      • Subtopic: Priority order, parent-chain selection and substituent numbering
    • Correct answer: (a) phenanthrene
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: Among the given aromatic species, phenanthrene contains the maximum number of \(\pi\)-electrons. Phenanthrene has 14 \(\pi\)-electrons, naphthalene has 10 \(\pi\)-electrons, and both cyclopentadienyl anion and cycloheptatrienyl cation have 6 \(\pi\)-electrons each.
    • \(\pi\)-electron comparison of the given aromatic species
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • This question is not asking which is aromatic. All the listed species are aromatic.
      • It is asking which one has the maximum number of \(\pi\)-electrons.
      • So simply count the total aromatic \(\pi\)-electrons in each species.
      • Phenanthrene: It is a fused three-ring aromatic system and contains 14 \(\pi\)-electrons.
      • Naphthalene: It is a fused two-ring aromatic system and contains 10 \(\pi\)-electrons.
      • Cyclopentadienyl anion: It has two double bonds giving 4 \(\pi\)-electrons, and the lone pair on the negatively charged carbon contributes 2 more, so total = 6 \(\pi\)-electrons.
      • Cycloheptatrienyl cation: Also called tropylium cation, it has three double bonds giving 6 \(\pi\)-electrons.
      • Now compare: \(14 > 10 > 6\).
      • Therefore, phenanthrene has the maximum number of \(\pi\)-electrons.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Aromatic compounds are cyclic, planar, conjugated systems that follow Hückel’s rule: \(4n+2\) \(\pi\)-electrons.
      • For each option:
        • Phenanthrene: \(14 = 4n+2\) with \(n=3\)
        • Naphthalene: \(10 = 4n+2\) with \(n=2\)
        • Cyclopentadienyl anion: \(6 = 4n+2\) with \(n=1\)
        • Cycloheptatrienyl cation: \(6 = 4n+2\) with \(n=1\)
      • All are aromatic, but the total \(\pi\)-electron count is largest in phenanthrene.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (a) is correct because phenanthrene contains 14 \(\pi\)-electrons, which is the highest among the given choices.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Option (b) is wrong because naphthalene has only 10 \(\pi\)-electrons, which is less than phenanthrene.
      • Option (c) is wrong because cyclopentadienyl anion has 6 \(\pi\)-electrons only.
      • Option (d) is wrong because cycloheptatrienyl cation also has 6 \(\pi\)-electrons only.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • In fused aromatic hydrocarbons, more fused benzene-like rings usually mean a larger total \(\pi\)-electron count.
      • Quick memory pattern:
        • benzene = 6
        • naphthalene = 10
        • anthracene / phenanthrene = 14
      • Also remember the classic aromatic ions:
        • cyclopentadienyl anion = 6
        • tropylium cation = 6
    • Final result
      • The aromatic compound/species with maximum number of \(\pi\)-electrons is phenanthrene.
      • Therefore, the correct answer is (a).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry – Aromatic Compounds
      • Topic: Aromaticity
      • Subtopic: Hückel rule and \(\pi\)-electron counting
    • Correct answer: (d) \(CH_3CO^+\)
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: Ethyne first trimerises in a red hot iron tube to form benzene. Then benzene reacts with \(CH_3COCl\) in presence of anhydrous \(AlCl_3\) by Friedel–Crafts acylation. In this reaction, the attacking electrophile is the acylium ion \(CH_3CO^+\).
    • Reaction sequence used in the question
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • The first step is \(C_2H_2\) passed through a red hot iron tube at 873 K.
      • Three molecules of ethyne combine to give benzene.
      • The reaction is: \(3CH \equiv CH \rightarrow C_6H_6\)
      • So compound A is benzene.
      • In the next step, benzene reacts with acetyl chloride \((CH_3COCl)\) in presence of anhydrous \(AlCl_3\).
      • This is the standard Friedel–Crafts acylation reaction.
      • \(AlCl_3\) acts as a Lewis acid and helps generate the electrophile from acetyl chloride.
      • The electrophile formed is the acylium ion: \(CH_3CO^+\)
      • This electrophile attacks the benzene ring and finally gives acetophenone as product B.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • This question combines two standard concepts:
        • polymerisation / trimerisation of ethyne to benzene,
        • electrophilic aromatic substitution through Friedel–Crafts acylation.
      • In Friedel–Crafts acylation, an acyl halide reacts with \(AlCl_3\) to produce an acylium ion.
      • For acetyl chloride:
        • \(CH_3COCl + AlCl_3 \rightarrow CH_3CO^+ + AlCl_4^-\)
      • The ion \(CH_3CO^+\) is resonance-stabilised and therefore acts as the electrophile.
      • Its resonance forms are \(CH_3-C \equiv O^+\) and \(CH_3-C^+=O\).
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (d) is correct because the reagent used is acetyl chloride, not methyl chloride.
      • So the electrophile must be the acetyl cation / acylium ion \(CH_3CO^+\), not \(CH_3^+\).
      • This is exactly the electrophile involved in acylation of benzene.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Option (a) \(CH_3^+\) would be related to Friedel–Crafts alkylation using a methyl halide, not acylation with acetyl chloride.
      • Option (b) \(ClCO^+\) does not match the reagent present here. The acyl group attached is \(CH_3CO-\), not \(ClCO-\).
      • Option (c) \(Cl^+\) is not the electrophile in this reaction.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • Whenever you see:
        • \(RCOCl / AlCl_3\) with benzene, think Friedel–Crafts acylation
        • electrophile = \(RCO^+\)
      • Whenever you see:
        • \(RCl / AlCl_3\) with benzene, think Friedel–Crafts alkylation
        • electrophile = \(R^+\)
      • This distinction alone solves many MCQs quickly.
    • Final result
      • A = benzene
      • B = acetophenone
      • The electrophile involved in the conversion of A to B is \(CH_3CO^+\).
      • Hence, the correct answer is (d).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
      • Topic: Aromatic hydrocarbons
      • Subtopic: Friedel–Crafts acylation and electrophile generation
    • Correct answer: (b) 2-methylpropene
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: Ozonolysis cuts the double bond and converts each alkene carbon into a carbonyl carbon. To obtain methanal \((HCHO)\), one end of the double bond must be \(CH_2\). To obtain propan-2-one \((CH_3COCH_3)\), the other double-bond carbon must be attached to two \(CH_3\) groups. The alkene that matches this is 2-methylpropene.
    • Ozonolysis pattern shown in the solution
    • Step-by-step explanation
      • In ozonolysis, the carbon-carbon double bond breaks completely.
      • Each carbon of the double bond becomes part of a carbonyl compound.
      • The products given are:
        • methanal = \(HCHO\)
        • propan-2-one = \(CH_3COCH_3\)
      • Now work backward.
      • If one product is methanal, then one alkene carbon must originally have been \(CH_2=\).
      • If the other product is propan-2-one, then the other alkene carbon must have been attached to two methyl groups.
      • So the alkene must be \(CH_2=C(CH_3)_2\).
      • This compound is 2-methylpropene.
    • Concept used / theory behind it
      • Ozonolysis is one of the most important methods for locating the position of a double bond.
      • Under reductive workup, the double bond is cleaved to give aldehydes and/or ketones.
      • Useful reverse rules:
        • \(HCHO\) in products means one side of the double bond was terminal \(CH_2\).
        • A ketone in products means that alkene carbon had no hydrogen attached to it.
        • \(CH_3COCH_3\) specifically means that carbon had two \(CH_3\) groups attached.
    • Why the correct option is right
      • Option (b) \(CH_2=C(CH_3)_2\) on ozonolysis gives:
        • \(HCHO\)
        • \(CH_3COCH_3\)
      • That matches the given products exactly.
    • Why other options are wrong
      • Option (a) propene gives methanal and ethanal, not propan-2-one.
      • Option (c) 2-methylbut-1-ene gives methanal and butan-2-one, not propan-2-one.
      • Option (d) 2-methylbut-2-ene gives acetone and ethanal, not methanal.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue
      • In reverse ozonolysis questions:
        • first identify whether a product is aldehyde or ketone,
        • then reconstruct each carbon of the original double bond.
      • If methanal appears, immediately look for a terminal alkene.
      • If acetone appears, think of a double-bond carbon carrying two methyl groups.
    • Final result
      • \(X = CH_2=C(CH_3)_2\)
      • Therefore, \(X\) is 2-methylpropene.
      • Hence, the correct answer is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
      • Topic: Alkenes
      • Subtopic: Ozonolysis and double-bond cleavage
    • Correct answer: (b) \(IV < I < III < II\)
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: The acidic strength here depends mainly on the hybridisation of the carbon attached to the acidic hydrogen. Greater the \(s\)-character, greater is the acidity. So \(sp\)-hybridised carbon compounds are more acidic than \(sp^2\), and \(sp^2\) are more acidic than \(sp^3\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Let us identify the hybridisation of the carbon bearing the acidic hydrogen in each case.
      • I. \(H_2C = CH_2\)
        • Hydrogens are attached to \(sp^2\)-hybridised carbon.
      • II. \(CH \equiv CH\)
        • Terminal hydrogen is attached to \(sp\)-hybridised carbon.
      • III. \(CH_3 - C \equiv CH\)
        • The terminal hydrogen is again attached to \(sp\)-hybridised carbon.
      • IV. \(CH_3 - CH_3\)
        • Hydrogens are attached to \(sp^3\)-hybridised carbon.
      • Now remember the rule:
        • \(sp\) carbon has \(50\%\ s\)-character
        • \(sp^2\) carbon has \(33\%\ s\)-character
        • \(sp^3\) carbon has \(25\%\ s\)-character
      • More \(s\)-character means the conjugate base holds negative charge closer to the nucleus, so it is more stable.
      • More stable conjugate base means stronger acid.
      • Therefore:
        • \(sp > sp^2 > sp^3\) in acidity
      • So the least acidic is IV \((CH_3-CH_3)\).
      • Next comes I \((H_2C=CH_2)\).
      • Then the terminal alkynes III and II are more acidic.
      • Among II and III, acetylene \((CH \equiv CH)\) is slightly more acidic than propyne \((CH_3-C \equiv CH)\) because the \(CH_3\) group in III has a \(+I\) effect, which slightly destabilizes the conjugate base.
      • Hence the correct order is:
        • \(IV < I < III < II\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Acidity of hydrocarbons is usually very low, but still there is a relative order.
      • The main idea is stability of the conjugate base formed after loss of \(H^+\).
      • When negative charge is on an \(sp\)-hybridised carbon, it is held more tightly because of high \(s\)-character.
      • So terminal alkynes are more acidic than alkenes, and alkenes are more acidic than alkanes.
      • Important general order:
        • alkane \(<\) alkene \(<\) alkyne
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) correctly places:
        • ethane as least acidic
        • ethene next
        • propyne next
        • acetylene as most acidic among the four
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • Any option placing alkane above alkene or alkyne is wrong.
      • Any option placing alkene above terminal alkyne is wrong because \(sp\)-hybridised carbon gives greater acidity than \(sp^2\).
      • Any option placing acetylene below propyne ignores the slight electron-releasing effect of the methyl group in propyne.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • First sort by hybridisation:
        • \(sp^3 < sp^2 < sp\)
      • Then among terminal alkynes, a simple unsubstituted one like acetylene is slightly more acidic than an alkyl-substituted one like propyne.
      • This gives the final order immediately.
    • Final result:
      • The correct order of acidic strength is \(IV < I < III < II\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Acidity of hydrocarbons
      • Subtopic: Effect of hybridisation on acidic strength
    • Correct answer: (c) unsaturated alcohol, ketone
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: \(C_3H_4\) here is propyne. On hydration in the presence of \(Hg^{2+}/H^+\), it first forms an enol, which is an unsaturated alcohol. That enol then undergoes tautomerisation to give a ketone, namely acetone.
    • Hydration and tautomerisation scheme:
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The formula \(C_3H_4\) can represent propyne in this reaction context.
      • Terminal alkynes on hydration in the presence of \(Hg^{2+}/H^+\) follow Markovnikov addition of water.
      • So propyne \((CH_3-C \equiv CH)\) first forms an enol.
      • The intermediate \(X\) is:
        • \(CH_3-C(OH)=CH_2\)
      • This compound is an unsaturated alcohol because:
        • it contains an \(OH\) group
        • it still contains a double bond
      • Such enols are usually unstable.
      • They undergo keto-enol tautomerisation.
      • So \(CH_3-C(OH)=CH_2\) rearranges to:
        • \(CH_3-CO-CH_3\)
      • This product \(Y\) is acetone, which is a ketone.
      • Therefore:
        • \(X =\) unsaturated alcohol
        • \(Y =\) ketone
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This is the hydration of alkynes followed by keto-enol tautomerism.
      • When water adds to an alkyne in acidic medium with \(Hg^{2+}\), the immediate product is not directly the ketone.
      • The first product is an enol.
      • An enol is a compound having:
        • a double bond
        • an \(OH\) group attached to a doubly bonded carbon
      • Enols generally convert to more stable carbonyl compounds.
      • For terminal alkynes except ethyne, Markovnikov hydration gives methyl ketones after tautomerisation.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) correctly states that the first product \(X\) is an unsaturated alcohol and the final product \(Y\) is a ketone.
      • This exactly matches hydration of propyne followed by tautomerisation to acetone.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • Option (a) is wrong because the final product is not an aldehyde here.
      • Option (b) is wrong because the first product is not a saturated alcohol; it is an enol, so it is unsaturated.
      • Option (d) is wrong for both reasons.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorize this high-frequency rule:
        • Terminal alkyne + \(H_2O\), \(Hg^{2+}/H^+\) \(\rightarrow\) enol \(\rightarrow\) ketone
      • The first species is often an unsaturated alcohol, and the final stable product is usually a carbonyl compound.
      • For propyne specifically, the final product is acetone.
    • Final result:
      • \(X\) is an unsaturated alcohol and \(Y\) is a ketone.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Reactions of alkynes
      • Subtopic: Hydration of terminal alkyne and keto-enol tautomerism
    • Correct answer: (b) free radical
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: In the presence of peroxide, addition of \(HBr\) to propene follows the anti-Markovnikov pathway, and this happens through a free radical mechanism, not the usual ionic mechanism.
    • Peroxide-effect addition to propene:
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Normally, when \(HBr\) adds to an alkene without peroxide, the reaction follows Markovnikov’s rule.
      • But in the presence of peroxide \((ROOR)\), the mechanism changes completely.
      • Peroxide undergoes homolytic cleavage and generates free radicals.
      • These radicals initiate a chain reaction.
      • In this radical pathway, bromine radical \((Br \cdot)\) adds first to the double bond of propene.
      • The addition occurs in such a way that the more stable carbon radical is formed.
      • So \(Br \cdot\) attaches to the terminal carbon, giving a secondary radical intermediate.
      • That radical then abstracts hydrogen from \(HBr\), producing \(1\)-bromopropane.
      • Thus the bromine finally appears on the less substituted carbon, which is opposite to Markovnikov addition.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This is called the peroxide effect or Kharasch effect.
      • It is seen prominently with \(HBr\), not with \(HCl\) or \(HI\) under normal exam conditions.
      • The key idea is:
        • without peroxide \(\rightarrow\) ionic addition
        • with peroxide \(\rightarrow\) free radical addition
      • Anti-Markovnikov addition occurs because the radical intermediate formed must be the more stable one.
      • Secondary radical is more stable than primary radical, so the reaction chooses that path.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) is correct because peroxide-based addition of \(HBr\) proceeds through radical chain steps.
      • The mechanism involves formation of radical intermediates, so free radical is the correct explanation.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • Electrophile is associated with the usual ionic addition mechanism, not the peroxide effect.
      • Nucleophile is not the key attacking species in this reaction.
      • Carbene is a completely different reactive intermediate and is not involved here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever you see:
        • \(HBr +\) alkene + peroxide
      • immediately think:
        • anti-Markovnikov addition
        • free radical mechanism
      • This is one of the most repeated objective chemistry patterns.
    • Final result:
      • Addition of \(HBr\) to propene in presence of peroxide is explained by a free radical mechanism.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Addition reactions of alkenes
      • Subtopic: Peroxide effect and anti-Markovnikov addition of \(HBr\)
    • Correct answer: (a) \(-NO_2\)
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: The rate of electrophilic attack on a benzene ring is least when the substituent is a strong electron-withdrawing group. Among the given groups, \(-NO_2\) is the strongest electron-withdrawing and strongly deactivates the ring toward electrophilic substitution.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Electrophiles attack regions of high electron density.
      • Benzene ring normally has \(\pi\)-electron density and therefore undergoes electrophilic substitution.
      • But substituents already attached to the ring can change its reactivity.
      • If a group donates electrons to the ring, the ring becomes more reactive toward electrophiles.
      • If a group withdraws electrons from the ring, the ring becomes less reactive toward electrophiles.
      • Now compare the groups:
        • \(-CH_3\) is electron-donating by \(+I\) effect and hyperconjugation.
        • \(-OH\) strongly donates by resonance.
        • \(-NH_2\) strongly donates by resonance even more effectively.
        • \(-NO_2\) strongly withdraws electrons by both \(-I\) and \(-R\) effects.
      • Therefore \(-NO_2\) makes the benzene ring least reactive toward electrophilic attack.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question is from electrophilic aromatic substitution.
      • Substituent effects decide whether the benzene ring is activated or deactivated.
      • Activating groups increase the rate of electrophilic substitution.
      • Deactivating groups decrease the rate.
      • \(-NO_2\) is one of the strongest deactivating groups because:
        • it pulls electron density through the sigma bond \(( -I )\)
        • it also withdraws electron density through resonance \(( -R )\)
      • So nitrobenzene is much less reactive toward electrophiles than benzene itself.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (a) is correct because \(-NO_2\) deactivates the ring most strongly and thus gives the least rate of electrophilic attack.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • \(-CH_3\) activates the ring slightly.
      • \(-OH\) activates the ring strongly.
      • \(-NH_2\) is also a strong activating group.
      • So none of these can give the least rate of electrophilic attack.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For electrophilic aromatic substitution:
        • \(-OH\), \(-NH_2\), \(-OR\), alkyl groups \(\rightarrow\) activating
        • \(-NO_2\), \(-CN\), \(-CHO\), \(-COOH\), \(-SO_3H\) \(\rightarrow\) deactivating
      • Among the options here, \(-NO_2\) stands out immediately as the strongest deactivator.
    • Final result:
      • The rate of attack of an electrophile is least when \(X = -NO_2\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Electrophilic aromatic substitution
      • Subtopic: Activating and deactivating substituent effects
    • Correct answer: (b) \(CH_3-CH_2-CH_2OH\)
      Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: 2-bromopropane first undergoes dehydrohalogenation with alcoholic KOH to form propene \((X)\). Then propene undergoes hydroboration-oxidation with \(B_2H_6\) followed by \(H_2O_2/OH^-\), giving propan-1-ol as the major product \(Y\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Dehydrohalogenation of 2-bromopropane
        • The starting compound is 2-bromopropane: \(CH_3-CHBr-CH_3\).
        • With alcoholic KOH and heat, haloalkanes usually undergo \(\beta\)-elimination.
        • That means one \(H\) and one \(Br\) are removed from adjacent carbon atoms.
        • So the product \(X\) becomes propene:
          • \(CH_3-CHBr-CH_3 \xrightarrow[\Delta]{alc.\ KOH} CH_3-CH=CH_2\)
      • Step 2: Hydroboration-oxidation of propene
        • Now propene reacts with \(B_2H_6\), followed by \(H_2O_2/OH^-\).
        • This reaction adds water across the double bond, but in an anti-Markovnikov manner.
        • That means the \(OH\) group goes to the less substituted carbon atom.
        • So for propene, the product is:
          • \(CH_3-CH_2-CH_2OH\)
        • This is propan-1-ol.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Alcoholic KOH generally promotes elimination, not substitution, especially on heating.
      • This converts haloalkanes into alkenes.
      • Hydroboration-oxidation is a very important alkene reaction.
      • It gives alcohols with anti-Markovnikov addition.
      • So:
        • Markovnikov hydration of propene would give 2-propanol,
        • but hydroboration-oxidation gives 1-propanol.
      • This difference is exactly what the examiner is testing here.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) is \(CH_3-CH_2-CH_2OH\), which is propan-1-ol.
      • This is the expected anti-Markovnikov alcohol formed from propene by hydroboration-oxidation.
      • Hence option (b) is correct.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) \(CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_3\) is 2-propanol. This would come from Markovnikov hydration, not hydroboration-oxidation.
      • (c) \(CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_2OH\) is a dihydroxy alcohol structure and is not formed in this sequence.
      • (d) \(HO-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-OH\) is a diol and again not formed here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever you see:
        • \(B_2H_6\)
        • then \(H_2O_2/OH^-\)
      • immediately think hydroboration-oxidation = anti-Markovnikov alcohol.
      • Also remember:
        • alc. KOH + heat \(\rightarrow\) elimination
        • aq. KOH \(\rightarrow\) substitution
      • This pair of ideas solves many EAPCET organic questions very fast.
    • Final result:
      • The major product \(Y\) is propan-1-ol, \(CH_3-CH_2-CH_2OH\).
      • Therefore, the correct answer is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes / Hydrocarbons / Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Topic: Dehydrohalogenation and hydroboration-oxidation
      • Subtopic: Conversion of haloalkane to alkene and anti-Markovnikov hydration
    • Correct answer: (b) Clemmensen reduction
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
    • Explanation: Benzene first undergoes Friedel-Crafts acylation with \(CH_3COCl\) and anhydrous \(AlCl_3\) to form acetophenone \((X)\). Then the carbonyl group of acetophenone is reduced to a methylene group in the presence of \(Zn-Hg\) and concentrated \(HCl\), giving ethyl benzene \((Y)\). This reduction is called Clemmensen reduction.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Formation of \(X\)
        • Benzene reacts with acetyl chloride \((CH_3COCl)\) in presence of anhydrous \(AlCl_3\).
        • This is a Friedel-Crafts acylation reaction.
        • The acyl group \((CH_3CO-)\) enters the benzene ring.
        • So \(X\) is acetophenone:
          • \(C_6H_6 \xrightarrow{CH_3COCl/Anhy.\ AlCl_3} C_6H_5COCH_3\)
      • Step 2: Conversion of \(X\) into \(Y\)
        • Now acetophenone is treated with \(Zn-Hg\) and concentrated \(HCl\).
        • These are the characteristic reagents of Clemmensen reduction.
        • In this reaction, the carbonyl group \((>C=O)\) of aldehydes or ketones is reduced to \(CH_2\).
        • So:
          • \(C_6H_5COCH_3 \xrightarrow{Zn-Hg/conc.\ HCl} C_6H_5CH_2CH_3\)
        • Thus \(Y\) is ethyl benzene.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Clemmensen reduction reduces aldehydes and ketones to hydrocarbons in strongly acidic medium using zinc amalgam and concentrated hydrochloric acid.
      • It is especially useful when the compound can tolerate acidic conditions.
      • The carbonyl oxygen is effectively removed, and the \(C=O\) becomes \(CH_2\).
      • In aromatic ketones like acetophenone, this converts the side-chain carbonyl compound into an alkylbenzene.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • The reagent set \(Zn-Hg/conc.\ HCl\) is the signature of Clemmensen reduction.
      • The reaction converts acetophenone \((X)\) into ethyl benzene \((Y)\) by reducing the ketone group to a methylene group.
      • Hence option (b) is correct.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Rosenmund reduction converts acid chlorides into aldehydes using poisoned catalyst, so it does not fit here.
      • Wolff-Kishner reduction also reduces carbonyl group to \(CH_2\), but it uses hydrazine and strong base, not \(Zn-Hg/conc.\ HCl\).
      • Stephen reaction converts nitriles into aldehydes, so it is unrelated here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorise the reagent-reaction pair:
        • \(Zn-Hg/conc.\ HCl \rightarrow\) Clemmensen reduction
        • \(NH_2NH_2/KOH,\ \Delta \rightarrow\) Wolff-Kishner reduction
      • If the medium is acidic, think Clemmensen.
      • If the medium is strongly basic with hydrazine, think Wolff-Kishner.
    • Final result:
      • The conversion of \(X\) into \(Y\) is Clemmensen reduction.
      • Therefore, the correct answer is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids
      • Topic: Aromatic substitution and reduction of carbonyl compounds
      • Subtopic: Friedel-Crafts acylation and Clemmensen reduction
    • Correct answer: (a) Toluene + \(CrO_2Cl_2\)
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
    • Explanation: Etard reaction is the oxidation of the side chain methyl group of an aromatic compound like toluene using chromyl chloride \((CrO_2Cl_2)\), giving an aldehyde. Therefore, the main reactants involved are toluene and chromyl chloride.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • In Etard reaction, an aromatic methyl compound such as toluene is treated with chromyl chloride.
      • The methyl side chain \((−CH_3)\) is oxidised, but not fully to the carboxylic acid.
      • Instead, it stops at the aldehyde stage.
      • So, toluene gives benzaldehyde through Etard oxidation.
      • The key reagent that identifies the reaction is \(CrO_2Cl_2\), called chromyl chloride.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Etard reaction is a named reaction from aldehydes and aromatic side-chain oxidation chemistry.
      • It is especially useful for converting:
        • \(Ar-CH_3 \rightarrow Ar-CHO\)
      • Normal strong oxidation can push the side chain all the way to \(−COOH\), but Etard reaction is remembered because it gives the aldehyde.
      • This is why it is a very favourite exam reaction.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (a) contains toluene and chromyl chloride \((CrO_2Cl_2)\).
      • That is the exact reagent-substrate combination associated with Etard reaction.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (b) Toluene + \(CrO_3\) + \((CH_3CO)_2O\) is not Etard reaction.
      • (c) Toluene + \(Cl_2/h\nu\) is free radical side-chain chlorination.
      • (d) Benzene + CO + HCl / anhydrous \(AlCl_3\) is Gattermann-Koch type formylation, not Etard reaction.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorise this direct pair:
        • Etard reaction \(\rightarrow\) toluene + chromyl chloride \((CrO_2Cl_2)\)
      • Whenever you see chromyl chloride in an aromatic side-chain oxidation question, think Etard reaction immediately.
    • Final result:
      • The main reactants involved in Etard reaction are toluene and \(CrO_2Cl_2\).
      • Hence, the correct answer is (a).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
      • Topic: Aromatic compounds and named reactions
      • Subtopic: Etard reaction
    • Correct answer: (b) m-chlorobenzoic acid
      Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles, Techniques and Hydrocarbons TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing C, H and O TTR
      Organic Compounds Containing Nitrogen TTR
      Haloalkanes and Haloarenes TTR
    • Explanation: Benzoic acid first undergoes nitration to give m-nitrobenzoic acid because the \(−CO_2H\) group is meta-directing. The nitro group is then reduced to amino group, forming m-aminobenzoic acid. Finally, diazotisation followed by Sandmeyer reaction replaces the diazonium group by chlorine, giving m-chlorobenzoic acid as the major product \(Z\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Nitration of benzoic acid
        • Benzoic acid is treated with concentrated \(HNO_3\) and concentrated \(H_2SO_4\).
        • This is nitration of an aromatic ring.
        • The \(−CO_2H\) group is strongly electron-withdrawing and is a meta-directing group.
        • So the incoming \(−NO_2\) group enters mainly at the meta position.
        • Thus, \(X\) is m-nitrobenzoic acid.
      • Step 2: Reduction of nitro group
        • \(Sn/HCl\) reduces aromatic nitro group \((−NO_2)\) to amino group \((−NH_2)\).
        • So \(X\) is converted into m-aminobenzoic acid.
        • Thus, \(Y\) is m-aminobenzoic acid.
      • Step 3: Diazotisation
        • \(NaNO_2/HCl\) at \(0^\circ C\) converts the aromatic amine into a diazonium salt.
        • So the \(−NH_2\) group is converted into \(−N_2^+Cl^-\).
      • Step 4: Sandmeyer reaction
        • Treatment with \(Cu_2Cl_2/HCl\) replaces the diazonium group by chlorine.
        • So the final product \(Z\) becomes m-chlorobenzoic acid.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines three very important aromatic chemistry ideas:
        • directive influence in electrophilic substitution,
        • reduction of nitro group to amino group,
        • diazotisation and Sandmeyer substitution.
      • The \(−COOH\) group is deactivating and meta-directing because it withdraws electron density from the ring.
      • That is why nitration does not mainly give ortho or para product here.
      • Then \(−NO_2\) can be reduced to \(−NH_2\), and aromatic amines can be converted to diazonium salts at low temperature.
      • Diazonium salts are highly useful intermediates because the diazonium group can be replaced by \(Cl\), \(Br\), \(CN\), \(OH\), etc.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • The carboxyl group fixes the incoming nitro group at meta position in the first step.
      • That meta position is retained throughout the sequence.
      • After reduction and diazotisation, chlorine finally occupies that same meta position.
      • So the final product is m-chlorobenzoic acid.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • (a) m-nitrobenzoic acid is only the first intermediate \(X\), not the final product \(Z\).
      • (c) m-aminobenzoic acid is the second intermediate \(Y\), not the final product.
      • (d) m-chlorobenzoyl chloride is wrong because the \(−COOH\) group is never converted into \(−COCl\) anywhere in the sequence.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Keep this reaction flow in memory:
        • \(Ar-NO_2 \xrightarrow{Sn/HCl} Ar-NH_2\)
        • \(Ar-NH_2 \xrightarrow{NaNO_2/HCl,\ 0^\circ C} Ar-N_2^+Cl^-\)
        • \(Ar-N_2^+Cl^- \xrightarrow{Cu_2Cl_2/HCl} Ar-Cl\)
      • Also remember that \(−COOH\) is a classic meta director.
      • Those two facts almost solve the whole question instantly.
    • Final result:
      • The major product \(Z\) is m-chlorobenzoic acid.
      • Therefore, the correct answer is (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Amines / Hydrocarbons / Haloarenes
      • Topic: Aromatic substitution, reduction, diazotisation and Sandmeyer reaction
      • Subtopic: Reaction sequence involving benzoic acid derivatives

    2022
    • Correct answer: (a) \(IV > III > II > I\)
    • Explanation: Stability based on hyperconjugation depends on how many \(\alpha\)-hydrogens or adjacent \(C-H\) bonds can interact with the \(\pi\)-bond. Greater the number of adjacent \(C-H\) bonds available for hyperconjugation, greater is the stability. Among the given compounds, this count is maximum in \(IV\) and minimum in \(I\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Hyperconjugation is the delocalisation of electrons from an adjacent \(\sigma\)-bond, usually a \(C-H\) bond, into a nearby \(\pi\)-system or vacant orbital.
      • So, for alkenes, we look at the number of \(C-H\) bonds on the carbon atom adjacent to the double bond.
      • The more such adjacent \(C-H\) bonds present, the greater the hyperconjugative stabilization.
      • In the given set, the structures are compared by counting the number of \(\alpha\)-hydrogens next to the double bond.
    • Step-by-step comparison of the four structures:
      • Compound \(I\):
        • The double bond is exocyclic, and the number of adjacent \(C-H\) bonds contributing to hyperconjugation is the least among the four.
        • So it gets the least stabilization.
      • Compound \(II\):
        • It has more adjacent \(C-H\) bonds than \(I\), so it is more stable than \(I\).
      • Compound \(III\):
        • Because of the methyl substituent near the double bond, the number of adjacent \(C-H\) bonds increases further.
        • So \(III\) is more stable than \(II\).
      • Compound \(IV\):
        • It has the maximum number of adjacent \(C-H\) bonds available for hyperconjugation.
        • Therefore, it is the most stable.
      • Hence, the order is:
        • \(IV > III > II > I\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Hyperconjugation is sometimes called “no-bond resonance.”
      • It stabilizes alkenes, carbocations, and radicals when a neighboring \(C-H\) bond can overlap with the adjacent unsaturated system or empty \(p\)-orbital.
      • For alkene stability:
        • more alkyl substitution \(\rightarrow\) more adjacent \(C-H\) bonds \(\rightarrow\) more hyperconjugation \(\rightarrow\) greater stability
      • This is why more substituted alkenes are generally more stable than less substituted ones.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (a) is correct because the adjacent \(C-H\) bond count increases in the order \(I < II < III < IV\), so the stability follows \(IV > III > II > I\).
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • Options (b), (c), and (d) place one or more less-hyperconjugated structures above a more-hyperconjugated one.
      • That contradicts the basic alkene stabilization rule through hyperconjugation.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Do not directly guess only from “number of alkyl groups.”
      • For strict hyperconjugation questions, count the adjacent \(C-H\) bonds next to the double bond.
      • More adjacent \(C-H\) bonds means more hyperconjugative structures and greater stability.
    • Final result:
      • The correct order of stability is \(IV > III > II > I\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: General Organic Chemistry
      • Subtopic: Hyperconjugation and alkene stability
    • Correct answer: (a) \([D] > [C] > [A] > [B]\)
    • Explanation: Addition of \(Br_2\)/water to an alkene proceeds through formation of a bromonium ion. The faster reaction occurs for the alkene that can better stabilize this positively polarized intermediate. Alkyl groups donate electron density and increase the rate, while the nitro group \((NO_2)\) strongly withdraws electron density and decreases the rate.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • When an alkene reacts with \(Br_2\) in water, the \(\pi\)-bond attacks bromine first.
      • This forms a cyclic bromonium ion intermediate.
      • The rate of reaction depends on how easily the alkene donates electron density to bromine and how stable the intermediate is.
      • Alkyl groups are electron-releasing by the \(+I\) effect and also help stabilize positive character in the intermediate.
      • So, more alkyl substitution on the double bond means faster addition.
      • Now compare the four alkenes:
        • \([D]\) \(CH_3 - CH(CH_3) = CH_2\): two alkyl groups effectively support the double bond environment the most, so it reacts fastest.
        • \([C]\) \(CH_3 - CH_2 - CH = CH_2\): one alkyl group is attached, so it reacts faster than ethene.
        • \([A]\) \(CH_2 = CH_2\): no alkyl substituent, so it is less reactive than \(C\) and \(D\).
        • \([B]\) \(CH_2 = CH - NO_2\): the \(NO_2\) group is strongly electron-withdrawing, making the double bond electron-poor and least reactive toward electrophilic bromination.
      • Therefore, the rate order is:
        • \([D] > [C] > [A] > [B]\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This is an electrophilic addition reaction of alkenes.
      • The alkene acts as a nucleophile and attacks bromine.
      • Anything that increases electron density on the double bond increases the rate.
      • Anything that pulls electron density away from the double bond decreases the rate.
      • So:
        • alkyl groups \(\rightarrow\) increase rate
        • electron-withdrawing groups like \(NO_2\) \(\rightarrow\) decrease rate
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (a) is correct because \(D\) has the strongest electron-donating alkyl effect, followed by \(C\), then \(A\), while \(B\) is deactivated by the strongly withdrawing \(NO_2\) group.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • Option (b) places \(B\) above \(A\), which is wrong because \(NO_2\) makes the alkene much less reactive than ethene.
      • Options (c) and (d) incorrectly place unsubstituted or nitro-substituted alkene above the more alkyl-substituted alkenes.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For electrophilic addition to alkenes, ask one quick question:
        • Which double bond is richest in electrons?
      • More alkyl groups usually mean faster reaction.
      • Strong electron-withdrawing groups like \(NO_2\) strongly slow it down.
    • Final result:
      • The correct order of rate is \([D] > [C] > [A] > [B]\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Hydrocarbons
      • Subtopic: Electrophilic addition reactions of alkenes
    • Correct answer: (a) \(P\): isopropyl benzene, \(Q\): benzyl alcohol
    • Explanation: In reaction \(A\), benzene reacts with \(n\)-propyl chloride in the presence of \(AlCl_3\), but the initially formed primary carbocation rearranges to the more stable secondary carbocation, giving isopropyl benzene as the major Friedel–Crafts alkylation product. In reaction \(B\), benzene first forms toluene, then side-chain bromination gives benzyl bromide, and finally aqueous \(KOH\) converts it into benzyl alcohol.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Reaction \(A\):
        • Benzene is treated with \(CH_3CH_2CH_2Cl\) in the presence of \(AlCl_3\).
        • This is a Friedel–Crafts alkylation reaction.
        • At first, one may think the product should be \(n\)-propyl benzene.
        • But the electrophile generated from \(n\)-propyl chloride is initially a primary carbocation-like species, which is unstable.
        • It undergoes a \(1,2\)-hydride shift to form the more stable secondary carbocation:
          • \(CH_3 - CH^+ - CH_3\)
        • Benzene then attacks this secondary carbocation.
        • So the major product \(P\) is isopropyl benzene, also called cumene.
      • Reaction \(B\):
        • Step (i): \(CH_3Cl/AlCl_3\) converts benzene into toluene by Friedel–Crafts methylation.
        • Step (ii): \(Br_2, h\nu\) causes free-radical side-chain bromination, not ring bromination.
        • So toluene gives benzyl bromide \((C_6H_5CH_2Br)\).
        • Step (iii): Aqueous \(KOH\) hydrolyses benzyl bromide to benzyl alcohol \((C_6H_5CH_2OH)\).
        • Thus, the major product \(Q\) is benzyl alcohol.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines three core ideas from aromatic chemistry:
        • Friedel–Crafts alkylation
        • carbocation rearrangement
        • benzylic free-radical bromination followed by nucleophilic substitution
      • In Friedel–Crafts alkylation, if a rearrangement can give a more stable carbocation, the rearranged product often becomes the major product.
      • Under \(Br_2/h\nu\), alkyl benzenes usually undergo substitution at the benzylic side chain because benzylic radicals are highly stabilized.
      • Benzyl halides then react readily with aqueous \(KOH\) to form benzyl alcohol.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (a) is correct because:
        • \(P\) is isopropyl benzene due to rearrangement in Friedel–Crafts alkylation.
        • \(Q\) is benzyl alcohol after methylation, benzylic bromination, and hydrolysis.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Options giving unrearranged propyl product for \(P\) are wrong because the secondary carbocation is more stable and dominates.
      • Options giving ketone, benzhydrol, or benzoic acid for \(Q\) are inconsistent with the reagent sequence shown.
      • \(Br_2/h\nu\) followed by aqueous \(KOH\) points toward benzylic bromination followed by substitution to alcohol, not oxidation or acylation.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For \(RCl/AlCl_3\) on benzene, always ask:
        • Can the carbocation rearrange to a more stable one?
      • For \(Br_2/h\nu\) on an alkyl benzene, think:
        • benzylic bromination
      • Then:
        • benzyl halide + aqueous \(KOH\) \(\rightarrow\) benzyl alcohol
    • Final result:
      • The major products are \(P =\) isopropyl benzene and \(Q =\) benzyl alcohol.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Hydrocarbons
      • Subtopic: Aromatic hydrocarbons, Friedel–Crafts alkylation, benzylic bromination and hydrolysis
    • Correct answer: (a) 3-hexanone
    • Explanation: Propene first gives \(n\)-propyl bromide by anti-Markovnikov addition of \(HBr\) in the presence of peroxide. This is then converted to propyl magnesium bromide, which reacts with propanal to give hexan-3-ol after hydrolysis. Oxidation of this secondary alcohol with \(CrO_3\) gives 3-hexanone.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step (i): Reaction of propene with \(HBr\) in the presence of peroxide
        • This is the peroxide effect or Kharasch effect.
        • \(HBr\) adds by anti-Markovnikov rule.
        • So propene gives \(n\)-propyl bromide:
          • \(CH_3CH = CH_2 \xrightarrow{HBr,\ peroxide} CH_3CH_2CH_2Br\)
      • Step (ii): Reaction with magnesium
        • Alkyl bromide reacts with \(Mg\) in dry ether to form Grignard reagent.
        • So:
          • \(CH_3CH_2CH_2Br \xrightarrow{Mg} CH_3CH_2CH_2MgBr\)
        • This is propyl magnesium bromide.
      • Step (iii): Reaction with propanal \((CH_3CH_2CHO)\)
        • Grignard reagent adds to the carbonyl carbon of aldehyde.
        • Propyl group \((C_3H_7)\) adds to propanal \((C_3)\).
        • After hydrolysis, the product is:
          • hexan-3-ol
        • Structure:
          • \(CH_3CH_2CH(OH)CH_2CH_2CH_3\)
      • Step (iv): Oxidation with \(CrO_3\)
        • Hexan-3-ol is a secondary alcohol.
        • Secondary alcohol on oxidation gives ketone.
        • So:
          • hexan-3-ol \(\rightarrow\) hexan-3-one
      • Hence, the final major product is 3-hexanone.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines four standard organic chemistry ideas:
        • peroxide effect in \(HBr\) addition
        • formation of Grignard reagent
        • addition of Grignard reagent to aldehyde
        • oxidation of secondary alcohol to ketone
      • Whenever \(HBr\) is used with peroxide, remember that only \(HBr\) shows the peroxide effect among hydrogen halides in this standard syllabus context.
      • Grignard reagent adds carbon chain to carbonyl compounds, increasing carbon count.
      • Aldehyde other than formaldehyde gives secondary alcohol on reaction with Grignard reagent.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (a) is correct because the sequence leads to hexan-3-ol first, and oxidation of that secondary alcohol gives 3-hexanone.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Option (b) 2-hexanone would require the \(OH\) group to be on carbon-2 before oxidation, which is not the case here.
      • Option (c) 2-methyl-3-pentanone would require a branched carbon skeleton, but the chain formed here is straight.
      • Option (d) hexanal would come from oxidation of a primary alcohol, not a secondary alcohol.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • In multistep organic questions, track the carbon skeleton carefully.
      • Here:
        • propene \(\rightarrow\) propyl bromide \(\rightarrow\) propyl Grignard
        • propyl Grignard + propanal \(\rightarrow\) \(3 + 3 = 6\) carbon alcohol
      • Then ask:
        • Is the alcohol primary, secondary, or tertiary?
      • That immediately tells the oxidation product.
    • Final result:
      • The major product formed is 3-hexanone.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Hydrocarbons / Haloalkanes and Haloarenes / Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers
      • Subtopic: Peroxide effect, Grignard reagent, aldehyde addition and oxidation of alcohols
    • Correct answer: (d) p-bromophenol
    • Explanation: Cumene on oxidation with oxygen followed by acidic hydrolysis gives phenol and acetone. Phenol is an ortho/para directing group. Bromination in non-polar solvent \((CHCl_3)\) at low temperature gives mainly mono-substitution, and para substitution dominates over ortho because the para product is less hindered. Hence the major aromatic product is p-bromophenol.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step (i) and (ii): Oxidation of cumene followed by acidic treatment
        • Cumene \((isopropylbenzene)\) reacts with oxygen to form cumene hydroperoxide.
        • On acidic hydrolysis, cumene hydroperoxide cleaves to give:
          • phenol
          • acetone
        • This is the well-known cumene process.
      • So after the first two steps, the aromatic compound of interest is phenol.
      • Step (iii): Bromination with \(Br_2\) in \(CHCl_3\) at \(273\ \text{K}\)
        • The \(-OH\) group in phenol is strongly activating and ortho/para directing.
        • However, the conditions matter a lot.
        • In a non-polar solvent like \(CHCl_3\) and at low temperature, bromination is controlled and mainly mono-bromination occurs.
        • Both ortho and para products are formed.
        • But para product is major because ortho substitution suffers more steric hindrance near the \(-OH\) group.
      • Therefore, the major aromatic product is p-bromophenol.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines:
        • cumene process for phenol manufacture
        • directive influence of phenol in electrophilic substitution
        • effect of solvent and conditions on bromination
      • Phenol is strongly activating due to resonance donation from oxygen.
      • So it directs incoming electrophiles to ortho and para positions.
      • Under aqueous bromine conditions, phenol can give 2,4,6-tribromophenol easily.
      • But under controlled non-polar solvent conditions, mono-bromination predominates, and para is major.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) is correct because phenol formed in the earlier step undergoes bromination in \(CHCl_3\) at low temperature to give mainly p-bromophenol.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Option (a) 2,4,6-tribromophenol is formed typically with bromine water, not under these controlled non-polar conditions.
      • Option (b) 2,4-dibromophenol is not the major product under the given conditions.
      • Option (c) o-bromophenol is formed only as minor product because of steric hindrance.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • If you see cumene + \(O_2\) + acid, immediately think:
        • phenol + acetone
      • Then for phenol bromination:
        • bromine water \(\rightarrow\) tribromophenol
        • \(Br_2\) in non-polar solvent at low temperature \(\rightarrow\) mono-bromination, para major
    • Final result:
      • The major aromatic product is p-bromophenol.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Phenols
      • Subtopic: Cumene process and bromination of phenol
    • Correct answer: (b) 2-pentanol
    • Explanation: A chiral molecule must have at least one carbon atom attached to four different groups and must not possess an internal plane of symmetry. In 2-pentanol, the carbon bearing the \(-OH\) group is attached to four different groups, so it is chiral.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Let us test each molecule for a chiral carbon.
      • (a) Isopropyl alcohol
        • Structure: \(CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_3\)
        • The carbon carrying \(-OH\) is attached to:
          • \(CH_3\)
          • \(CH_3\)
          • \(OH\)
          • \(H\)
        • Since two groups are identical \((CH_3, CH_3)\), it is not chiral.
      • (b) 2-pentanol
        • Structure: \(CH_3-CH(OH)-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3\)
        • The second carbon is attached to:
          • \(CH_3\)
          • \(CH_2CH_2CH_3\)
          • \(OH\)
          • \(H\)
        • All four groups are different.
        • Therefore this carbon is chiral, so the molecule is chiral.
      • (c) 1-bromo-3-butene
        • Structure: \(CH_2 = CH-CH_2-CH_2Br\)
        • No carbon is attached to four different groups.
        • So it is achiral.
      • (d) Isobutyl alcohol
        • Structure: \((CH_3)_2CH-CH_2OH\)
        • The possible carbon has two identical \(CH_3\) groups.
        • So it is not chiral.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Chirality usually arises when a tetrahedral carbon is bonded to four different atoms or groups.
      • Such a carbon is called an asymmetric or chiral carbon.
      • If two attached groups are identical, the carbon cannot be chiral.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) is correct because 2-pentanol has one carbon attached to four different groups.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Isopropyl alcohol has two identical methyl groups.
      • 1-bromo-3-butene has no chiral carbon.
      • Isobutyl alcohol also lacks a carbon with four different substituents.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For chirality questions, quickly inspect the carbon attached to the functional group.
      • If it has:
        • two identical groups \(\rightarrow\) achiral
        • four different groups \(\rightarrow\) chiral
    • Final result:
      • The chiral molecule is 2-pentanol.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Stereoisomerism
      • Subtopic: Chirality and asymmetric carbon atom
    • Correct answer: (d) dry ether
    • Explanation: Wurtz reaction is carried out with sodium metal and alkyl halides. The solvent must be aprotic, must not react with sodium, and should dissolve the alkyl halide well. Dry ether satisfies all these conditions, so it is the most suitable solvent.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Wurtz reaction is:
        • \(2R-X + 2Na \xrightarrow{dry\ ether} R-R + 2NaX\)
      • Since sodium metal is used, the solvent must be chosen carefully.
      • The solvent should:
        • not contain water
        • not react with sodium
        • be able to dissolve organic reactants
      • Dry ether is suitable because:
        • it is aprotic
        • it does not react with sodium under reaction conditions
        • it dissolves alkyl halides well
      • Dry ethanol is unsuitable because it is protic and reacts with sodium.
      • Other listed solvents are not the standard and suitable medium for Wurtz reaction in this syllabus context.
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Wurtz reaction involves sodium metal, so moisture must be completely absent.
      • That is why the solvent is specifically dry ether.
      • Dry ether is a classic reaction medium for organometallic-type sodium reactions and related coupling chemistry.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) is correct because dry ether is the accepted solvent for Wurtz reaction and fulfills the required conditions.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Dry ethanol is wrong because sodium reacts with alcohols.
      • Dry acetonitrile and dry dichloromethane are not the standard suitable solvents here.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • For Wurtz reaction, memorize the pair together:
        • sodium metal + dry ether
      • If ethanol appears among options, eliminate it immediately because it is protic.
    • Final result:
      • The most suitable solvent for Wurtz reaction is dry ether.
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
      • Topic: Alkanes preparation
      • Subtopic: Wurtz reaction and reaction conditions
    • Correct answer: (d) \(C_6H_{10}O\)
    • Explanation: Propyne on hydration with \(HgSO_4\) / dilute \(H_2SO_4\) gives acetone \((P)\). Acetone, when heated with \(Ba(OH)_2\), undergoes aldol condensation to form an \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated ketone. The final product \(Q\) has molecular formula \(C_6H_{10}O\).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Hydration of propyne
        • Propyne is:
          • \(CH_3-C \equiv CH\)
        • With \(HgSO_4\) / dilute \(H_2SO_4\), terminal alkynes undergo Markovnikov hydration.
        • The enol formed tautomerises to ketone.
        • So product \(P\) is:
          • acetone, \(CH_3COCH_3\)
      • Step 2: Reaction of acetone with \(Ba(OH)_2\)
        • \(Ba(OH)_2\) acts as a base and promotes aldol reaction.
        • Two molecules of acetone combine first to form diacetone alcohol.
        • On heating, dehydration occurs and an \(\alpha,\beta\)-unsaturated ketone is formed.
        • The product is mesityl oxide:
          • \(CH_3COCH=C(CH_3)_2\)
      • Now calculate molecular formula of this product:
        • Number of carbons \(= 6\)
        • Number of hydrogens \(= 10\)
        • Number of oxygens \(= 1\)
      • Therefore:
        • \(Q = C_6H_{10}O\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines:
        • hydration of terminal alkyne
        • keto-enol tautomerism
        • aldol condensation of ketone
      • Terminal alkyne with \(HgSO_4/H_2SO_4\) generally gives a ketone after tautomerism.
      • Acetone under basic conditions undergoes self-aldol condensation, and on heating gives an unsaturated ketone.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (d) is correct because acetone formed in the first step gives an aldol condensation product having formula \(C_6H_{10}O\).
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • \(C_3H_6O\) corresponds to acetone itself, which is only intermediate \(P\), not final \(Q\).
      • \(C_3H_8O\) is an alcohol formula, not correct here.
      • \(C_6H_{12}O_2\) would correspond to the addition product before dehydration, not the heated condensation product.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Remember:
        • propyne + \(HgSO_4/H_2SO_4\) \(\rightarrow\) acetone
      • Then:
        • acetone + base + heat \(\rightarrow\) aldol condensation product (unsaturated ketone)
      • If the question asks final product after heating, do not stop at the aldol addition stage.
    • Final result:
      • The molecular formula of product \(Q\) is \(C_6H_{10}O\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Aldehydes and Ketones
      • Topic: Hydration of alkynes and aldol condensation
      • Subtopic: Conversion of propyne to acetone and self-condensation of acetone
    • Correct answer: (c) \(P\): Alkene (cis), \(Q\): Alkene (trans)
    • Explanation: An alkyne on reduction with \(\mathrm{H_2}\) in the presence of Lindlar catalyst gives a cis-alkene because hydrogen adds by syn-addition. The same alkyne on treatment with \(\mathrm{Na/liquid\ NH_3}\) gives a trans-alkene because hydrogen addition occurs by anti-addition. Therefore, \(P\) is cis-pent-2-ene and \(Q\) is trans-pent-2-ene.
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting alkyne is pent-2-yne:
        • \(\mathrm{CH_3-C{\equiv}C-CH_2CH_3}\)
      • Reaction for \(P\): \(\mathrm{H_2}\), Lindlar catalyst
        • Lindlar catalyst is a poisoned catalyst.
        • It reduces alkynes only up to alkene stage.
        • The addition of hydrogen occurs from the same side.
        • This is called syn-addition.
        • So the product is cis-alkene.
        • Therefore, \(P =\) cis-pent-2-ene.
      • Reaction for \(Q\): \(\mathrm{Na/liquid\ NH_3}\)
        • This is dissolving metal reduction.
        • Hydrogen adds from opposite sides.
        • This is anti-addition.
        • So the product is trans-alkene.
        • Therefore, \(Q =\) trans-pent-2-ene.
      • Hence:
        • \(P\) is alkene (cis)
        • \(Q\) is alkene (trans)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Alkynes can be selectively reduced in two important ways:
        • Lindlar catalyst \(\rightarrow\) cis-alkene
        • \(\mathrm{Na/liquid\ NH_3}\) \(\rightarrow\) trans-alkene
      • This is one of the most important stereochemistry conversions in hydrocarbon chemistry.
      • The big exam idea is not just alkene formation, but whether the alkene is cis or trans.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) correctly matches:
        • Lindlar reduction \(\rightarrow\) cis-alkene
        • dissolving metal reduction \(\rightarrow\) trans-alkene
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • (a) is wrong because sodium in liquid ammonia does not give cis-alkene.
      • (b) is wrong because Lindlar catalyst does not usually reduce alkynes fully to alkane.
      • (d) is wrong because Lindlar reduction does not give trans-alkene.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Memorize this pair exactly:
        • Lindlar \(\rightarrow\) cis
        • \(\mathrm{Na/NH_3}\) \(\rightarrow\) trans
      • That single memory rule solves many alkyne reduction questions instantly.
    • Final result:
      • \(P\) is alkene (cis) and \(Q\) is alkene (trans).
      • Hence, the correct option is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons
      • Topic: Reactions of alkynes
      • Subtopic: Partial reduction of alkynes to cis and trans alkenes
    • Correct answer: (c)
    • Explanation: The given benzylic dibromide first undergoes dehydrohalogenation with alcoholic KOH to form a vinyl bromide. Then \(\mathrm{NaNH_2}\), being a stronger base, removes one more molecule of HBr to form phenylacetylene. On heating phenylacetylene in a red hot iron tube at \(873\ \mathrm{K}\), cyclotrimerisation occurs to give 1,3,5-triphenylbenzene. This matches option (c).
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • The starting compound is a dihalide attached to a phenyl ring:
        • \(\mathrm{Ph-CHBr-CH_2Br}\)
      • Step 1: alcoholic KOH
        • Alcoholic KOH causes elimination of HBr.
        • One molecule of HBr is removed.
        • This forms a vinyl bromide:
          • \(\mathrm{Ph-CH=CHBr}\)
      • Step 2: \(\mathrm{NaNH_2}\)
        • \(\mathrm{NaNH_2}\) is a very strong base.
        • It eliminates one more molecule of HBr from the vinyl bromide.
        • This gives an alkyne:
          • \(\mathrm{Ph-C{\equiv}CH}\)
        • This compound is phenylacetylene.
      • Step 3: red hot iron tube, \(873\ \mathrm{K}\)
        • Alkynes undergo polymerisation or cyclotrimerisation under such conditions.
        • Three molecules of phenylacetylene combine.
        • This forms a benzene ring carrying three phenyl groups at 1,3,5-positions.
        • The product is 1,3,5-triphenylbenzene.
      • This corresponds to option (c).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines three standard reactions:
        • dehydrohalogenation of vicinal or geminal dihalides to alkenes
        • further elimination from vinyl halides using strong base such as \(\mathrm{NaNH_2}\) to form alkynes
        • cyclotrimerisation of alkynes at high temperature
      • Phenylacetylene is a classic substrate for forming substituted benzenes by trimerisation.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) represents 1,3,5-triphenylbenzene, which is the correct trimerisation product of phenylacetylene.
    • Why other options are wrong:
      • (a), (b), and (d) show other phenyl-substituted benzene patterns, but the trimerisation of identical phenylacetylene molecules gives the symmetric 1,3,5-substitution pattern.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Whenever a dihalide is treated with:
        • alc. KOH then \(\mathrm{NaNH_2}\)
      • think:
        • dihalide \(\rightarrow\) alkene \(\rightarrow\) alkyne
      • And if that alkyne is then heated in red hot iron tube, think cyclotrimerisation to aromatic ring.
    • Final result:
      • The major product is 1,3,5-triphenylbenzene.
      • Hence, the correct option is (c).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Hydrocarbons / Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
      • Topic: Dehydrohalogenation and reactions of alkynes
      • Subtopic: Formation of alkynes from dihalides and cyclotrimerisation of alkynes
    • Correct answer: (b)
    • Explanation:
      • We must follow the reaction sequence step by step. Benzene first gets brominated, then methylation occurs para to bromine, then the aryl bromide forms a Grignard reagent, and finally that Grignard reagent adds to acetaldehyde. On hydrolysis, the product is \(p\)-methyl phenyl ethanol derivative, which matches option (b).
    • Reaction sequence in the question
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Bromination of benzene
        • \(Br_2/FeBr_3\) causes electrophilic aromatic substitution.
        • Benzene gives bromobenzene.
      • Step 2: Friedel-Crafts alkylation
        • Now bromobenzene reacts with \(CH_3Cl/AlCl_3\).
        • Bromine is deactivating but ortho-para directing.
        • So the incoming \(CH_3\) group goes mainly at the para position because para product is less sterically hindered than ortho.
        • This gives mainly para-bromotoluene.
      • Step 3: Formation of Grignard reagent
        • With \(Mg\) in dry ether, the \(C-Br\) bond converts into \(C-MgBr\).
        • So para-bromotoluene becomes \(p\)-tolyl magnesium bromide.
      • Step 4: Reaction with acetaldehyde
        • Grignard reagents attack the carbonyl carbon of aldehydes and ketones.
        • Here the reagent attacks \(CH_3CHO\).
        • This forms an alkoxide intermediate:
          • \(p-CH_3C_6H_4-CH(OMgBr)-CH_3\)
      • Step 5: Hydrolysis
        • On adding water, the alkoxide becomes alcohol.
        • So the final product is:
          • \(p-CH_3C_6H_4-CH(OH)-CH_3\)
      • This is a secondary alcohol attached to a para-methyl substituted benzene ring.
      • That matches option (b).
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question combines several standard organic reactions in one chain:
        • electrophilic aromatic substitution
        • directive influence of substituents
        • Grignard reagent formation
        • nucleophilic addition to carbonyl compounds
      • Important theory points:
        • halogens are deactivating but ortho-para directing
        • Grignard reagent behaves like a carbon nucleophile
        • reaction of \(RMgX\) with an aldehyde other than formaldehyde gives a secondary alcohol after hydrolysis
      • Since the carbonyl compound here is acetaldehyde, the product must be a secondary alcohol, not a tertiary alcohol.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (b) is correct because it shows the para-methyl substituted ring attached to \(CH(OH)CH_3\).
      • That is exactly what comes from the reaction of \(p\)-tolyl magnesium bromide with acetaldehyde followed by hydrolysis.
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Options (a) and (c) show a tertiary alcohol type carbon center, which would require reaction with a ketone, not acetaldehyde.
      • Option (c) also places methyl substitution ortho, whereas para substitution is the major product in the Friedel-Crafts step.
      • Option (d) has the ortho-methyl arrangement, which is not the major product.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • In long reaction chains, do not panic. Track one step at a time:
        • ring substitution position
        • what Grignard becomes
        • what type of alcohol aldehyde or ketone will give
      • Quick memory:
        • Grignard + aldehyde \(\rightarrow\) secondary alcohol
        • Grignard + ketone \(\rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol
      • That alone eliminates options (a) and (c).
    • Final result:
      • The major product is the para-substituted secondary alcohol shown in option (b).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Electrophilic substitution and Grignard reagents
      • Subtopic: Friedel-Crafts alkylation, aryl Grignard formation, and addition to aldehydes
    • Correct answer: (c) \(C_9H_{12}O\)
    • Explanation:
      • Benzene reacts with acetyl chloride in presence of anhydrous \(AlCl_3\) to form acetophenone by Friedel-Crafts acylation. Then methylmagnesium bromide adds a methyl group to the carbonyl carbon of acetophenone. After hydrolysis, a tertiary alcohol is formed whose molecular formula is \(C_9H_{12}O\).
    • Formation of acetophenone and its Grignard reaction
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • Step 1: Friedel-Crafts acylation
        • Benzene reacts with acetyl chloride \((CH_3COCl)\) in presence of anhydrous \(AlCl_3\).
        • This introduces the acetyl group \((COCH_3)\) onto benzene.
        • So product \(P\) is acetophenone:
          • \(C_6H_5COCH_3\)
      • Step 2: Reaction with methylmagnesium bromide
        • \(CH_3MgBr\) is a Grignard reagent.
        • It attacks the carbonyl carbon of acetophenone.
        • A methyl group gets added to the carbonyl carbon.
      • Step 3: Hydrolysis
        • On treatment with water, the alkoxide becomes alcohol.
        • The product is:
          • \(C_6H_5C(OH)(CH_3)_2\)
      • Now count atoms carefully:
        • Phenyl group = \(C_6H_5\)
        • Two methyl groups = \(C_2H_6\)
        • One \(OH\) group = \(OH\)
      • Total:
        • Carbon = \(6 + 2 + 1 = 9\)
        • Hydrogen = \(5 + 6 + 1 = 12\)
        • Oxygen = 1
      • So the molecular formula is:
        • \(C_9H_{12}O\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • Friedel-Crafts acylation converts benzene into an aryl ketone.
      • Grignard reagents add to carbonyl compounds:
        • with aldehydes they give primary or secondary alcohols depending on the aldehyde
        • with ketones they give tertiary alcohols
      • Since acetophenone is a ketone, adding \(CH_3MgBr\) must give a tertiary alcohol after hydrolysis.
    • Why the correct option is right:
      • Option (c) is correct because the final product \(Q\) is \(C_6H_5C(OH)(CH_3)_2\), whose formula is \(C_9H_{12}O\).
    • Why other options are wrong or less suitable:
      • Options (a) and (b) have only 8 carbons, but the final product clearly contains 9 carbons.
      • Option (d) has the wrong hydrogen count.
    • Exam tip / shortcut / elimination clue:
      • Quick route:
        • benzene + acetyl chloride \(\rightarrow\) acetophenone
        • ketone + Grignard reagent \(\rightarrow\) tertiary alcohol
      • Then just write the structure and count atoms. That avoids confusion from formula guessing.
    • Final result:
      • The molecular formula of \(Q\) is \(C_9H_{12}O\).
    • Chapter / topic / subtopic:
      • Chapter: Organic Chemistry
      • Topic: Friedel-Crafts acylation and Grignard reagents
      • Subtopic: Reaction of ketones with Grignard reagent and molecular formula determination
    • Correct answer: (d) A - IV, B - II, C - III, D - I
    • Explanation:
      • This is a direct named-reaction matching question. Each transformation is associated with one standard reagent set. Once those famous conversions are remembered, the answer becomes straightforward.
    • Standard conversions used in the match
    • Step-by-step explanation:
      • (A) Acid chloride to aldehyde
        • Acid chlorides are converted to aldehydes by Rosenmund reduction.
        • The reagent is:
          • \(H_2\), Pd - \(BaSO_4\)
        • So, \(A \rightarrow IV\).
      • (B) Benzene to benzaldehyde
        • Benzene is converted to benzaldehyde by Gattermann-Koch reaction.
        • The reagents are:
          • CO, HCl, anhyd. \(AlCl_3\)
        • So, \(B \rightarrow II\).
      • (C) Acetylene to aldehyde
        • Acetylene on hydration in presence of \(HgSO_4\) and \(H_2SO_4\) gives enol first, which tautomerizes to acetaldehyde.
        • So, \(C \rightarrow III\).
      • (D) Ester to aldehyde
        • Esters are selectively reduced to aldehydes by DIBAL-H under controlled conditions.
        • So, \(D \rightarrow I\).
      • Hence the correct full match is:
        • \(A - IV\)
        • \(B - II\)
        • \(C - III\)
        • \(D - I\)
    • Concept used / theory behind it:
      • This question is built on very standard organic name-reaction memory points.
